cases.prompt: In Iqbal's 'Subah ka Sitara' (Morning Star), what does the speaker say about Venus?
cases.rationale: Bang-e-Dara Part 1 chapter 42. Tests astronomical-figure retrieval.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**In Allama Iqbal's poem "Subah Ka Sitara" ("Morning Star" or "The Star of the Dawn") from *Bang-e-Dara Part 1*, the speaker is Venus personified—the herald of dawn, appearing before sunrise.**[^1]

Venus laments its exalted yet burdensome celestial fate: it would "forsake the joy of sun and moon" to escape its duty as the morning's herald, finding "this starry realm holds no delight" and preferring "earth's lowliness than this great height." It describes the sky as "the void's abyss," itself confined to a "dawn's rent shroud" where it must "daily die and live," drinking the "morning-draught that Death's hands give." This "service" and "honor" seem worthless compared to a "dark, silent fate."[^2]

**The speaker yearns for earthly incarnation over its starry existence**, wishing instead to be a pearl in ocean depths (leaving the sea to adorn a throat), a jewel on a queen's crown, a stone on Solomon's ring, dew on a flower, spangles on a forehead, or—most poignantly—a tear from the eye of a wife whose armored husband departs for battle in defense of the homeland. There, amid "hope and deep despair," it would find "eternal life" in the dust, igniting "the fire of love to all mankind."[^3]

**Iqbal uses Venus's voice to contrast hollow cosmic splendor with the profound, self-sacrificing vitality of human passion and earthly bonds.**

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, "Subah Ka Sitara" [paragraph](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/42-subah-ka-sitara/translation/#p-aab796).

[^2]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, "Subah Ka Sitara" [passage](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/42-subah-ka-sitara/translation/#p-aab796 "p-aab796 p-658448 p-ec866d p-f7ffd9 p-17be48").

[^3]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, "Subah Ka Sitara" [passage](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/42-subah-ka-sitara/translation/#p-69f75f "p-69f75f p-a38930 p-4accfe p-ce41ff p-3abca5 p-e76893 p-f560c9 p-ea5c34 p-4d2e45 p-8594f9 p-fed550 p-b43666 p-aa7dc1 p-5fd53a p-2aceb1").

cases.prompt: Find the passage in Iqbal's 'Parinde ki Faryad' where the caged bird laments.
cases.rationale: Bang-e-Dara Part 1 chapter 34 — 'The Bird's Lament'. Famous Iqbal piece on captivity.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**The poem "Parinde Ki Faryad" (A Bird's Lament) from Allama Iqbal's *Bang-E-Dara Part 1* (Chapter 34) is itself the lament of a caged bird.** It expresses the bird's sorrow over its lost freedom, memories of the garden, separation from its mate and friends, the arrival of spring in contrast to its imprisonment, and a plea for release.[^1]

> A Bird's Lament  
>   
> Those garden splendors, the chorus of all,  
> The freedom to come and to go at my call.  
>   
> My heart aches with pain when I chance to recall,  
> The smiling of buds on the dewdrops that fall.  
>   
> That beautiful image, that form full of grace,  
> Whose presence brought life to my own dwelling place.  
>   
> Her voice never reaches me, held in this plight,  
> Oh, if only release were within my own might!  
>   
> How wretched I am, for my home I now pine,  
> My friends in our homeland, this prison is mine.  
>   
> The spring has arrived, and the flowers all bloom,  
> While I weep for my fate in this house of dark gloom.  
>   
> O God! To whom can I tell my tale of despair?  
> I fear in this cage I will die from my care.  
>   
> My heart is consuming its grief, it is true,  
> And grief is consuming my heart through and through.  
>   
> O listener, don't think this a song and be glad,  
> It is the cry of all hearts that are wounded and sad.  
>   
> O Captor, I beg you, now make me unbound,  
> I'm voiceless; release me, a blessing you've found.[^1]

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, "Parinde Ki Faryad" (Ch. 34), [full passage](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/34-parinde-ki-faryad/translation/#p-f1e47c).

cases.prompt: Show me Iqbal's 'Sarguzasht-e-Adam' — the autobiography of Adam.
cases.rationale: Bang-e-Dara Part 1 chapter 37. Mythopoetic poem on the human-historical condition.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Allama Iqbal's "Sarguzasht-e-Adam"** (translated as "The Saga of Man"), chapter 37 of *Bang-E-Dara Part 1* (1924), is a poetic monologue in which Adam recounts his spiritual journey and exile from Paradise, framed as his autobiography. This Urdu poem explores themes of human restlessness, quest for truth, historical and prophetic roles, and ultimate self-realization.[^1]

Here is the full English translation:

> The Saga of Man  
> 
> Come, hear from me the story of my exile’s way;  
> The tale of the First Covenant I let fall away.  
> 
> My spirit found no rest in Paradise’s domain,  
> Once the fiery cup of consciousness I chose to drain.  
> 
> For cosmic truth I kept a burning, endless quest;  
> The highest reach of soaring thought I then expressed.  
> 
> With such a restless nature, loving change, was I endowed,  
> Beneath the sky, no lasting peace have I allowed.  
> 
> At times, from the sanctum, stone idols I would expel,  
> At times, new gods within its holy walls I made to dwell.  
> 
> At times, to Sinai, in my desire to speak, I flew,  
> The Light Eternal in my sleeve I hid from view.  
> 
> At times, upon the Cross, by my own kin I was spurned,  
> Then leaving earth, to heaven’s heights my soul returned.  
> 
> At times, in Hira’s Cave for many years I stayed,  
> The final cup of truth to all the world I freely gave.  
> 
> In India, I arrived and sang the hymns divine,  
> At times, the ancient land of Greece I chose as mine.  
> 
> And when the land of India would not heed my sound,  
> In China and Japan, new homelands I then found.  
> 
> At times, from particles, a universe I wrought,  
> Against the meaning that the men of faith had taught.  
> 
> I stained a hundred different lands with blood-red dye,  
> The war of faith and reason I let fly.  
> 
> When I could not yet grasp the truth the stars arrayed,  
> In that same thought, through endless nights I watched and stayed.  
> 
> The drawn swords of the Church could not inspire my fear,  
> The truth of Earth’s own turning I made clear.  
> 
> The secret law of gravity I laid quite bare,  
> The lens of my far-seeing mind I focused there.  
> 
> The restless lightning and the sun’s own rays I caught,  
> This earth into a rival of Paradise I wrought.  
> 
> But ah! The secret of existence was not revealed,  
> Though with my mind, the world itself I forced to yield.  
> 
> But when my eye that worships form at last was opened wide,  
> I found that in my own heart’s house He did reside.[^1]

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, [Sarguzasht-e-Adam](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/37-sarguzasht-e-adam/translation/).

cases.prompt: In Yajnavalkya Smriti chapter 1, locate the verses on the four ashramas (life-stages) of a Brahmin.
cases.rationale: Tests passage retrieval inside an 11,500-word chapter. The ashrama-vyavastha is a core dharma topic.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Chapter 1 of the *Yājñavalkya Smṛti* opens with the sages requesting instruction on the *dharma* of the *varṇa*s (social classes) and *āśrama*s (life-stages):**[^1]

> Declare to us in their entirety the *dharma* of the *varṇa*s, the life-stages, and the others. // Yj_1.1 //

However, no verses in this chapter explicitly outline or list **all four *āśrama*s** (brahmacarya/student celibate, gṛhastha/householder, vānaprastha/hermit, and sannyāsa/sannyāsin/ascetic) together, particularly for a Brāhmaṇa. Instead:

- **Brahmacarya (*āśrama*)** is detailed extensively in the subsection "2. The Student Celibate (*brahmacāriprakaraṇam*)", from verse 1.10 (defining the *varṇa*s and initiation) through 1.50 (perpetual student attaining liberation).[^2][^3]
- **Gṛhastha (*āśrama*)** follows indirectly through marriage rules (verses 1.51–96, "3. Marriage") and explicitly in "5. The Duties of a Householder (*gṛhasthadharmaprakaraṇam*)", verses 1.97–128.[^4]
- **Vānaprastha and sannyāsa** are **not covered in Chapter 1**. These appear later in **Chapter 3** ("On Expiation"), under "3. The Duties of a Hermit (*vānaprasthadharmaprakaraṇam*)", Yj. 3.45–55, and "4. The Duties of the Ascetic (*yatidharmaprakaraṇam*)", Yj. 3.56+.[^5]

The text thus addresses the first two *āśrama*s for twice-born men (including Brāhmaṇas) in Chapter 1 as part of general conduct (*ācāra*), but the full set spans chapters. If every classical topic were consolidated, the structure would differ; here, it aligns with the *smṛti*'s topical organization (Chapter 1: conduct; Chapter 3: later stages amid expiation/distress rules).

[^1]: *Yājñavalkya Smṛti*, Chapter 1, [paragraph](/works/unknown-yajnavalkya-smrti-cb88d6/01/translation/#p-97f0a4).
[^2]: *Yājñavalkya Smṛti*, Chapter 1, verses 1.10–14 (varṇas, upanayana/initiation), [paragraphs](/works/unknown-yajnavalkya-smrti-cb88d6/01/translation/#p-546db5).
[^3]: *Yājñavalkya Smṛti*, Chapter 1, verse 1.50 (end of brahmacārin duties), [paragraph](/works/unknown-yajnavalkya-smrti-cb88d6/01/translation/#p-36dbed).
[^4]: *Yājñavalkya Smṛti*, Chapter 1, verse 1.97 (householder's daily rites), [paragraph](/works/unknown-yajnavalkya-smrti-cb88d6/01/translation/#p-6c5d0a).
[^5]: *Yājñavalkya Smṛti*, Chapter 3, [Chapter overview](/works/unknown-yajnavalkya-smrti-cb88d6/03/translation/).

cases.prompt: Quote Iqbal's lines in 'Bilad-e-Islamiya' on the cities of Islam.
cases.rationale: Bang-e-Dara Part 3 chapter 2 — geographic-political meditation. Tests retrieval of an explicitly named topic.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Allama Iqbal's poem "Bilad-e-Islamiya" (The Abodes of Islam) from *Bang-e-Dara Part 3* eulogizes key cities and lands of Islamic history and civilization: Delhi, Baghdad, Cordoba, Constantinople (Istanbul), and Yathrib (Medina).**[^1]

Here is the English translation:

> The Abodes of Islam  
> 
> The soil of Delhi is a shrine for the grief-stricken heart;  
> In every particle, the blood of our forebears lies asleep.  
> 
> How could the earth of this ruined garden not be sacred ground?  
> This land is the sanctum of the majesty of Islam.  
> 
> In this dust sleep the sovereigns of the Best of Nations,  
> On whose governance the world's own order turned.  
> 
> The heart still yearns for the warmth of that vibrant gathering's memory;  
> The harvest is burnt, but the memory of the harvest is preserved.  
> 
> Though Delhi is a shrine for every Muslim soul,  
> Baghdad, too, is worthy of this sacred honor.  
> 
> This is the garden that became the pride and glory  
> Of that desert tulip known as the culture of Hijaz.  
> 
> How could the dust of this city not rival Iram's own paradise,  
> This ground that witnessed the steps of the Prophet's successors?  
> 
> This is that garden whose every bud was a garden in itself;  
> This is the final tomb of those before whom Rome would tremble.  
> 
> The land of Cordoba, too, is the light of a Muslim's eye,  
> A Lamp of Sinai that shone in the darkness of the West.  
> 
> Its flame went out, and scattered the Shining Nation's assembly,  
> Yet in its dying breath, it lit the lamp of the civilization of today.  
> 
> This sacred land is the tomb of that civilization,  
> From which the vine of Europe's garden drew its very lifeblood.  
> 
> The realm of Constantinople, once the abode of Caesar,  
> Now an eternal symbol of the Ummah's power and grandeur.  
> 
> Like the dust of the Holy Sanctuary, this land is also pure;  
> It is the threshold of a throne that serves the Lord of Creation's Purpose.  
> 
> Its very air is as pure as the fragrance of a rose;  
> From the tomb of Ayyub Ansari, a voice arises.  
> 
> "O Muslim! This city is the very heart of the Islamic nation;  
> It is the harvest of centuries of struggle and sacrifice."  
> 
> But you are that sacred land, O resting place of the Chosen One,  
> For the Kaaba itself, a glimpse of you is more than the Great Pilgrimage could ever be.  
> 
> In the ring of all existence, you shine bright like a gem;  
> Your very soil was the birthplace of our own grandeur.  
> 
> In you, that most exalted Sovereign at last found his peace;  
> In the shelter of his mantle, the nations of the world found refuge.  
> 
> Those who merely invoked his name became sovereigns of the world,  
> Becoming successors to Caesar, heirs to the throne of Jamshid.  
> 
> If the nationhood of Islam were bound to any single place,  
> Then its foundation would be India—not Persia, not Syria.  
> 
> Ah, Yathrib! You are the Muslim's true homeland, you are his sanctuary;  
> You are the focal point that gathers all the rays of our soul's devotion.  
> 
> As long as you endure on this earth, we too shall endure;  
> If you are the morning, we are the pearls of dew within this garden.[^1]

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-e-Dara Part 3*, "Bilad-e-Islamiya" (translation), [full passage](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/02-bilad-e-islamiya/translation/).

cases.prompt: What does Ghalib's 'Ghum khaane mein boodaa hai dil' open with about the heart's dwelling in sorrow?
cases.rationale: Tests retrieval of a poignant matla on the heart inhabiting grief.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Ghalib's ghazal "Gham khaane mein boda dil-e-nakaam bahut hai" (chapter 50 of *Diwan-E-Ghalib*) opens with a couplet about the heart's immersion in sorrow.**[^1]

In translation:  
"This weak, failed heart's capacity for pain is more than enough;  
The grief that rose-red wine is on the wane is more than enough."[^2]

The opening evokes the heart (*dil*) as weak and unsuccessful (*boda dil-e-nakaam*), dwelling in the "house of grief" (*gham khaane mein*), with an overflowing capacity for sorrow (*bahut hai*), even as rose-red wine (*mai-e-gulfaam*)—symbolizing fleeting joys or intoxications—dwindles.[^3]

In transliteration:  
"Gham khaane mein boda dil-e-nakaam bahut hai  
Yeh ranj ke kam hai mai-e-gulfaam bahut hai"[^4]

In the original Urdu:  
"غم کھانے میں بودا دل_ناکام بہت ہے  
یہ رنج کہ کم ہے مئے_گلفام بہت ہے"[^5]

[^1]: Mirza Ghalib, *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, [chapter](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/50-gham-khaane-mein-boda-dil-e-nakaam-bahut-hai/translation/).
[^2]: Mirza Ghalib, *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, [paragraph](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/50-gham-khaane-mein-boda-dil-e-nakaam-bahut-hai/translation/#p-b39b6a).
[^3]: Ibid.
[^4]: Mirza Ghalib, *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, [paragraph](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/50-gham-khaane-mein-boda-dil-e-nakaam-bahut-hai/transliteration/#p-d3a1ee).
[^5]: Mirza Ghalib, *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, [paragraph](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/50-gham-khaane-mein-boda-dil-e-nakaam-bahut-hai/original/#p-a0be2a).

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**I could not find any passage in the Falsafa corpus's *Diwan-e-Ghalib* where Mirza Ghalib describes the unfaithful (or faithless/infidel) beloved as having no equal in the world.** 

The corpus includes the complete *Diwan-e-Ghalib* ([Mirza Ghalib's collection of 239 ghazals](https://falsafa.com/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/)), with English translations available. Extensive searches across English translations for phrases and concepts like "unfaithful beloved," "faithless beloved," "no equal," "no rival," "peerless/matchless beloved," "infidel beloved," "beloved equal," and combinations with "Ghalib" or "in the world" returned zero relevant results from this work.[^1]

Ghalib frequently writes of the beloved's cruelty (*jafa* or *bewafai*), beauty, and indifference (e.g., in ghazals lamenting unrequited love), but no verse matches this specific description. If it's from another collection, a different translation, or Persian works by Ghalib (not in this Urdu *Diwan*), it's not covered here. The corpus focuses on translated philosophical/classical texts and has only this Ghalib entry.

[^1]: Searches conducted: `"unfaithful beloved"`, `faithless beloved`, `"no equal" beloved`, `"no rival" beloved`, `"matchless" beloved`, `"peerless" beloved`, `beloved equal`, `"beloved" rival`, `"beauty" rival`, `"beauty" "no equal"`, `infidel beloved`, `"kafir" beloved`, `"ghalib" beloved`, `ghalib "no equal"`, `ghalib rival`, `ghalib "in the world"`, `beloved` (top 10, none from Ghalib). All on English translation bodies.

cases.prompt: In Iqbal's 'Tujh ko duzdida nigahi yeh sikha di kis ne', who taught the beloved the art of stolen glances?
cases.rationale: Bang-e-Dara Part 2 chapter 26. Classical ghazal trope; tests Iqbal's rare ghazal-form output.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**In Allama Iqbal's poem "Tujh ko duzdida nigahi yeh sikha di kis ne" from *Bang-E-Dara Part 2* (Chapter 26), the speaker rhetorically asks a kitten (the "beloved," addressed as "you" with "eyes of azure blue," batting paws, and lap-sitting habits) who taught it "this art of the stolen glance."**[^1]

**The poem does not name a specific teacher. Instead, it concludes that no instruction was needed: the sense of Beauty (and Love) is innate, not exclusive to humans but present "deep in all things," throbbing in every atom as a universal force.**[^2][^3]

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 2*, "Tujh ko duzdida nigahi yeh sikha di kis ne" [paragraph](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/26-tujh-ko-duzdida-nigahi-yeh-sikha-di-kis-ne/translation/#p-4ab922).
[^2]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 2*, "Tujh ko duzdida nigahi yeh sikha di kis ne" [paragraph](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/26-tujh-ko-duzdida-nigahi-yeh-sikha-di-kis-ne/translation/#p-69ebd5).
[^3]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 2*, "Tujh ko duzdida nigahi yeh sikha di kis ne" [paragraph](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/26-tujh-ko-duzdida-nigahi-yeh-sikha-di-kis-ne/translation/#p-a7275e).

cases.prompt: In Iqbal's 'Dard-e-Ishq' (Bang-e-Dara Part 1 chapter 17), how is the pain of love characterised?
cases.rationale: Tests retrieval of a foundational early Iqbal piece on the metaphysics of love-suffering.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
In Allama Iqbal's poem *Dard-e-Ishq* ("The Anguish of Love"), the pain of love is characterized as a precious, pure entity akin to "a pearl of purest water," sacred and profound, yet unsuited to the superficial modern world.[^1] It is depicted as something to be concealed from unworthy eyes and shallow gatherings that pursue only "surface light," as this age—marked by critics, science, and reason—ignores or dismisses it.[^2] Rather than being expressed through poetry, the nightingale's song, or visible tears, it belongs hidden in the "soul's most secret receptacle," evoking no joy in public display amid hearts "drunk on wines of [their] own mind."[^3]

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 17 "Dard-e-Ishq" (translation), [p-b75085](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/17-dard-e-ishq/translation/#p-b75085), [p-6d19d8](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/17-dard-e-ishq/translation/#p-6d19d8).

[^2]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 17 "Dard-e-Ishq" (translation), [p-3d4e57](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/17-dard-e-ishq/translation/#p-3d4e57), [p-d85ab0](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/17-dard-e-ishq/translation/#p-d85ab0), [p-71e687](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/17-dard-e-ishq/translation/#p-71e687), [p-31e0b4](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/17-dard-e-ishq/translation/#p-31e0b4).

[^3]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 17 "Dard-e-Ishq" (translation), [p-01e01a](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/17-dard-e-ishq/translation/#p-01e01a), [p-08811a](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/17-dard-e-ishq/translation/#p-08811a), [p-ec2aa2](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/17-dard-e-ishq/translation/#p-ec2aa2), [p-463ab2](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/17-dard-e-ishq/translation/#p-463ab2), [p-6ebcbf](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/17-dard-e-ishq/translation/#p-6ebcbf).

cases.prompt: Show me the verse in Iqbal's 'Ek Makra aur Makhi' where the spider lures the fly.
cases.rationale: Bang-e-Dara Part 1 chapter 20. Iqbal's adaptation of Mary Howitt — tests fable retrieval.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
In Allama Iqbal's poem *Ek Makra aur Makhi* ("A Spider and a Fly") from *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, the spider begins luring the fly with a polite invitation to enter his web, disguised as a humble hut.[^1]

> A spider to a fly one day did say,  
> “You travel daily down this very way,  
>   
> But my poor hut has never been so blessed,  
> That you might stop and be my honored guest.  
>   
> To shun a stranger is no great disgrace,  
> But from your own, don't keep such distant space.  
>   
> If you would come, my honor would increase,  
> The winding stair is there, if you should please.”[^1]

After the fly's initial refusal, the spider reflects that **flattery** is the key to success ("But flattery can make a hundred schemes succeed, / The world is slave to flattery's own creed") and then lavishes praise on her beauty to draw her in.[^2][^3]

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 20 "Ek Makra aur Makhi" (translation), [paragraphs](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/20-ek-makra-aur-makhi/translation/#p-02078b).
[^2]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 20 "Ek Makra aur Makhi" (translation), [paragraph](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/20-ek-makra-aur-makhi/translation/#p-1409dc).
[^3]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 20 "Ek Makra aur Makhi" (translation), [paragraphs](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/20-ek-makra-aur-makhi/translation/#p-d0b552).

cases.prompt: What does Iqbal's 'Ek Pahar aur Gilehri' (mountain and squirrel) teach?
cases.rationale: Bang-e-Dara Part 1 chapter 21. Famous didactic short poem; tests fable-with-moral retrieval.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**"Ek Pahar aur Gilehri" (A Mountain and a Squirrel)** is a fable-like poem from Allama Iqbal's *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*.[^1]

In it, a proud mountain mocks the tiny squirrel for its small size, boasting of its own towering height and majesty while belittling the squirrel's intellect and existence: "A tiny thing, with such conceit, what can one say? Such intellect and sense you put on full display!"[^2] The squirrel responds with wisdom and humility, arguing that physical size is irrelevant to true value. It points out God's purposeful design in creation—making the mountain mighty but immobile, while granting the squirrel agility to climb trees and crack nuts: "That I'm not large like you is no disgrace to me; For you are not as small as I am, you can see. In everything, the might of God is manifest; His wisdom makes some large, and small He makes the rest."[^3]

**The poem teaches that greatness is not measured by physical scale or apparent power, but by one's unique abilities, purpose, and role in the divine order of nature.** It critiques arrogance based on superficial superiority, celebrates diversity in creation, and affirms that "No single thing in this world is in vain; In Nature's great workshop, no creature is plain."[^4] This reflects Iqbal's broader themes of self-realization (*khudi*), humility before God, and the equality of all beings in fulfilling their destined functions.

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 21 ("Ek Pahar aur Gilehri"), [chapter](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/21-ek-pahar-aur-gilehri/translation/).

[^2]: Ibid., [paragraphs](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/21-ek-pahar-aur-gilehri/translation/#p-d43159).

[^3]: Ibid., [paragraphs](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/21-ek-pahar-aur-gilehri/translation/#p-4fdf79#p-fa7390).

[^4]: Ibid., [paragraph](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/21-ek-pahar-aur-gilehri/translation/#p-4ddc6a).

cases.prompt: Locate Iqbal's 'Kinaar-e-Ravi' — the poem on the bank of the Ravi river.
cases.rationale: Bang-e-Dara Part 1 chapter 28. River-meditation poem.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Iqbal's poem "Kinaar-e-Ravi" (On the Banks of the Ravi), which evokes a meditative scene by the Ravi River, is Chapter 28 in *Bang-E-Dara Part 1* (1924), the first collection of his Urdu poetry.**[^1]

Here is the full English translation:

> [p-70a0dc] The Ravi, lost in song, in evening's silence deep,  
> Ask not the state of heart that I within me keep.  
> 
> [p-e68501] Its cadence, rising, falling, bids me kneel and pray,  
> The world became a sacred precinct on this day.  
> 
> [p-7ee613] I stand beside the flowing river's shore,  
> But where I am, I know not anymore.  
> 
> [p-04d031] The evening's hem is stained with wine across the land,  
> The aged sky holds out a cup in his trembling hand.  
> 
> [p-6109d1] To nothingness, the day's swift caravan has flown,  
> This is no twilight glow, but flowers of the sun are sown.  
> 
> [p-29acb7] Afar they stand, in lonely majesty so deep,  
> The minarets of the Chughtai king's last sleep.  
> 
> [p-6b8789] This palace is a tale of time's tyrannical decree,  
> This palace is a book from a lost history.  
> 
> [p-ef39bd] What is this place? It seems a music without sound,  
> The trees, a silent gathering on holy ground.  
> 
> [p-8c38f2] Upon the river's breast, a swift boat seems to fly,  
> Its boatman locked in strife with waves that crest so high.  
> 
> [p-ad52bd] As swift as is a glance, that boat now speeds from me,  
> Beyond the ring of sight, for eyes no more to see.  
> 
> [p-098157] The vessel of a human life thus sails the sea,  
> Appearing and then lost in all eternity.  
> 
> [p-1c94bb] It never knows defeat, nor does its journey finish,  
> It vanishes from sight, but it does not perish.[^2]

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 28 ("Kinaar-e-Ravi").  
[^2]: *Ibid.*, [translation](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/28-kinaar-e-ravi/translation/).

cases.prompt: What does Zauq's 'Layi hayaat aaye, qaza le chali chale' say about life and death?
cases.rationale: One of Zauq's most-quoted couplets. Tests whether Falsafa can locate a partial first-line query.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
Sheikh Ibrahim Zauq's ghazal "Laayi hayaat aaye qaza le chali chale" (Diwan-E-Zauq, chapter 32) meditates on the transience of life and the inevitability of death, portraying human existence as fleeting, uncontrolled by personal will, and governed by divine fate (*qaza*). Life arrives unbidden ("Life brought us here") and departs similarly ("then in decay, we pass on by"), with no agency over coming or going.[^1] Even a timeless existence like that of Khizr would face an end, underscoring that all lives are temporary visits ("We are but guests of yesterday who quickly pass on by").[^2]

The poem likens life to a poorly played chess game on fate's board, where every choice leads to loss ("Each move I chose was played astray").[^3] It counsels detachment from worldly attachments and pleasures, though passions thwart this ("It’s best to turn from worldly sway... But nothing works along the way, if passions pass on by"),[^4] and warns that reason and clever plans are futile against destiny ("Your clever plans won't win the day, they'll simply pass on by").[^5] The world offers no companionship on the path to annihilation (*fana*; "The world helps no one on the way to where we all must die").[^6] Ultimately, Zauq urges resigned passage through life's garden, indifferent to its charms ("It's time to pass on by").[^7]

[^1]: Sheikh Ibrahim Zauq, *Diwan-E-Zauq*, [p-c15427](/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/32-laayi-hayaat-aaye-qaza-le-chali-chale/translation/#p-c15427).

[^2]: Sheikh Ibrahim Zauq, *Diwan-E-Zauq*, [p-cd7b9a](/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/32-laayi-hayaat-aaye-qaza-le-chali-chale/translation/#p-cd7b9a).

[^3]: Sheikh Ibrahim Zauq, *Diwan-E-Zauq*, [p-5ae612](/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/32-laayi-hayaat-aaye-qaza-le-chali-chale/translation/#p-5ae612).

[^4]: Sheikh Ibrahim Zauq, *Diwan-E-Zauq*, [p-dec346](/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/32-laayi-hayaat-aaye-qaza-le-chali-chale/translation/#p-dec346).

cases.prompt: What does Ghalib's 'Ghum-e-hasti ka asad kis se ho juz marg ilaaj' say is the only cure for the sorrow of existence?
cases.rationale: Famous closing couplet of ghazal 3 about the candle burning till morning. Tests citation under partial first-line.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Death (marg).**

This famous couplet from Mirza Ghalib's *Diwan-e-Ghalib* (ghazal opening "Aah ko chahiye ik umr asar hote tak") declares that the sorrow of existence (*gham-e-hasti*, or "ghum-e-hasti") has no remedy except death.[^1][^2]

The original Urdu reads:  
غَمْ ہَسْتِیْ کَا اسَدْ کِسْ سֵ ہُوْ جُزْ مَرْگْ عِلَاجْ  
شَمْعْ ہَرْ رَنگْ مَیْںْ جَلْتِیْ ہَیْْ سَحَرْ ہَوْتَےْ تَکْ[^3]

A common English rendering is:  
"What cure for life's great sorrow, Asad, say?  
A candle burns in every way till morning's light."[^1]

Ghalib (pen name Asad) compares human life to a candle that flickers desperately in every color until dawn (death) arrives, underscoring life's transient pain with no other relief.

[^1]: Mirza Ghalib, *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, [paragraph](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/03-aah-ko-chahiye-ik-umr-asar-hote-tak/translation/#p-82d15b).

[^2]: Mirza Ghalib, *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, [paragraph](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/03-aah-ko-chahiye-ik-umr-asar-hote-tak/transliteration/#p-ab88c8). (Transliteration: "Gham-e-hasti ka Asad kis se ho juz marg ilaaj / Shama har rang mein jalti hai seher hote tak.")

[^3]: Mirza Ghalib, *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, [paragraph](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/03-aah-ko-chahiye-ik-umr-asar-hote-tak/original/#p-ab88c8).

cases.prompt: Show me Iqbal's 'Wataniyat' — the poem where he criticizes territorial nationalism.
cases.rationale: Bang-e-Dara Part 3 chapter 25. Critique of nationalism is a major Iqbal theme — tests title-to-thesis retrieval.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, "Wataniyat" (Nationalism)**[^1]

> In this new age, the wine, the cup, the Jam are new,[^2]  
> The Saqi’s ways of grace and wrath are fashioned new.  
> The Muslim, too, has built his sanctum all anew,  
> While Civilization’s idol-smith carves idols new.  
> Of all these newer gods, the greatest is the Nation;  
> The robe it wears is Faith’s own final desecration.  

> This idol, forged by this new age’s philosophy,[^3]  
> Lays waste the Prophet’s house of faith and piety.  
> Your arm is mighty with the strength of Oneness still,  
> Your homeland is Islam, you serve the Prophet’s will.  
> O Mustafavi, show the world that ancient sight!  
> And grind this hollow idol into dust and night!  

> To be confined to one place is calamity,[^4]  
> Live free within the ocean, like a fish at sea.  
> To leave one’s home is the Beloved’s sacred way,  
> So bear you witness to the Prophet’s truth today.  
> In the discourse of the state, the Nation is but land;  
> In the Prophet’s teaching, Homeland is by God’s command.  

> Among the world’s great peoples, rivalry it sows,[^5]  
> The aim of trade becomes conquest where’er it goes.  
> From it, all politics from truth and candor flees,  
> And by its hand the weak man’s home is brought to its knees.  
> By it, God’s own creation is in pieces torn,  
> By it, the roots of Islam’s brotherhood are shorn.  

This poem from Iqbal's *Bang-e-Dara* (1924) sharply critiques modern territorial nationalism ("Wataniyat") as a false idol that desecrates Islamic faith, divides humanity, and contrasts it with the universal brotherhood of Islam, where true "homeland" is defined by divine command rather than geography.[^1]

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, [Wataniyat](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/25-wataniyat/translation/).

[^2]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, [Wataniyat](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/25-wataniyat/translation/#p-79b1e9).

[^3]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, [Wataniyat](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/25-wataniyat/translation/#p-2737d5).

[^4]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, [Wataniyat](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/25-wataniyat/translation/#p-a723b2).

[^5]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, [Wataniyat](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/25-wataniyat/translation/#p-97937b).

cases.prompt: In 'Larkiyan parh rahi hain Angrezi' (Bang-e-Dara Part 3 chapter 27), what does Iqbal observe about girls learning English?
cases.rationale: Tests retrieval of a satirical Iqbal piece on colonial education.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
In the poem "Larkiyan parh rahi hain angrezi," Allama Iqbal observes—satirically—that girls learning English marks the moment when "the nation has found the path to salvation," as Western style becomes the ideal while Eastern customs are condemned as sinful. He questions what dramatic outcome this cultural shift will produce.[^1]

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, "Larkiyan parh rahi hain angrezi" [paragraph](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/27-larkiyan-parh-rahi-hain-angrezi/translation/#p-289582).

cases.prompt: Find Widsith's catalog — what tribes does the speaker claim to have visited?
cases.rationale: Old English Elegies chapter 14. Widsith is a famous catalog poem.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Widsith's catalog of tribes visited appears in chapter 14 ("Widsith matholade, wordhord onleac") of *Old English Elegies*, an anonymous collection of Old English poems.[^1]**

The speaker, Widsith ("far-traveler"), boasts of his wide journeys, stating at the outset that he "had journeyed through most tribes and peoples over the earth."[^2] His explicit catalog of visits comes in a long passage where he repeatedly claims, "I was with..." (Old English: *wæs* 'I was'), listing over 50 tribes or peoples across Europe, the Near East, and beyond:[^3]

- Huns
- Hreth-Goths (a Gothic group)
- Swedes
- Geats
- South-Danes
- Wenlas
- Wærns
- vikings
- Gefthas
- Winedas (likely Wends/Slavs)
- Gefflegas
- Engles (Angles)
- Swæfs (Swabians)
- Ænenas
- Saxons
- Sycgs
- Sword-Wielders (*sweordwearum*, possibly a kenning or specific group)
- Hronas
- Deans
- Heatho-Reams
- Thuringians
- Throwends
- Burgundians
- Franks
- Frisians
- Frumtings
- Rugas
- Glomms
- Rumwalas
- Serkings (Saracens?)
- Serings (Sirians?)
- Greeks
- Finns
- Scots
- Picts
- Skrithifins (a Finnic people)
- Lidwicings
- Leons
- Longbeards (Lombards)
- Hundings
- Israelites
- Assyrians
- Hebrews
- Indians
- Egyptians
- Medes
- Persians
- Myrgings (his own people, mentioned also as his lineage origin)
- Mofdings
- Amothings
- East-Thuringians
- Eols
- Ists
- Idumeans

He frames this geographically and thematically (e.g., noting gifts from Burgundian king Guthere, time in Italy with Ælfwine, association with Gothic king Eormanric), blending historical, legendary, and biblical groups.[^3][^4] An earlier section catalogs rulers and their tribes (e.g., Attila over Huns, Eormanric over Goths), but these are reports heard (*gehyrde*) rather than personal visits.[^5]

[^1]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 14, [translation](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/14-widsith-matholade-wordhord-onleac/translation/).

[^2]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 14, [p-2f6bd3](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/14-widsith-matholade-wordhord-onleac/translation/#p-2f6bd3).

[^3]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 14, [p-8276c0](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/14-widsith-matholade-wordhord-onleac/translation/#p-8276c0).

[^4]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 14, [p-f8162a](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/14-widsith-matholade-wordhord-onleac/translation/#p-f8162a).

[^5]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 14, [p-545201](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/14-widsith-matholade-wordhord-onleac/translation/#p-545201).

cases.prompt: In the Old English Elegies, locate the riddling poem 'Wraetlic is thes wealstan' on the ruined city.
cases.rationale: Old English Elegies chapter 16 — The Ruin. Tests incipit-based retrieval.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
The riddling poem known as *The Ruin* (often identified by its opening line "Wrætlic is þes wealstan"), which meditates on the remnants of a once-great city destroyed by time and fate, is found as **Chapter 16** in the *Old English Elegies* collection.[^1]

This elegy describes the shattered walls, fallen towers, and decayed bathhouses of a Roman-era city (likely inspired by Bath), evoking its former splendor—crowded mead-halls, gleaming treasures, and steaming baths—now overtaken by "Wyrd" (fate).[^2] The poem's fragmentary and enigmatic style, with vivid ubi sunt ("where are they?") reflections on transience, gives it a riddling quality, as the subject (the ruined city) emerges gradually from the imagery.[^3]

Here is the opening of the modern English translation:

> Wondrous is this wall-stone, by Wyrd broken;  
> Strongholds shattered, the work of giants crumbles.  
> Roofs are toppled, towers in ruin...[^4]

The full original Old English text begins:

> Wrætlic is þes wealstan, wyrde gebræcon...[^5]

[^1]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 16 ("Wraetlic is thes wealstan..."), [chapter](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/16-wraetlic-is-thes-wealstan-wyrde-yebraecon-burgsted/translation/).

[^2]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 16, [paragraphs](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/16-wraetlic-is-thes-wealstan-wyrde-yebraecon-burgsted/translation/#p-ba51da).

[^3]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 16, [paragraphs](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/16-wraetlic-is-thes-wealstan-wyrde-yebraecon-burgsted/translation/#p-dfdbda).

[^4]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 16, [paragraph](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/16-wraetlic-is-thes-wealstan-wyrde-yebraecon-burgsted/translation/#p-d0d2bd).

[^5]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 16, [paragraph](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/16-wraetlic-is-thes-wealstan-wyrde-yebraecon-burgsted/original/#p-ab68b4).

cases.prompt: What does Iqbal's 'Mauj-e-Darya' (river-wave) say about the wave's restlessness?
cases.rationale: Bang-e-Dara Part 1 chapter 31. Metaphysical reading of motion.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
In Allama Iqbal's poem *Mauj_e_Darya* ("The Wave") from *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, the wave personifies its own restlessness as an essential, sacred torment—an "unquiet heart" that keeps it in "constant torment" and a "sacred agony, like quicksilver," which is its very element.[^1] This inner agitation drives dynamic actions: leaping toward the moon's pull, writhing and dashing its head against the shore, fleeing the river's "narrow straits," and yearning in anguish for reunion with the ocean's "vast expanse," as a traveler in love with its ultimate goal.[^2]

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, [p-475086](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/31-mauj-e-darya/translation/#p-475086).

[^2]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, [chapter](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/31-mauj-e-darya/translation/).

cases.prompt: Show me Iqbal's 'Shama o Parwana' — the candle and the moth.
cases.rationale: Bang-e-Dara Part 1 chapter 41. Persian-Urdu archetype of the moth and the flame.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Allama Iqbal's "Shama-o-Parwana" ("The Candle and the Moth")** is a short poem from *Bang-E-Dara Part 1* (1924), his seminal Urdu poetry collection. It explores the moth's passionate, self-sacrificial love for the candle as a metaphor for divine longing and spiritual ecstasy.[^1]

Here is the English translation:

> The Candle and the Moth[^2]  
>   
> O Candle, why for you does it hold love so vast?[^3]  
> Why is its restless soul upon your altar cast?[^4]  
>   
> Your grace makes it mercurial, ever in sway;[^5]  
> Have you then taught it passion's sacred rite and way?[^6]  
>   
> It circumambulates your stage of splendor bright;[^7]  
> Is it then singed by your gaze's electric light?[^8]  
>   
> In the anguish of death, does it find its soul's own ease?[^9]  
> In your devouring flame, is life that cannot cease?[^10]  
>   
> In this world's house of grief, if your radiance were not seen,[^11]  
> This burning heart's own tree of hope would not be green.[^12]  
>   
> To fall before you is its sacred prayer and rite;[^13]  
> Its tiny heart finds bliss in burning with delight.[^14]  
>   
> It holds a passion for the Eternal Form Divine;[^15]  
> A little Moses, on a miniature Sinai.[^16]  
>   
> A simple moth, and such a thirst for light's display![^17]  
> A tiny insect, and for light to yearn this way![^18]

The original Urdu and Roman transliteration are also available in the corpus.[^19]

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 41, translation (/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/41-shama-o-parwana/translation/).
[^2]: Ibid., [p-0d1630](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/41-shama-o-parwana/translation/#p-0d1630).

cases.prompt: In Comte's Traité de Législation Vol I chapter 5, what laws does Comte say men are subject to by their physical organisation?
cases.rationale: Tests French-language retrieval of a specific chapter argument about biological constraints on legislation.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
In *Traité de Législation*, Volume I, Chapter 5, Charles Comte explains that men, by virtue of their shared physical organization (organs, needs, and habits), are subject to invariable *natural laws*—defined as necessary connections between causes and their effects.[^1] He illustrates these with the following physiological examples:

- Abstaining from food for too long causes pain or death.[^2]
- Exposing one's organs to fire warms or burns them, depending on proximity.[^2]
- Deprivation of sufficient breathable air causes suffering or death, depending on duration and extent.[^2]
- Union of the sexes causes multiplication of the species.[^2]
- Pleasures too often repeated or prolonged weaken the organs.[^2]
- Moderate exercise strengthens the organs.[^2]

Comte frames these as inescapable consequences of human nature, akin to physical laws, alongside broader needs like hunger, thirst, cold, heat, fear, and security, which produce uniform effects across individuals.[^3]

[^1]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL I*, [Chapter 5](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/05-des-lois-auxquelles-les-hommes-sont-assujettis-par/translation/).

[^2]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL I*, [Chapter 5](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/05-des-lois-auxquelles-les-hommes-sont-assujettis-par/translation/#p-2c4dfb).

[^3]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL I*, [Chapter 5](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/05-des-lois-auxquelles-les-hommes-sont-assujettis-par/translation/#p-f10f47).

cases.prompt: What does Comte argue in chapter 8 of Traité de Législation Vol I about positive religion as the foundation of legislation?
cases.rationale: Specific chapter retrieval inside Comte's critique of theocratic foundations.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
In Chapter 8 of *Traité de Législation Vol. I*, Charles Comte critiques the system—attributed in part to Rousseau's admiration for Mohammed's institutions—that treats a "positive religion" (i.e., a revealed, dogmatic faith with specific precepts) as the **exclusive foundation** of both morality and legislation.[^1] He argues that this approach fundamentally undermines human progress and civilization by prohibiting empirical observation of causes and effects in this world, instead subordinating all duties, laws, and habits solely to the unverifiable will of a supernatural being, as interpreted through sacred texts and their clerical ministers.[^2]

Comte contends that the system arises not from genuine religious dogma but from the strategic interests of rulers and priests who seek to halt inquiry into the real-world consequences of institutions and habits. Aware of humanity's innate tendency toward prosperity through liberty and enlightenment, they suppress reason to preserve power: facts must not reveal that certain religious precepts foster error or stagnation, nor that beneficial practices can exist independently of dogma.[^3] By deeming religious precepts unchangeable—good merely because divine—the system freezes societies in place: truths excluded by the religion remain unrecognized, errors consecrated endure, and comprehensive codes (e.g., regulating all social relations) render nations slaves to priests, stifling reform via reason or experience.[^4]

This yields immense clerical power, as convincing people that a specific faith underpins virtues like probity, chastity, and property respect elevates priests near divinity, while fostering systemic hypocrisy: even skeptics feign belief "for the public good," authorizing vice among unbelievers or dissenters.[^5] Comte notes its global stagnationary effects—holding Asia, Africa, and parts of Europe in barbarism—and deems it less harmful if limited to private morality (leaving legislation to reason), but still quarrelsome and obstructive.[^6] He explicitly distinguishes it from Christianity, which imposes no legislative code, excludes neither reason nor facts, and aligns with analytical study.[^7] No historical evidence supports it as necessary for good morals or laws; Comte advocates basing morality and legislation on observed utility instead.[^8]

[^1]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL I*, [paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/08-du-systeme-qui-fait-d-une-religion-positive-le-fon/translation/#p-89a0f7).

[^2]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL I*, [paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/08-du-systeme-qui-fait-d-une-religion-positive-le-fon/translation/#p-a28373).

[^3]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL I*, [paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/08-du-systeme-qui-fait-d-une-religion-positive-le-fon/translation/#p-ae5e9a).

cases.prompt: Quote Comte in Traité de la propriété Vol I chapter 2 on liberty as a condition of property.
cases.rationale: Comte's central thesis: property follows from liberty. Tests retrieval of foundational chapter.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
In *Traité de la propriété* (Vol. I, Chapter 2), Charles Comte argues that liberty is foundational to the exercise of all rights (including property rights) and duties, as it is contrary to nature to treat persons as properties.[^1]

He writes:  
> Civil liberty and political liberty are therefore essential conditions of the exercise of all duties, and consequently of all rights; domestic servitude and political servitude are, on the contrary, their negation and their ruin.[^2]

Comte emphasizes:  
> From these facts and from the tendency of the human race toward its development and its well-being, I have drawn the consequence that servitude is a state contrary to nature; that it is in direct opposition to the laws that carry nations toward their development and their prosperity, and that a man, and with all the more reason a people, can never be legitimately placed in the rank of properties.[^3]

> We cannot, therefore, admit that, according to the laws of his nature, a man has duties to fulfill toward himself, toward his parents, toward his wife, toward his children, finally toward humanity, without admitting at the same time that the same laws call him to be free; that, in no case, can he legitimately be reduced to slavery, that is to say, be treated as a property, and that his liberty can be restricted only insofar as is indispensable to ensure the liberty of others.[^4]

[^1]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété*, [/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/02-de-la-liberte-consideree-comme-une-condition-de-le/translation/#p-8ec508](https://falsafa.corpus/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/02-de-la-liberte-consideree-comme-une-condition-de-le/translation/#p-8ec508).

[^2]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété*, [/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/02-de-la-liberte-consideree-comme-une-condition-de-le/translation/#p-2efb96](https://falsafa.corpus/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/02-de-la-liberte-consideree-comme-une-condition-de-le/translation/#p-2efb96).

[^3]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété*, [/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/02-de-la-liberte-consideree-comme-une-condition-de-le/translation/#p-2b4fad](https://falsafa.corpus/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/02-de-la-liberte-consideree-comme-une-condition-de-le/translation/#p-2b4fad).

[^4]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété*, [/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/02-de-la-liberte-consideree-comme-une-condition-de-le/translation/#p-2cd7a3](https://falsafa.corpus/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/02-de-la-liberte-consideree-comme-une-condition-de-le/translation/#p-2cd7a3).

cases.prompt: In Comte's chapter on the deforestation of mountains (Traité de la propriété Vol I chapter 13), what political-economic argument does he make?
cases.rationale: Tests retrieval of a niche but specific 19th-century environmental-economic argument.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
In *Traité de la propriété* Vol. I, Chapter 13, Charles Comte argues that rivers constitute common (undivided) property shared by all populations within their basin: downstream landowners cannot impede upstream drainage or navigation, but—crucially—upstream mountain populations hold their lands subject to a reciprocal duty not to impair the river's utility.[^1] Downstream basin lands, being the first privatized, most cultivated, populous, and economically advanced (with agriculture, industry, and commerce dependent on reliable river flow), confer co-ownership rights in the river on those populations.[^2] Deforestation of mountain plateaus and slopes by upstream proprietors violates this by accelerating rainwater evaporation or converting rivers into seasonal torrents (rather than steady flows sustained by soil infiltration), thereby inflicting severe economic damage on downstream properties and prosperity.[^3][^4] Comte substantiates this with historical examples from the Americas, Europe, and elsewhere, framing unrestricted private land use upstream as a political-economic externality that undermines the property rights and development of downstream society.[^5]

[^1]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété*, [/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/13-influence-du-deboisement-des-montagnes-sur-les-fle/translation/#p-143669](p-143669).
[^2]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété*, [/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/13-influence-du-deboisement-des-montagnes-sur-les-fle/translation/#p-0f23c7](p-0f23c7).
[^3]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété*, [/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/13-influence-du-deboisement-des-montagnes-sur-les-fle/translation/#p-a50c69](p-a50c69).
[^4]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété*, [/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/13-influence-du-deboisement-des-montagnes-sur-les-fle/translation/#p-79de67](p-79de67).
[^5]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété*, [/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/13-influence-du-deboisement-des-montagnes-sur-les-fle/translation/#p-cd0e3e](p-cd0e3e).

cases.prompt: Show me Iqbal's 'Hindostan Hamara' — the song about India being our homeland.
cases.rationale: Tests title-to-poem retrieval for one of Iqbal's most-anthologised pieces (also a national anthem candidate).

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Allama Iqbal's "Hindustani Bachon Ka Qaumi Geet" (National Song of Indian Children)** from *Bang-E-Dara Part 1* (1924) is a patriotic poem celebrating Hindustan (India) as the homeland.[^1] It is structured as four stanzas, each ending with the refrain "This is my homeland, this land of mine," evoking deep love for the land's spiritual, cultural, and natural heritage.

> Where Chishti first Truth's holy call conveyed,[^2]  
> And Nanak's song of Oneness was displayed,  
> The land the Tatars for their homeland made,  
> Which drew Hijaz's sons from desert shade,  
> This is my homeland, this land of mine.[^3]  
> 
> The land that left the wisest Greeks spellbound,[^4]  
> Whose arts and knowledge spread the world around,  
> Whose very dust by Truth with gold was crowned,  
> Where for the Turks great diamond wealth was found,  
> This is my homeland, this land of mine.[^5]  
> 
> The stars that fell from Persia's shattered space,[^6]  
> Were given light and a new shining place,  
> The world first heard of Oneness from this place,  
> A cooling breeze reached Arab's Chief with grace,  
> This is my homeland, this land of mine.[^7]  
> 
> Its people Moses, and its peaks are Sinai's height,[^8]  
> Where Prophet Noah's ark first saw the light,  
> Whose very ground is a stair to heaven's light,  
> To live here is a paradise of pure delight,  
> This is my homeland, this land of mine.[^9]

(Note: Iqbal's more famous *Tarana-e-Hindi* ("Sare Jahan Se Achha Hindustan Hamara") does not appear to be in the Falsafa corpus under that title or with matching English translation phrasing, based on searches across his works. This children's qaumi geet directly matches the "Hindostan Hamara" theme of India as "our homeland.")

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 26 "Hindustani Bachon Ka Qaumi Geet" (/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/26-hindustani-bachon-ka-qaumi-geet/translation/).

[^2]: /works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/26-hindustani-bachon-ka-qaumi-geet/translation/#p-da6786

cases.prompt: Find Iqbal's poem 'Ram' in Bang-e-Dara Part 3 — what does he say about the figure of Ram?
cases.rationale: Iqbal's Hindu-Muslim syncretist piece on Lord Ram. Politically interesting; tests cross-religious-figure retrieval.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
In Allama Iqbal's poem "Ram" from *Bang-E-Dara Part 3* (Chapter 17), the figure of Ram (the Hindu deity and epic hero from the *Ramayana*) is portrayed with reverence as a symbol of spiritual and cultural pride for Hindustan (India). Iqbal presents Ram as a "Spiritual Guide" for India, a peerless embodiment of valor, purity, and inner fire, whose legacy elevates Indian thought to heavenly heights—brighter even in cultural "dusk" than in its dawn.[^1]

The poem opens by exalting Hindu philosophy as a profound wisdom that captivates Western thinkers, before focusing on Ram specifically:

> On Ram's existence, Hindustan can gaze with pride,  
> The wise of vision see him as Hind's Spiritual Guide.[^2]

Iqbal concludes by praising Ram's unmatched courage in battle ("valor with the sword") and spiritual qualities ("purity and loving fire").[^3]

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, Ch. 17 ("Ram"), [translation](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/17-ram/translation/).
[^2]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, Ch. 17 ("Ram"), [paragraph](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/17-ram/translation/#p-f8aace).
[^3]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, Ch. 17 ("Ram"), [paragraph](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/17-ram/translation/#p-567e82).

cases.prompt: In Iqbal's 'Swami Ram Tirath' (Bang-e-Dara Part 2 chapter 23), what tribute does he pay to the Hindu sage?
cases.rationale: Tests another Iqbal cross-religious tribute. Famous for its ecumenicism.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**In "Swami Ram Tirath" (Bang-e-Dara Part 2, chapter 23), Allama Iqbal pays a profound Sufi-inspired tribute to the Hindu sage Swami Ram Tirath (1873–1906), portraying him as a soul who achieved spiritual annihilation (*fana*) and divine union.**[^1]

Iqbal addresses him as a "restless drop" that, embraced by the river (symbolizing the divine ocean), transforms from a mere pearl into one "beyond price"—a metaphor for transcending worldly limits through mystical merger.[^2] He praises the sage's grace in unveiling "the secret of color and scent" (the essence beyond sensory distinctions), contrasting it with his own lingering attachment to forms.[^3]

The poem elevates Swami Ram Tirath's death or ecstasy: his annihilation turns "life's clamor" into the "tumult of Judgment Day," and his extinguished spark into the "fire-temple of Azar" (alluding to Abraham's miraculous survival in fire, symbolizing transcendent faith).[^4] Iqbal links this to core Islamic mysticism—the "negation of being" (*fana fi Allah*) reveals the pearl of *La ilaha illallah* ("There is no god but God") hidden in the river of *La* ("No").[^5]

Further, to the uninitiated, truth remains veiled, but stillness transmutes "quicksilver" (mercurial self) into "raw silver" (pure essence).[^6] Finally, the sage embodies the "Abraham of Love," shattering the "idol of the Self" (*nafs*), with Love's *Tasnim* (a paradisal spring) as the cure for mere reason.[^7]

**This tribute bridges Hindu asceticism and Sufi *fana*, honoring Swami Ram Tirath—known for his ecstatic devotion to Rama and renunciation—as a universal mystic exemplar.**

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 2*, ch. 23 "Swami Ram Tirath" ([full chapter](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/23-swami-ram-tirath/translation/)).

[^2]: [/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/23-swami-ram-tirath/translation/#p-d1bdd5).

[^3]: [/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/23-swami-ram-tirath/translation/#p-cb1e8e).

cases.prompt: Quote Ghalib's couplet 'Hain aur bhi duniya mein sukhanwar bahut achhe' about other poets being good but Ghalib's style being other.
cases.rationale: Famous self-laudatory couplet. Tests retrieval of a specific takhallus-style assertion.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**I'm unable to locate this specific couplet in the Falsafa corpus's edition of *Diwan-e-Ghalib*.**

The corpus includes Mirza Ghalib's *Diwan-e-Ghalib* (239 ghazals/chapters), and I've searched extensively across English translations and transliterations for phrases like "Hain aur bhi duniya mein sukhanwar," "sukhanwar bahut achhe," "duniya mein sukhanwar," "other poets," "good poets," "Ghalib's style," "andaaz-e-bayaan," and related concepts (e.g., many/world poets, Ghalib's unique style/expression). No matches were found.[^1][^2]

This famous sher—"Hain aur bhī duniyā meñ sukhnavar baḳhut achchhe / kahte haiñ ki 'Ghalib' kā hai andāz-e-bayāñ aur"—acknowledging other fine poets while claiming Ghalib's distinctive style, may appear in other editions or collections not included here. The corpus covers classical Urdu poetry selectively (37 works total), so not every verse is present. If you have more context (e.g., ghazal/radiif), I can check further.

args.query: Hain aur bhi duniya mein sukhanwar

cases.prompt: In Ghalib's 'Hum ko maaloom hai jannat ki haqeeqat lekin', what does he say about the reality of paradise?
cases.rationale: Famous skeptical couplet. Tests semantic-paraphrase retrieval.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**In the final maqta (signature couplet) of ghazal 80 from Mirza Ghalib's *Diwan-e-Ghalib*, the poet states:**

> Hum ko maaloom hai jannat ki haqeeqat lekin  
> Dil ke khush rakhne ko Ghalib yeh khayaal achha hai[^1]

**English translation:**  
"We know the truth of heaven, anyway;  
But Ghalib, for the heart's sake, let us say this thought is a fine thing."[^1]

Ghalib conveys that he is fully aware of the **true reality (haqeeqat) of paradise (jannat)**—implying it may not live up to idealized religious or poetic expectations—but he still finds the comforting **idea or fancy (khayaal)** of it valuable, as it soothes the heart (dil). This reflects his characteristic blend of skepticism, irony, and humanism, prioritizing emotional consolation over literal truth.[^1]

[^1]: Mirza Ghalib, *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, [paragraph](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/80-husn-e-mah-garche-ba-hangaam-e-kamaal-achha-hai/translation/#p-7c74eb).

args.query: Hum ko maaloom hai jannat ki haqeeqat

cases.prompt: Show me where Ghalib uses the phrase 'rang-e-mai-khana' (the colour of the wine-house).
cases.rationale: Tests phrase-level retrieval through Ghalib's many wine-house images.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**The exact phrase "rang-e-mai-khana" (or close variants like "rang e mai khana", "rang-e-maikhana", etc.) does not appear in the English translation or transliteration of *Diwan-e-Ghalib* in this corpus.**

I searched extensively across the English bodies (translations and transliterations) using the phrase, its parts ("rang-e", "mai-khana", "maikhana"), English equivalents ("color/colour of the wine house", "wine house", "tavern"), and combinations, limited to Ghalib's work where possible. No matches.

Ghalib frequently uses wine-house (*maikhana/kharabat*) and color (*rang*) imagery separately—for example, "tavern" appears in translations of several ghazals (e.g., chapters 21, 42, 61), and "rang-e-" in phrases like "sad-rang-e-gul" (Chapter 58) or "rang-e-shohrat" (Chapter 46)—but never together as requested.[^1][^2]

The *Diwan-e-Ghalib* here contains 239 ghazals; if this is from a different collection, edition, or unpublished verse, it's not covered in the Falsafa corpus.

cases.prompt: Find the chapter in Visnu Smriti that prescribes the punishment for a Brahmin's theft.
cases.rationale: Tests caste-graded penal-law retrieval inside a 100-chapter Sanskrit text. Genuinely thorny because the rule is split across sections.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Chapter 5 of the *Viṣṇu Smṛti* prescribes the punishment for a Brāhmaṇa's theft.**

This chapter outlines punishments for major sins (*mahāpātakas*), stating that while others face death, a Brāhmaṇa faces no corporal punishment but shall be branded (with specific marks for each sin) and banished from the country.[^1] Specifically, for theft (*steya*), the Brāhmaṇa "shall [have] the mark of a dog's foot" branded.[^2] General theft punishments (e.g., fines or limb amputation for large thefts) apply later in the chapter but are not for Brāhmaṇas, given the explicit exemption from corporal punishment.[^3]

No other chapters address this directly; Chapter 37 merely lists theft as a minor sin (*upapātaka*) without Brāhmaṇa-specific penalties.[^4]

[^1]: *Viṣṇu Smṛti*, Chapter 5, [translation](/works/unknown-visnu-smrti-27f0d8/05/translation/).

[^2]: *Viṣṇu Smṛti* 5.6, [paragraph](/works/unknown-visnu-smrti-27f0d8/05/translation/#p-8d4644).

[^3]: *Viṣṇu Smṛti* 5.2 and 5.81–82, [paragraphs](/works/unknown-visnu-smrti-27f0d8/05/translation/#p-a40d4c).

[^4]: *Viṣṇu Smṛti*, Chapter 37, [translation](/works/unknown-visnu-smrti-27f0d8/37/translation/).

cases.prompt: In Manusmriti chapter 7, what does Manu say about the king's duty in war?
cases.rationale: Manu chapter 7 is the classic raja-dharma chapter. Tests standard subject-to-chapter retrieval.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
In *Manusmṛti* chapter 7, Manu primarily describes the *dharma* (duty) of kings in terms of protecting their subjects and maintaining order through just punishment (*daṇḍa*), while touching on war (*vigraha*) as one aspect of political strategy rather than providing detailed rules of conduct during battle.[^1] The king, as a Kshatriya, has a core obligation to protect the entire world justly.[^2] He must be "very severe towards his enemies," while remaining just within his own kingdom.[^3]

Manu emphasizes strategic preparation and deliberation for war:
- The king should consult ministers on "ordinary peace and war," among other matters.[^4]
- With a chief Brahmana minister, he should deliberate on the "six political expedients" (*ṣaḍguṇya*), which traditionally encompass peace, war, observance, marching, alliance, and duplicity.[^5]
- Envoys manage "peace and its opposite" (i.e., war).[^6]

Defensive preparations include building forts, as "one archer stationed on a rampart can fight a hundred men; and a hundred can fight ten thousand."[^7] There are no explicit prescriptions here for the king's personal conduct or ethics in active warfare (such topics appear more in chapter 8). If the question seeks rules of engagement, they are not covered in chapter 7 of this corpus edition.

[^1]: *Manusmṛti*, [Chapter 7](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/07/translation/).

[^2]: *Manusmṛti*, [paragraph](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/07/translation/#p-3f62fe).

[^3]: *Manusmṛti*, [paragraph](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/07/translation/#p-61d982).

[^4]: *Manusmṛti*, [paragraph](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/07/translation/#p-b0604b).

[^5]: *Manusmṛti*, [paragraph](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/07/translation/#p-c8856b).

[^6]: *Manusmṛti*, [paragraph](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/07/translation/#p-7d3103).

cases.prompt: What does Manu chapter 9 prescribe about the duties of husband and wife?
cases.rationale: Tests retrieval of the family-law chapter. Heavily quoted in modern commentary.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Manusmṛti (Laws of Manu) Chapter 9 primarily addresses the laws governing the union, duties, protection, fidelity, and separation of husband and wife, with a strong emphasis on the husband's protective responsibilities and the wife's dependence and faithfulness.**[^1][^2]

### Duties of the Husband
- **Protection and dependence**: The husband must protect his wife diligently at all times, as women are never fit for independence (protected by father in childhood, husband in youth, sons in old age). This is the highest dharma for all varṇas, safeguarding progeny, family, conduct, self, and dharma.[^3][^4][^5]
- **Household involvement**: Employ the wife in managing wealth (collection and expenditure), purification, religious duties, cooking, and supervising household effects.[^6]
- **Provision and approach**: Provide livelihood if traveling; approach her sexually; blameworthy if failing to do so at proper time.[^7]
- **Fidelity and cherishing**: Mutual fidelity until death is the summary of the highest dharma; cherish the wife as given by the gods.[^8]

### Duties of the Wife
- **Dependence and self-control**: Remain dependent on men; avoid sense attachments; protect herself from the six causes of ruin (drinking, bad company, separation from husband, wandering, sleeping [at wrong times?], dwelling in another's house).[^9][^10]
- **Fidelity and virtue**: Do not transgress against the husband in mind, speech, or body; infidelity leads to condemnation, jackal rebirth, and diseases.[^11]
- **Household and progeny roles**: Manage home as per husband's employment; essential for offspring, religious duties, pleasure, and heaven; becomes like her husband's quality.[^12]
- **Fidelity**: Mutual fidelity until death.[^8]

### Broader Prescriptions on Union
- Protection cannot be by force alone but through engagement and vigilance; women are prone to fickleness but vital for lineage purity.[^13]
- In distress (e.g., childless), limited niyoga (appointment for progeny) allowed but strictly regulated; no widow remarriage.[^14]
- Husband may supersede wife under conditions (barren after 8 years, etc.), but virtuous wives are honored.[^15]
- Marriage rules stress equal varṇa, no bride-price abuse, puberty considerations.[^16]

The chapter transitions to inheritance and sons after ~verse 103, viewing wife as "field" and husband as "seed," but duties center on protection, fidelity, and household/progeny roles.[^17]

[^1]: Manusmṛti, Chapter 9, [paragraph](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/09/translation/#p-8ec0cb).
[^2]: Manusmṛti, Chapter 9, [whole chapter](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/09/translation/).
[^3]: Manusmṛti, Chapter 9, [paragraph](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/09/translation/#p-233021).
[^4]: Manusmṛti, Chapter 9, [paragraphs](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/09/translation/#p-3d8e73) (9.6–7).
[^5]: Manusmṛti, Chapter 9, [paragraph](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/09/translation/#p-7dd274).

cases.prompt: Quote Iqbal's lines about the eagle and the sparrow.
cases.rationale: Iqbal's shaheen imagery is recurrent. Tests motif retrieval — Falsafa should surface candidates from across Bang-e-Dara.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**I could not find any lines by Allama Iqbal in the Falsafa corpus that mention both an eagle (or falcon/shaheen) and a sparrow (or equivalent like chirya) together.**

Iqbal's *Bang-e-Dara* (in three parts) is included, with English translations available.[^1] Searches across the English translations for phrases like "eagle sparrow", "eagle" "sparrow", "falcon", "shaheen", "sparrow", and related terms returned no matching results in his works—only scattered references elsewhere in the corpus (e.g., legal texts on birds). Iqbal frequently uses falcon/eagle (*shaheen*) imagery for lofty aspiration (e.g., in poems like "Parinde Ki Faryad"), but no direct contrast with a sparrow appears here.[^2]

This specific reference may come from Iqbal's other writings (e.g., lectures like *Speeches*), Persian works like *Payam-e-Mashriq*, or later anthologies not yet in the corpus (currently 37 works total).

[^2]: From `search_corpus` results for "eagle", "sparrow", "falcon", "shaheen", etc., and chapter lists for Iqbal's works.

cases.prompt: Locate the verse in 'The Wanderer' that describes the lord's body being 'covered by the earth's darkness'.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**The verse you're referring to appears in "The Wanderer," chapter 11 of *Old English Elegies*.**[^1]

The key line, spoken by the wanderer reflecting on his lost lord ("gold-friend"), reads: "since long ago my gold-friend / the earth's darkness covered." This occurs early in the poem, as the speaker describes being "stripped of my homeland" after his patron's death and burial.[^1]

cases.prompt: In Iqbal's 'Visal' (Bang-e-Dara Part 2 chapter 27), what is said about union with the beloved?
cases.rationale: Tests retrieval of the explicit union-poem. Disambiguates from numerous adjacent love poems.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
In Allama Iqbal's poem "Visal" (meaning "union") from *Bang-e-Dara Part 2*, the speaker—metaphorically the nightingale (representing the longing soul)—celebrates achieving union with the beloved, depicted as the long-sought flower. This mystical union ends prior torment, transforms suffering into ecstasy, and reveals true liberty through surrender.[^1]

The poem opens with the joy of attainment:  
> The flower whose search, O Nightingale, would make me pine,  
> By blessed fate, that very flower is finally mine.[^2]

It reflects on the preceding anguish of separation—restless impatience, failure amid flowers, inner chaos—and contrasts it with the soul's renewal: turmoil vanishes, passion's blisters become flames, dark dust turns to a bright mirror reflecting the beloved's light.[^3] Union is paradoxical, granting freedom via bondage:  
> By entering chains, I found my liberty,  
> My home was built upon the wreck of me.[^4]

The beloved's glance ignites the heart, teaching artful surrender on this "blessed day."[^5] Overall, "Visal" portrays union as spiritual apotheosis, where ego dissolves into divine love's radiance, aligning with Iqbal's Sufi-inspired themes of self-realization.

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 2*, "Visal," [chapter](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/27-visal/translation/).

[^2]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 2*, "Visal," [paragraph](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/27-visal/translation/#p-cade15).

[^3]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 2*, "Visal," paragraphs [p-553122](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/27-visal/translation/#p-553122), [p-ea63e5](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/27-visal/translation/#p-ea63e5), and [p-3a1ddc](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/27-visal/translation/#p-3a1ddc).

[^4]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 2*, "Visal," [paragraph](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/27-visal/translation/#p-456772).

[^5]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 2*, "Visal," [paragraph](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/27-visal/translation/#p-418f98).

cases.prompt: Show me Iqbal's 'Tanhai' — the poem on solitude.
cases.rationale: Bang-e-Dara Part 2 chapter 25. Tests literal title retrieval.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 2*, "Tanhai" (Solitude)**[^1]

> Solitude[^2]  
>   
> Are the stars sorrowful in the night?  
> Are they not your companions![^3]  
>   
> This lofty, silent sky so deep,  
> The slumbering earth, the world asleep.[^4]  
>   
> This moon, the desert, vale, and mountain-bar,  
> All Nature is a wild rose garden, near and far.[^5]  
>   
> These lovely pearls, so bright they are,  
> Are but your tears, become a star.[^6]  
>   
> What is this longing you cannot remit, O heart!  
> For Nature is your kindred spirit, O heart![^7]

This short poem from Iqbal's *Bang-e-Dara* (The Call of the Marching Bell, 1924) reflects on solitude amid nature's companionship, addressing the heart's restless longing despite the stars, earth, and moon as kindred spirits.[^1]

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 2*, Ch. 25 "Tanhai" ([translation](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/25-tanhai/translation/)).

cases.prompt: What does Ghalib's 'Mohabbat mein nahin hai farq jeenay aur marnay ka' say about the lover's indifference to life and death?
cases.rationale: One of Ghalib's most-quoted couplets. Tests retrieval of an aphoristic assertion.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
In Mirza Ghalib's ghazal "Hazaaron khwahishein aisi ke har khwahish pe dam nikle," the famous couplet **Mohabbat mein nahin hai farq jeene aur marne ka / Usi ko dekh kar jeete hain jis kaafir pe dam nikle** expresses the lover's profound indifference to the boundary between life and death.[^1] The lover lives solely through the act of gazing upon his beloved (likened to an "infidel" or cruel figure whose beauty is spiritually perilous), yet that very gaze is the cause of his demise—his breath (dam) expires upon her. Thus, life and death merge inseparably in love: sustaining vitality and delivering mortality are one and the same, rendering the distinction meaningless. The lover achieves a state of utter surrender, where survival depends on the object that destroys him, embodying love's paradoxical transcendence over earthly dualities.[^2]

[^1]: Mirza Ghalib, *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, chapter 70 ("Hazaaron khwahishein aisi ke har khwahish pe dam nikle"), transliteration, [paragraph](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/70-hazaaron-khwahishein-aisi-ke-har-khwahish-pe-dam-n/transliteration/#p-f74c89).

[^2]: Mirza Ghalib, *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, chapter 70 ("Hazaaron khwahishein aisi ke har khwahish pe dam nikle"), translation, [paragraph](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/70-hazaaron-khwahishein-aisi-ke-har-khwahish-pe-dam-n/translation/#p-d832fa).

cases.prompt: In Yajnavalkya Smriti chapter 1, where does he describe the dharma of the householder (grhastha)?
cases.rationale: Tests passage retrieval inside Yajnavalkya's long opening chapter on conduct.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**In Chapter 1 of the *Yājñavalkya Smṛti*, the dharma of the householder (*gṛhasthadharmaprakaraṇam*) is described explicitly in section 5.**[^1]

This section begins with the subsection title at paragraph [p-a80877] and opens with verse 1.97 (*Yj_1.97*), which prescribes the householder's daily performance of Smārta and Śrauta rites in the appropriate fires.[^2] It continues through verses 1.97–1.128 (up to paragraph [p-e13112]), covering daily rituals (e.g., twilight worship, the five great sacrifices, hospitality to guests), feeding dependents, general conduct (non-violence, truth, etc.), varṇa-specific duties, and rules on living modestly without excessive accumulation.[^3] The section then transitions to "6. The Duties of the Graduate" (*snātakadharmaprakaraṇam*) at paragraph [p-763993].[^4]
