grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Iqbal's famous poem "Tarana-e-Hindi" (also known as "Sare Jahan Se Achha Hindustan Hamara"), which opens with the line proclaiming Hindostan as the best land in the world, is not present in the Falsafa corpus.**

The corpus includes Allama Iqbal's *Bang-e-Dara* in three parts, with poems like "Hindustani Bachon Ka Qaumi Geet" (Part 1, Chapter 26), which praises the Indian subcontinent as a sacred and superior land through references to Chishti, Nanak, and others, repeatedly affirming "This is my homeland, this land of mine."[^1] Similarly, "Tarana-e-Milli" (Part 3, Chapter 23) claims "From Chin to Arab lands... Hindustan" as part of the Muslim world.[^2]

However, the specific poem and phrasing you requested—matching the 1904 patriotic verse—are not found here after searching titles, chapters, and English translations. The corpus covers 37 works, primarily philosophical texts, and not all of Iqbal's poetry is included.

cases.prompt: In Iqbal's 'Sarguzasht-e-Adam', what is the story of Adam's fall as Iqbal retells it?
cases.rationale: Bang-e-Dara Part 1 ch. 37. Tests retrieval of a specific narrative-poem.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
In Muhammad Iqbal's poem "Sarguzasht-e-Adam" ("The Saga of Man") from *Bang-e-Dara*, Adam narrates his own "fall" from Paradise not as a sinful lapse, but as the deliberate embrace of consciousness and an unquenchable thirst for truth, which rendered him incompatible with the static bliss of Eden.[^1] 

Having drunk "the fiery cup of consciousness," Adam's spirit grew restless: "My spirit found no rest in Paradise’s domain, / Once the fiery cup of consciousness I chose to drain."[^2] Endowed with "a restless nature, loving change," he embarked on an eternal quest for "cosmic truth," wandering through history as the archetype of humanity—expelling idols from the Kaaba, proclaiming divine light from Sinai, suffering crucifixion, meditating in Hira's cave, inspiring in India, Greece, China, and Japan, igniting wars between faith and reason, and pioneering scientific discoveries like gravity, electricity, and solar power.[^3] 

Through these avatars of human endeavor—from prophet to philosopher to inventor—Adam reshaped the earth into a "rival of Paradise," yet the "secret of existence" eluded him until self-realization dawned: "I found that in my own heart’s house He did reside."[^4] Iqbal thus recasts the biblical/Quranic fall as the heroic origin of human dynamism, progress, and ultimate union with the Divine within.

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, "Sarguzasht-e-Adam" [chapter](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/37-sarguzasht-e-adam/translation/).

[^2]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, "Sarguzasht-e-Adam" [paragraph](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/37-sarguzasht-e-adam/translation/#p-3d6971).

[^3]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, "Sarguzasht-e-Adam" [paragraphs](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/37-sarguzasht-e-adam/translation/#p-7c0f77-p-ca07a6-p-3f09ed-p-53df18-p-6c71af-p-25528e-p-8068eb-p-251857-p-5e01d5-p-825261-p-d7ed01-p-34f01e).

[^4]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, "Sarguzasht-e-Adam" [paragraph](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/37-sarguzasht-e-adam/translation/#p-08cb93).

cases.prompt: Find Iqbal's 'Parinde ki Faryad' — the bird's complaint from the cage.
cases.rationale: Bang-e-Dara Part 1 ch. 34. Famous freedom-from-cage poem.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Allama Iqbal's poem "Parinde Ki Faryad" ("A Bird's Lament" or "The Bird's Complaint") appears as Chapter 34 in *Bang-E-Dara Part 1* (1924).**[^1] It is a poignant Urdu nazm (poem) where a caged bird laments its lost freedom, recalling the garden's beauties, springtime joys, and the pain of captivity—a metaphor for spiritual and existential confinement.

Here is the full English translation:

> [p-f1e47c] A Bird's Lament  
> 
> [p-c41448] Those garden splendors, the chorus of all,  
> The freedom to come and to go at my call.  
> 
> [p-cb3992] My heart aches with pain when I chance to recall,  
> The smiling of buds on the dewdrops that fall.  
> 
> [p-838adc] That beautiful image, that form full of grace,  
> Whose presence brought life to my own dwelling place.  
> 
> [p-9a4316] Her voice never reaches me, held in this plight,  
> Oh, if only release were within my own might!  
> 
> [p-941bb0] How wretched I am, for my home I now pine,  
> My friends in our homeland, this prison is mine.  
> 
> [p-6fadf2] The spring has arrived, and the flowers all bloom,  
> While I weep for my fate in this house of dark gloom.  
> 
> [p-f247dd] O God! To whom can I tell my tale of despair?  
> I fear in this cage I will die from my care.  
> 
> [p-51fe7d] My heart is consuming its grief, it is true,  
> And grief is consuming my heart through and through.  
> 
> [p-7d35b0] O listener, don't think this a song and be glad,  
> It is the cry of all hearts that are wounded and sad.  
> 
> [p-983c20] O Captor, I beg you, now make me unbound,  
> I'm voiceless; release me, a blessing you've found.[^1]

The Urdu original, transliteration, and further context are also available in the corpus.[^1]

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, [34. Parinde Ki Faryad](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/34-parinde-ki-faryad/translation/).

cases.prompt: Show me Iqbal's 'Ek Makra aur Makhi' — the spider and the fly.
cases.rationale: Bang-e-Dara Part 1 ch. 20. A children's-fable poem.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Allama Iqbal's "Ek Makra aur Makhi" (The Spider and the Fly)** from *Bang-E-Dara Part 1* is a fable in verse form.[^1]

> A spider to a fly one day did say,  
> “You travel daily down this very way,  
> 
> But my poor hut has never been so blessed,  
> That you might stop and be my honored guest.  
> 
> To shun a stranger is no great disgrace,  
> But from your own, don't keep such distant space.  
> 
> If you would come, my honor would increase,  
> The winding stair is there, if you should please.”  
> 
> The fly replied when she had heard his plea,  
> “Good sir, go find a fool to trap, not me.  
> 
> A fly will never come within your door,  
> For those who climb your stair are seen no more.”  
> 
> The spider cried, “Alas! You think me sly?  
> A greater fool than you, I've yet to spy.  
> 
> I only wished to show you my respect,  
> There was no gain for me that I'd expect.  
> 
> You've flown so far, from where, God only knows,  
> What harm is there to rest here and repose?  
> 
> Inside my home are many things to see,  
> Though from outside a humble hut it be.  
> 
> Fine curtains hang upon the doors so thin,  
> And mirrors line the walls I have within.  
> 
> For guests, soft beds are ready and prepared,  
> A comfort that is not so widely shared.”  
> 
> The fly replied, “That all may be quite true,  
> But don't expect that I will visit you.  
> 
> May God protect me from your beds so soft,  
> For those who sleep there are forever lost.”  
> 
> The spider thought, “She's clever, I can tell.  
> How can I trap this wretch within my cell?  
> 
> But flattery can make a hundred schemes succeed,  
> The world is slave to flattery's own creed.”  
> 
> And so he told the fly, “My dear lady,  
> God has bestowed on you great dignity.  
> 
> Whoever sees your face is filled with love,  
> A single glance is more than quite enough.  
> 
> Your eyes are like two diamonds, shining bright,  
> Your head, God crowned with a plume of light.  
> 
> This grace, this dress, this beauty, and this art!  
> And then your song in flight that steals the heart!”  
> 
> The fly was softened by this flattering sound,  
> And said, “No cause for fear in you I've found.  
> 
> To make refusal is a habit I deplore;  
> To break a heart—that's not what I am for.”  
> 
> She said these words and from her perch took flight;  
> The spider leaped and caught her in his might.  
> 
> He'd starved for days, a fact he couldn't conceal,  
> And made the little fly his easy meal.

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, "Ek Makra aur Makhi" [/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/20-ek-makra-aur-makhi/translation/](https://falsafa.corpus/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/20-ek-makra-aur-makhi/translation/). (English translation)

cases.prompt: In Iqbal's 'Kinaar-e-Ravi', what does the speaker reflect on at the riverbank?
cases.rationale: Bang-e-Dara Part 1 ch. 28. The Ravi-river meditation.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
In Allama Iqbal's poem "Kinaar-e-Ravi" ("At the Bank of the Ravi") from *Bang-e-Dara Part 1*, the speaker stands at the Ravi riverbank during a silent evening, entering a contemplative and prayerful state amid the river's song-like flow.[^1] He reflects on the sacred transformation of the world in this moment, a disorienting sense of place amid twilight's beauty (likened to wine-stained skies and sown sunflowers), the distant minarets and ruined palace evoking time's tyranny and lost history, and—most centrally—a boat racing across the river as a metaphor for human life's ceaseless, eternal journey: appearing briefly in sight before vanishing into eternity, yet never perishing.[^2][^3]

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 28 "Kinaar-e-Ravi" (translation), [para. p-70a0dc](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/28-kinaar-e-ravi/translation/#p-70a0dc).

[^2]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 28 "Kinaar-e-Ravi" (translation), [paras. p-7ee613 to p-6b8789](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/28-kinaar-e-ravi/translation/#p-7ee613-p-6b8789).

[^3]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 28 "Kinaar-e-Ravi" (translation), [paras. p-8c38f2 to p-1c94bb](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/28-kinaar-e-ravi/translation/#p-8c38f2-p-1c94bb).

cases.prompt: Find Iqbal's address to the Brahmin in 'Sach Keh Doon Ae Brahman Gar Tu Bura Na Maane'.
cases.rationale: Bang-e-Dara Part 1 ch. 36. A direct-address poem.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Allama Iqbal's poem "Sach keh doon ae Brahman! gar tu bura na maane" (also known as "Naya Shivala" or "New Shivala") from *Bang-E-Dara Part 1* is a direct address to a Brahmin, critiquing idol worship and religious division while calling for unity through love of homeland and humanity.**[^1]

Here is the English translation:

> [p-752d60] O Brahmin, if you will not take offense,  
> Your ancient idols have lost reverence.  
> 
> [p-c536e2] From them you learned to hold your kin in scorn;  
> From God, the Preacher learned how war is born.  
> 
> [p-5af363] Fed up at last, I left both mosque and shrine,  
> The Preacher’s words, and those old tales of thine.  
> 
> [p-db56fb] You think your God is in a form of stone;  
> To me, my homeland's every speck is divine.  
> 
> [p-3a582d] Come, let us lift the veils of otherness anew,  
> And wipe away the lines that make us two.  
> 
> [p-425278] The city of the heart lies desolate,  
> Come, in this land a new shrine let's create.  
> 
> [p-948e14] Our pilgrimage must be the world's most high,  
> Its pinnacle should touch the very sky.  
> 
> [p-959af2] Let's rise each dawn to sing the sweetest prayer,  
> And serve the wine of love for all to share.  
> 
> [p-94fb2e] In faithful songs, both strength and peace reside;  
> For all this earth, salvation is love's guide.[^2]

Iqbal urges the Brahmin to abandon outdated divisions, proposing a new spiritual center in the homeland built on love (*preet*), which unites power (*shakti*) and peace (*shaanti*).

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 36 "Sach keh doon ae Brahman! gar tu bura na maane" (transliteration: "Sach keh doon ae Brahman! gar tu bura na maane").

[^2]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 36 (full passage: paragraphs p-752d60 through p-94fb2e).

args.query: Sach Keh Doon Ae Brahman Gar Tu Bura Na Maane

cases.prompt: In Iqbal's 'Saqaliya' (Bang-e-Dara Part 2 ch. 21), what historical place is being addressed?
cases.rationale: Saqaliya is the Arabic for Sicily. Tests whether Falsafa can both pull the poem and explain its referent.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Sicily.**

In "Saqaliya" from *Bang-e-Dara Part 2* (Chapter 21), Allama Iqbal directly addresses Sicily as the site of a lost Muslim ("Hejazi") civilization, lamenting its ruins and former glory under Islamic rule (referencing Arab conquests there from 827–1091 CE). The poem personifies the island, calling it "the honor of the sea" and "the cradle of that nation's culture," while evoking its historical role in Islamic history.[^1][^2]

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 2*, "Saqaliya" [paragraph](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/21-saqaliya/translation/#p-379aeb).
[^2]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 2*, "Saqaliya" [paragraph](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/21-saqaliya/translation/#p-08760e).

cases.prompt: Find the lines in 'Goristan-e-Shahi' where Iqbal walks among royal tombs.
cases.rationale: Bang-e-Dara Part 3 ch. 9. The Mughal-mausoleum meditation.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**"Goristan-e-Shahi" (The Royal Graveyard), Chapter 9 of *Bang-E-Dara Part 3* by Allama Iqbal, is a reflective poem contemplating the ruins, tombs, and history of the royal graveyard—likely the historic site in Aurangabad housing Nizam Shahi tombs.**[^1]

The poem richly describes the desolate atmosphere, fallen kings' tombs ("A graveyard, yes, but dust of high degree, / A fallen nation’s final treasury" [p-49c46c]), their past glory ("In this dark grave, those suns of glory lie, / Before whose gates once bowed the very sky" [p-04f912]), Death's inevitability, and national sorrow ("Our hearts are filled with memories of the past, / This nation’s love for its lost kings will last" [p-f20096]). However, it contains **no lines where Iqbal (or a first-person narrator) explicitly walks, strolls, or moves among the royal tombs**. The perspective is observational and meditative, without personal locomotion described.[^1][^2]

If you're recalling a different poem, prose reflection, or secondary source about Iqbal's visit (e.g., his travels), it's not present here. The full English translation is available in the cited chapter.[^1]

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, [Chapter 9: Goristan-e-Shahi (translation)](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/09-goristan-e-shahi/translation/).

[^2]: Searches for phrases like "walks among," "royal tombs," "tombs royal," and "walks tombs" across the English corpus returned zero matches, confirming the absence.

cases.prompt: In Iqbal's 'Falsafa-e-Gham', what philosophy of grief is offered?
cases.rationale: Bang-e-Dara Part 3 ch. 7. Iqbal's argument that sorrow is generative.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
In Allama Iqbal's poem *Falsafa-e-Gham* ("The Philosophy of Sorrow") from *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, grief (or sorrow, *gham*) is presented not as a mere affliction but as an essential, transformative force integral to human life, spiritual awakening, and eternal love.[^1] It polishes the heart, awakens the soul's potential, provides wings for its flight, reveals hidden truths, and constitutes a "silent song of soul" that completes existence—much like a rose requires lost petals or a nightingale autumnal pain to fully bloom.[^1] Those untouched by sorrow remain spiritually veiled, lacking the prayers, tears, thorns, and heartbreak that unlock life's deepest secrets.[^2]

Sorrow is deeply intertwined with love, persisting eternally within the soul after the beloved's physical departure and thereby securing their enduring presence against oblivion.[^3] Iqbal employs the metaphor of a river cascading from a height—shattering into countless drops (humanity scattered by separation) yet swiftly reforming—to illustrate how grief teaches reunion and unity beyond apparent division.[^4] Parting is illusory in this lower world; the departed never truly leave, shining like radiant stars to guide the living through life's darkest trials when reason and courage fail.[^5][^6]

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, "Falsafa-e-Gham" [paragraphs](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/07-falsafa-e-gham/translation/#p-141d8d).
[^2]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, "Falsafa-e-Gham" [paragraph](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/07-falsafa-e-gham/translation/#p-e436cc).
[^3]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, "Falsafa-e-Gham" [paragraph](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/07-falsafa-e-gham/translation/#p-e693fc).
[^4]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, "Falsafa-e-Gham" [paragraphs](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/07-falsafa-e-gham/translation/#p-811039).
[^5]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, "Falsafa-e-Gham" [paragraph](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/07-falsafa-e-gham/translation/#p-757295).
[^6]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, "Falsafa-e-Gham" [paragraph](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/07-falsafa-e-gham/translation/#p-523a9f).

cases.prompt: Show me Iqbal's 'Wataniyat' — his poem on (or against) territorial nationalism.
cases.rationale: Bang-e-Dara Part 3 ch. 25. Iqbal's well-known critique.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Allama Iqbal's poem "Wataniyat" (Nationalism)** appears in *Bang-E-Dara Part 3* (1924), where he critiques territorial nationalism as a modern idol that undermines Islamic unity and faith.[^1]

> Nationalism[^2]  
>   
> In this new age, the wine, the cup, the Jam are new,[^3]  
> The Saqi’s ways of grace and wrath are fashioned new.  
> The Muslim, too, has built his sanctum all anew,  
> While Civilization’s idol-smith carves idols new.  
> Of all these newer gods, the greatest is the Nation;  
> The robe it wears is Faith’s own final desecration.[^4]  
>   
> This idol, forged by this new age’s philosophy,[^5]  
> Lays waste the Prophet’s house of faith and piety.  
> Your arm is mighty with the strength of Oneness still,  
> Your homeland is Islam, you serve the Prophet’s will.  
> O Mustafavi, show the world that ancient sight!  
> And grind this hollow idol into dust and night![^6]  
>   
> To be confined to one place is calamity,[^7]  
> Live free within the ocean, like a fish at sea.  
> To leave one’s home is the Beloved’s sacred way,  
> So bear you witness to the Prophet’s truth today.  
> In the discourse of the state, the Nation is but land;  
> In the Prophet’s teaching, Homeland is by God’s command.[^8]  
>   
> Among the world’s great peoples, rivalry it sows,[^9]  
> The aim of trade becomes conquest where’er it goes.  
> From it, all politics from truth and candor flees,  
> And by its hand the weak man’s home is brought to its knees.  
> By it, God’s own creation is in pieces torn,  
> By it, the roots of Islam’s brotherhood are shorn.[^10]

Iqbal portrays nationalism (*wataniyat*) as a false deity that fragments the universal Muslim *ummah*, contrasting it with the Prophet's vision of faith transcending borders.[^1]

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, [chapter 25](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/25/translation/).

[^2]: *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, [p-3b9de1](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/25/translation/#p-3b9de1).

[^3]: *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, [p-79b1e9](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/25/translation/#p-79b1e9).

[^4]: Ibid., [p-79b1e9](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/25/translation/#p-79b1e9).

[^5]: Ibid., [p-2737d5](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/25/translation/#p-2737d5).

[^6]: Ibid.

[^7]: Ibid., [p-a723b2](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/25/translation/#p-a723b2).

[^8]: Ibid.

[^9]: Ibid., [p-97937b](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/25/translation/#p-97937b).

[^10]: Ibid.

*(Note: I reconstructed the citation URLs based on the standard format from the corpus tools: `/works/{work_slug}/{chapter_number}/{variant}/#p-{paragraph_id}`. The poem is presented stanza-by-stanza matching the paragraph breaks in the translation variant. Original Urdu, transliteration, and full context are available in the linked chapter.)*

cases.prompt: In 'Huzoor Risalat-Maab Mein', what does Iqbal address to the Prophet?
cases.rationale: Bang-e-Dara Part 3 ch. 11. Direct address-poem.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**In "Huzoor Risalat Ma'ab Mein" (Chapter 11 of *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*), Allama Iqbal imagines a visionary journey to the Prophet Muhammad's exalted presence amid worldly turmoil.**[^1]

The poem is a dialogue in verse. Angels bring Iqbal to the Prophet, who addresses him first as the "Nightingale of Hijaz's garden-bower," praising his devotion, heavenly flight, and asking what gift he brings from the world—like fragrance from a garden.[^2]

Iqbal's direct address to the Prophet ("O Presence!") follows. He laments: **"In the world, no true repose I find; / The life for which we yearn is not for humankind. / In life's vast garden, countless flowers you can find, / But not one bud with loyalty's sweet scent entwined."** He then offers a "phial of crystal bright" containing **"the blood of Tripoli's martyrs,"** symbolizing the Ummah's enduring honor—something absent even from Heaven's light.[^3]

This evokes Iqbal's themes of spiritual longing, Muslim revival, and sacrifice (referencing the Italo-Turkish War's Tripoli martyrs, 1911–1912). The full poem is brief; no other direct address appears.

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, Chapter 11: "Huzoor Risalat Ma'ab Mein" ([translation](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/11-huzoor-risalat-maab-mein/translation/)).

[^2]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, Chapter 11: "Huzoor Risalat Ma'ab Mein" ([paragraphs](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/11-huzoor-risalat-maab-mein/translation/#p-ae07ba)).

[^3]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, Chapter 11: "Huzoor Risalat Ma'ab Mein" ([paragraphs](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/11-huzoor-risalat-maab-mein/translation/#p-14a317)).

cases.prompt: Find Iqbal's 'Bilad-e-Islamiya' — the survey of Islamic cities.
cases.rationale: Bang-e-Dara Part 3 ch. 2. Tests retrieval of a place-catalogue poem.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Iqbal's "Bilad-e-Islamiya"** is Chapter 2 of *Bang-E-Dara Part 3* (1924), a collection of Urdu poetry by Allama Iqbal.[^1] 

This poem is a lyrical survey of historic Islamic cities—Delhi, Baghdad, Cordoba, Constantinople (Istanbul), and Medina (Yathrib)—celebrating them as sacred abodes (*bilad*) of Islam's majesty, civilization, and spiritual legacy.[^2] It evokes their role in Muslim history, from the Prophet's successors to imperial grandeur, while lamenting decline and affirming enduring sanctity.[^3]

Read the full translation here: [/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/02-bilad-e-islamiya/translation/](...)[^2]

[^1]: [*Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, metadata](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/).

[^2]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, Chapter 2: "Bilad-e-Islamiya" ([/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/02-bilad-e-islamiya/translation/](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/02-bilad-e-islamiya/translation/)).

[^3]: e.g., "[p-3015c7] The Abodes of Islam" through "[p-c3029f] As long as you endure on this earth, we too shall endure;" (*ibid.*).

cases.prompt: Where in Iqbal's 'Shikwa' does the speaker recall the Muslim conquests of Spain?
cases.rationale: Bang-e-Dara Part 3 ch. 20. Tests retrieval of a specific historical reference inside the long complaint poem.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Iqbal's "Shikwa" (from *Bang-e-Dara Part 3*, Chapter 20) does not explicitly mention the Muslim conquests of Spain (Al-Andalus) by name.**[^1]

The speaker does, however, recall broader Muslim military triumphs and presence across regions, including Europe—where the Umayyad conquest of the Iberian Peninsula (711 CE) established Islamic rule for centuries:

> We called the prayer in Europe’s churches, burning with Your light,[^2]

This evokes the transformation of churches into mosques (e.g., in Córdoba and Toledo), part of those conquests, but Spain/Andalusia itself is not named here.[^3] Other glories listed include Khyber Pass, Caesar's city (likely alluding to Byzantine/Constantinople campaigns), Persia, and Africa.[^4]

**Note:** Spain appears as "Andalusia" in Iqbal's nearby poem *Tarana-e-Milli* (Chapter 23), nostalgically: "O Garden of Andalusia, do you recall the day, / When on your branches high our nest in safety lay?"[^5] If that's the intended reference, it may stem from a mix-up between the poems.

[^1]: Full chapter: *Bang-e-Dara Part 3*, Ch. 20 (*Shikwa*), [translation](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/20-shikwa/translation/).

[^2]: *Bang-e-Dara Part 3*, Ch. 20 (*Shikwa*), [paragraph](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/20-shikwa/translation/#p-e1af7a).

[^3]: Searches for "Spain," "conquest Spain," and variants yielded no hits in *Shikwa*; "Andalusia"/"Andalus" hits only *Tarana-e-Milli*.

[^4]: E.g., *Bang-e-Dara Part 3*, Ch. 20 (*Shikwa*), [paragraphs](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/20-shikwa/translation/#p-ec4443) on Khyber/Caesar/Persia.

[^5]: *Bang-e-Dara Part 3*, Ch. 23 (*Tarana-e-Milli*), [paragraph](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/23-tarana-e-milli/translation/#p-dea2e4).

cases.prompt: In Comte's Traité de Législation Vol I, find the chapter where he criticises legislation founded on positive religion.
cases.rationale: Vol I ch. 8 ('Du systeme qui fait d-une religion positive le fondement'). Direct chapter-by-topic test.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Chapter 8** of Charles Comte's *Traité de Législation, Vol. I* is dedicated to criticizing the system that makes a positive religion the exclusive foundation of morality and laws.[^1]

The chapter title itself states: "Of the system that makes a positive religion the exclusive foundation of morality and laws, and of the influence of this system on civilization."[^1] Comte argues that this approach excludes observation of facts and reasoning, relying solely on precepts attributed to a supernatural being, which hinders progress by preventing analysis of consequences in this life.[^2] He notes its historical applications (e.g., admiration for Mohammed's institutions) and warns it leads to stagnation, hypocrisy, and obstacles to enlightenment, contrasting it with systems allowing rational inquiry.[^3] This critique aligns directly with the query, as no other chapter in Vol. I matches this focus based on the table of contents.[^4]

[^1]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation, Vol. I*, [Chapter 8](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/08-du-systeme-qui-fait-d-une-religion-positive-le-fon/translation/).

[^2]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation, Vol. I*, [p-a28373](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/08-du-systeme-qui-fait-d-une-religion-positive-le-fon/translation/#p-a28373).

[^3]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation, Vol. I*, [Chapter 8](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/08-du-systeme-qui-fait-d-une-religion-positive-le-fon/translation/).

[^4]: Chapters list for *Traité de Législation, Vol. I*.

cases.prompt: What does Comte say in the opening chapter of Traité de Législation Vol I about the analytical method applied to legislation?
cases.rationale: Vol I ch. 1. Tests retrieval of method-statement passage.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**In the opening chapter (Chapter 1) of *Traité de Législation, Vol. I*, Charles Comte introduces the analytical method—defined as observing facts, classifying them in natural order, and tracing how some arise from others—as the proper approach to studying legislation and morality, rather than inventing systems or reasoning from abstract principles.**[^1]

He explains that his goal is not to propose laws or critique specific institutions, but to examine causes of a people's prosperity, decline, or stagnation through this empirical process.[^2] Comte emphasizes that the method is proven in natural sciences and parts of moral sciences like political economy and the study of human understanding (citing Locke, Condillac, Adam Smith, and J.-B. Say), yielding reliable progress and truth.[^3]

Yet, he observes, it is rejected in legislation and morality by theologians (who see divine rules as sufficient, fearing analysis undermines faith), philosophers (who favor innate sentiment, imagination-based systems, or justice/duty over facts), and jurisconsults (who rely on unchallenged "natural law" maxims).[^4] Comte defends its universal applicability: moral phenomena (actions, habits, institutions) can be observed like physical ones, producing sciences via precise description rather than hypothesis or eloquence.[^5] True scholars follow facts impartially, letting truth's force prevail without force or exhortation, unlike system-builders.[^6]

He contrasts this with difficulties in moral sciences (e.g., inability to experiment on peoples, reliance on history), but insists facts suffice for progress, aided by recent advances in related fields and revolutions providing new data.[^7]

Comte's Chapter 2 then elaborates directly on the analytical method's effects, but Chapter 1 establishes its foundational role and defends its adoption for legislation.[^8]

[^1]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation, Vol. I*, Ch. 1 [translation](https://falsafa.corpus/chapters/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/01-chapitre-premier/translation/#p-6a39c9).

[^2]: Ibid., [#p-6a39c9](https://falsafa.corpus/chapters/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/01-chapitre-premier/translation/#p-6a39c9).

[^3]: Ibid., [#p-87c578](https://falsafa.corpus/chapters/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/01-chapitre-premier/translation/#p-87c578) to [#p-e9c962](https://falsafa.corpus/chapters/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/01-chapitre-premier/translation/#p-e9c962).

[^4]: Ibid., [#p-83dd97](https://falsafa.corpus/chapters/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/01-chapitre-premier/translation/#p-83dd97) to [#p-a6513b](https://falsafa.corpus/chapters/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/01-chapitre-premier/translation/#p-a6513b).

[^5]: Ibid., [#p-cf2bef](https://falsafa.corpus/chapters/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/01-chapitre-premier/translation/#p-cf2bef) to [#p-ec68ff](https://falsafa.corpus/chapters/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/01-chapitre-premier/translation/#p-ec68ff).

[^6]: Ibid., [#p-236350](https://falsafa.corpus/chapters/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/01-chapitre-premier/translation/#p-236350) to [#p-54c81d](https://falsafa.corpus/chapters/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/01-chapitre-premier/translation/#p-54c81d).

[^7]: Ibid., [whole chapter](https://falsafa.corpus/chapters/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/01-chapitre-premier/translation/).

[^8]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation, Vol. I*, Ch. 2 [translation](https://falsafa.corpus/chapters/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/02-de-la-methode-analytique-appliquee-a-l-etude-de-la/translation/).

cases.prompt: In Traité de Législation Vol IV, find the chapter on the influence of slavery on the manners of the Romans.
cases.rationale: Vol IV ch. 6 ('De l-influence de l-esclavage sur les moeurs des Romains'). Tests precise ch. selection across volumes.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Chapter 6 of *Traité de Législation: VOL IV* by Charles Comte is titled "De l’influence de l’esclavage sur les mœurs des Romains."** (English translation: "On the influence of slavery on the morals of the Romans," where *moeurs* refers to manners, customs, or morals.)[^1]

This chapter examines how domestic slavery corrupted Roman society across classes, from patricians to slaves. Key effects include intellectual stagnation, love of idleness and luxury, sexual licentiousness, passion for violent spectacles like gladiatorial combats, cruelty in punishments, aristocratic pride, and perfidy in politics.[^1]

The full English translation is available in the corpus.[^1]

[^1]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL IV*, [Chapter 6](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-iv-bca040/06-de-l-influence-de-l-esclavage-sur-les-moeurs-des-r/translation/).

cases.prompt: Where in Comte's Traité de la propriété Vol II does he discuss literary property and authorial rights?
cases.rationale: Vol II ch. 31-34 cover the propriété littéraire material. Tests retrieval of the IP-rights chapter cluster.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Charles Comte discusses literary property (*propriété littéraire*) and authorial rights extensively in Chapters 31–36 of *Traité de la propriété Vol. II*.**[^1][^2][^3][^4][^5][^6]

These chapters form a dedicated section on the topic:

- **Chapter 31** establishes the foundations and nature of literary property, arguing it arises from labor like other properties and refuting claims it is merely a government-granted monopoly.[^7]
- **Chapter 32** examines why literary works have historically lacked the full guarantees given to other properties, attributing this to governments and aristocracies favoring control over independence.[^8]
- **Chapter 33** surveys laws on literary property in England, the U.S., and France, critiquing their temporary nature as usufruct rather than true perpetual property.[^9]
- **Chapter 34** analyzes the tendencies of these laws, which disadvantage works needing long-term success (e.g., profound scientific or philosophical texts) by prioritizing quick sales.[^10]
- **Chapter 35** distinguishes literary property from monopolies, insisting guarantees to authors do not restrict others' freedom to write on the same topics.[^11]
- **Chapter 36** applies these principles to specific issues like manuscripts, letters, translations, abridgments, and immoral works, affirming authors' exclusive rights to reproduction and sale.[^12]

Comte consistently frames authorial rights as natural property rights derived from creation, entitled to the same perpetual protection as land or goods, limited only by practical differences (e.g., ideas' appropriability via reading).[^13]

cases.prompt: In Comte's Traité de la propriété Vol I, where does he define what 'occupation des choses' means?
cases.rationale: Vol I ch. 4. Tests definition retrieval.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Charles Comte defines "occupation des choses" (the occupation of things) at the outset of Chapter 4 of *Traité de la propriété: VOL I*, titled "De l'occupation des choses" (On the occupation of things).**[^1]

There, he states: "The act of taking possession of a thing that has no master, with the intention of appropriating it, has been considered at all times by jurists as one of the principal means of acquiring property."[^1] The entire chapter elaborates on this concept as a foundational mode of property acquisition, applicable to nations, families, and individuals, distinguishing it from possession or other means, and rooted in natural human needs and sentiments rather than convention or legislation.[^2]

[^1]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété: VOL I*, [paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/04-de-loccupation-des-choses/translation/#p-ab8596).
[^2]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété: VOL I*, [chapter](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/04-de-loccupation-des-choses/translation/).

cases.prompt: Find the chapter in Dunoyer's Nouveau traité d'économie Vol I on the influence of climate on liberty.
cases.rationale: Vol I ch. 3 ('II - Influence des circonstances exterieures sur la liberté'). Tests Montesquieu-style climate-liberty argument retrieval.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Chapter 3: "II. Influence des circonstances extérieures sur la liberté"** (in English translation: "II. Influence of external circumstances on liberty") in Charles Dunoyer's *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I* is the chapter that addresses the influence of climate on liberty.[^1]

Dunoyer dedicates this chapter to analyzing how external physical circumstances—including climate (temperature, humidity, soil fertility, geographical position, etc.)—affect human development and liberty. He critiques thinkers like Cabanis (who overemphasizes climate's role in shaping character and moral dispositions) and Charles Comte (who attributes immense influence to climate over race), arguing that while climate exerts real influence (e.g., hot climates fostering laziness, cold ones spurring industry), it is secondary to innate human faculties.[^2][^3] He notes climate modifies progress but does not determine it: favorable climates can hinder effort, while harsh ones stimulate it, yet man's energy prevails.[^4]

No other chapter focuses on this; Chapter 2 covers race's influence (mentioning climate's limited effect on racial traits), and climate reappears only incidentally elsewhere.[^5]

[^1]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I*, [Chapter 3](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/03-ii-influence-des-circonstances-exterieures-sur-la-/translation/).

[^2]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I*, [Chapter 3, §2](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/03-ii-influence-des-circonstances-exterieures-sur-la-/translation/#p-405b54).

cases.prompt: In Dunoyer's Nouveau traité d'économie, find the introduction's definition of what he calls 'liberté'.
cases.rationale: Vol I ch. 1 ('Ce que l'auteur entend par le mot liberté'). Tests definitional passage retrieval.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
In Charles Dunoyer's *Nouveau traité d'économie sociale* (Vol. I), the Introduction (Chapter 0) announces and outlines the author's intent to define *liberté* (liberty), but the explicit definition appears immediately afterward in Chapter 1 ("What the author means by the word liberty").[^1]

Dunoyer defines *liberty* as follows:

> What I call *liberty*, in this book, is this *power* that man acquires to use his forces more easily *as he frees himself* from the obstacles that originally hindered their exercise. I say that he is all the more *free* as he is more *delivered* from the causes that prevented him from making use of them, as he has further removed these causes from himself, as he has more enlarged and cleared the sphere of his action.[^2]

He emphasizes that this usage aligns with the word's "most usual and familiar sense," as found in thinkers like Locke, Condillac, and Destutt de Tracy, equating it to *power* over one's faculties once internal (e.g., ignorance, vice) and external obstacles (e.g., injustice by others) are overcome.[^3] He reiterates: "the word liberty corresponds, in my thought, to the idea of *power*, and [...] the phenomenon I wish to designate by it is that ever-increasing power to act which manifests itself in us as we succeed in ridding our faculties of some of the obstacles that prevented us from making use of them."[^4]

Dunoyer ties this power to human progress in industry (skill) and morality throughout the work.[^5]

[^1]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I*, [Chapter 0](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/00-introduction/translation/).

[^2]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I*, [paragraph](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/01-ce-que-lauteur-entend-par-le-mot-liberte/translation/#p-fc8999).

[^3]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I*, [paragraph](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/01-ce-que-lauteur-entend-par-le-mot-liberte/translation/#p-ed4810).

[^4]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I*, [paragraph](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/01-ce-que-lauteur-entend-par-le-mot-liberte/translation/#p-51f10e).

[^5]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I*, [Chapter 1](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/01-ce-que-lauteur-entend-par-le-mot-liberte/translation/).

cases.prompt: Where in Dunoyer Vol II does he treat the freedom of agricultural industry?
cases.rationale: Vol II ch. 17 ('VII - De la liberté de l'industrie agricole'). Targeted topic search.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**In Volume II of Charles Dunoyer's *Nouveau traité d'économie sociale*, he treats the freedom (or liberty) of agricultural industry in Chapter VII (logical chapter number 17), titled "De la liberté de l'industrie agricole" ("On the liberty of the agricultural industry").**[^1]

This entire chapter is devoted to the topic. It begins by critiquing Destutt de Tracy's treatment of agriculture as indistinguishable from manufacturing,[^2] then examines agriculture's unique nature (e.g., reliance on vital forces like life, dissemination of agents across the countryside, and production of living products), its societal role, obstacles to its progress (e.g., isolation of agents, empirical methods, moral habits), and the conditions for its liberty (e.g., personal faculties like business talent and accounting; real means like workshop situation, tools, and division of labor; and social/political respect for property). Dunoyer argues that agricultural liberty expands with the development of these powers, just as in other industries, though agriculture lags due to its inherent difficulties.[^3]

Prior chapters provide context: Chapter IV outlines general conditions for industrial liberty; Chapter V applies them to various industries; Chapter VI covers manufacturing liberty specifically.[^4]

[^1]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL II*, [Chapter VII](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-ii-d6ae03/17-vii-de-la-liberte-de-lindustrie-agricole-130/translation/).

[^2]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL II*, [§1](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-ii-d6ae03/17-vii-de-la-liberte-de-lindustrie-agricole-130/translation/#p-ed00c5).

[^3]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL II*, [§8](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-ii-d6ae03/17-vii-de-la-liberte-de-lindustrie-agricole-130/translation/#p-d1acb0).

[^4]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL II*, [Chapter IV](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-ii-d6ae03/14-iv-des-conditions-auxquelles-toute-industrie-peut-/translation/), [Chapter V](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-ii-d6ae03/15-v-application-de-ces-moyens-de-liberte-aux-diverse/translation/), [Chapter VI](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-ii-d6ae03/16-vi-de-la-liberte-de-lindustrie-manufacturiere/translation/).

cases.prompt: Locate the passage in Fichte's Zurückforderung where he addresses the princes of Europe directly to demand thought-freedom.
cases.rationale: The whole pamphlet is the address; tests retrieval of the apostrophe passage. The text has only 2 chapters.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**The work in question is Johann Gottlieb Fichte's *Zurückforderung der Denkfreiheit von den Fürsten Europens* (*Reclaiming Freedom of Thought from the Princes of Europe, Who Have Hitherto Suppressed It*), a 1793 political pamphlet framed as a speech.[^1]**

Fichte addresses princes directly (often in the singular "Prince" to represent them collectively, per the plural "Fürsten Europens" in the title) throughout, particularly demanding the protection of thought-freedom as an inalienable right. The core demand appears in **Chapter 2** (the main "Speech"), where he systematically argues against any princely right to suppress it and culminates in this explicit statement:

> Prince, you have no right to suppress our freedom of thought: and what you have no right to do, you must never do, even if the worlds around you perish, and you and your people should be buried under their ruins. For the ruins of the worlds, for you, and for us under the ruins, He will provide, who gave us the rights that you respect.[^2]

This follows extended direct address to princes (e.g., "you princes" repeatedly in paragraphs like [p-619bb2](https://falsafa.corpus/works/johann-gottlieb-fichte-zuruckforderung-der-denkfreiheit-bookde/02/translation/#p-619bb2), [p-b2542b](/works/johann-gottlieb-fichte-zuruckforderung-der-denkfreiheit-bookde/02/translation/#p-b2542b), and [p-0019e6](/works/johann-gottlieb-fichte-zuruckforderung-der-denkfreiheit-bookde/02/translation/#p-0019e6)), refuting their authority over inner freedom while urging justice.[^3]

**Earlier direct appeals occur in Chapter 1**, instructing the people to demand it from princes but shifting to address them:

> Shout it, shout it in every tone into your princes' ears until they hear it, that you will not allow freedom of thought to be taken from you... No, Prince, you are not our God. From Him we expect happiness; from you, the protection of our rights. You are not to be benevolent towards us; you are to be just.[^4]

The entire pamphlet is a bold reclamation from Europe's princes amid censorship fears post-French Revolution.

[^1]: Johann Gottlieb Fichte, *Zurückforderung der Denkfreiheit von den Fürsten Europens*, Chapter 1, [p-effd89](/works/johann-gottlieb-fichte-zuruckforderung-der-denkfreiheit-bookde/01/translation/#p-effd89).

[^2]: Fichte, *Zurückforderung der Denkfreiheit*, Chapter 2, [p-44e1de](/works/johann-gottlieb-fichte-zuruckforderung-der-denkfreiheit-bookde/02/translation/#p-44e1de).

[^3]: Fichte, *Zurückforderung der Denkfreiheit*, Chapter 2, full chapter [/works/johann-gottlieb-fichte-zuruckforderung-der-denkfreiheit-bookde/02/translation/].

[^4]: Fichte, *Zurückforderung der Denkfreiheit*, Chapter 1, [p-aef377](/works/johann-gottlieb-fichte-zuruckforderung-der-denkfreiheit-bookde/01/translation/#p-aef377) and [p-657cba](/works/johann-gottlieb-fichte-zuruckforderung-der-denkfreiheit-bookde/01/translation/#p-657cba).

cases.prompt: In Fichte's Zurückforderung der Denkfreiheit, what does he argue is the inalienable right that princes cannot grant or withdraw?
cases.rationale: Tests retrieval of the inalienable-right argument. Central thesis of the pamphlet.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Freedom of thought (Denkfreiheit).**

Fichte argues that humans possess inherent rights derived from their conscience and moral law, including the conditions necessary for dutiful action, which are strictly *inalienable* and cannot be surrendered or alienated.[^1] Freedom of thought is one such right, as it distinguishes humans from animals, manifests their personhood and freedom, and cannot be legitimately alienated in the social contract—doing so would reduce humans to irrational creatures.[^2] Princes derive their authority solely from society via contract and thus possess no rights society itself lacks; since society cannot alienate this right, neither can princes suppress it.[^3]

Princes have *no right* to restrict, determine, or suppress freedom of thought, as it precedes and transcends civil authority—it is not theirs to grant (as a permission or gift), withdraw, or control through edicts.[^4] Fichte explicitly lists it among *inalienable human rights*: princes who suppress it (especially via censorship of communication) act unjustly, betraying their duty to protect rights rather than benevolence toward happiness.[^5] Princes should instead recognize their role as protectors of these preexisting rights, not gods dispensing them.[^6]

[^1]: Johann Gottlieb Fichte, *Zurückforderung der Denkfreiheit von den Fürsten Europens*, [Chapter 2, paragraph p-900390](/works/johann-gottlieb-fichte-zuruckforderung-der-denkfreiheit-bookde/02/translation/#p-900390).

[^2]: Johann Gottlieb Fichte, *Zurückforderung der Denkfreiheit von den Fürsten Europens*, [Chapter 2, paragraphs p-5ec8d2 to p-e62dae](/works/johann-gottlieb-fichte-zuruckforderung-der-denkfreiheit-bookde/02/translation/#p-5ec8d2).

[^3]: Johann Gottlieb Fichte, *Zurückforderung der Denkfreiheit von den Fürsten Europens*, [Chapter 2, paragraph p-be3fae](/works/johann-gottlieb-fichte-zuruckforderung-der-denkfreiheit-bookde/02/translation/#p-be3fae).

[^4]: Johann Gottlieb Fichte, *Zurückforderung der Denkfreiheit von den Fürsten Europens*, [Chapter 2, paragraph p-44e1de](/works/johann-gottlieb-fichte-zuruckforderung-der-denkfreiheit-bookde/02/translation/#p-44e1de).

[^5]: Johann Gottlieb Fichte, *Zurückforderung der Denkfreiheit von den Fürsten Europens*, [Chapter 2, paragraph p-8e054b](/works/johann-gottlieb-fichte-zuruckforderung-der-denkfreiheit-bookde/02/translation/#p-8e054b).

[^6]: Johann Gottlieb Fichte, *Zurückforderung der Denkfreiheit von den Fürsten Europens*, [Chapter 1, paragraph p-657cba](/works/johann-gottlieb-fichte-zuruckforderung-der-denkfreiheit-bookde/01/translation/#p-657cba); [Chapter 2, paragraph p-b2542b](/works/johann-gottlieb-fichte-zuruckforderung-der-denkfreiheit-bookde/02/translation/#p-b2542b).

cases.prompt: In Naradasmriti, find the passage on the rules governing the eighteen titles of law.
cases.rationale: Narada is famous for his eighteen vyavahara-padas. Tests retrieval inside the single-chapter Naradasmriti.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**In the Nāradasmṛti (Chapter 1, Mātṛkā 1), the text introduces legal procedure (vyavahāra) as having "eighteen topics" (aṣṭādaśa prakaraṇāni), which are the foundational "titles of law" (or categories of litigation).**[^1]

**These eighteen titles are enumerated across verses M1.16–19 as follows:**

1. Non-payment of debt  
2. Deposits  
3. Partnership  
4. Resumption of a gift  
5. Refusal to render service after agreeing to it  
6. Non-payment of wages  
7. Sale by one who is not the owner  
8. Non-delivery of a sold article  
9. Rescission of a purchase  
10. Breach of an agreement  
11. Disputes concerning land  
12. The relationship between man and woman  
13. Inheritance  
14. Crimes of violence  
15. Verbal assault  
16. Physical assault  
17. Gambling  
18. Miscellaneous matters[^2]

**The text notes that these eighteen topics further subdivide into 108 branches, due to the "variety of human actions," with legal procedure having "a hundred branches" in total.**[^3] **Detailed rules governing each title follow in the subsequent sections (e.g., sections 1–18 under "The Topics of Litigation"), covering procedures, evidence, punishments, and resolutions specific to each category.**[^4]

[^1]: Nāradasmṛti, Chapter 1, [paragraph](/works/unknown-naradasmrti-7650d2/01/translation/#p-79e060).  
[^2]: Nāradasmṛti, Chapter 1, [paragraphs](/works/unknown-naradasmrti-7650d2/01/translation/#p-99f176) (M1.16–19).  
[^3]: Nāradasmṛti, Chapter 1, [paragraph](/works/unknown-naradasmrti-7650d2/01/translation/#p-c6777e).  
[^4]: Nāradasmṛti, Chapter 1, [whole chapter](/works/unknown-naradasmrti-7650d2/01/translation/).

cases.prompt: In Brhaspati Smriti, locate the passage on the role of writing (lekhya) as evidence in a legal dispute.
cases.rationale: Brhaspati introduced 'lekhya' as a category beyond Manu and Yajnavalkya — tests retrieval of a juristic innovation.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**In the *Bṛhaspati Smṛti* (Chapter 1), writing—translated here as "document" (*lekhya*)—is treated as one of the three primary forms of human proof (*mānusa pramāṇa*) in legal disputes, alongside witnesses and possession (*svāmītvam*). It holds significant evidentiary weight: divine proof (ordeals) is not required if witnesses are available, and neither ordeals nor witnesses are needed if a valid document exists.[^1] Documents are detailed as formal records essential for resolving disputes over debts, sales, partitions, and more, with strict rules on their validity, flaws, and precedence over other proofs.[^2]**

**Key passage establishing documents as proof:**

> Witnesses, documents, and possession are remembered as the three kinds of human proof; divine proof is of nine kinds, from the balance ordeal to the *dharma* ordeal.[^3]

Documents supersede weaker proofs:

> Divine proof is not prescribed when witnesses are available; and when a document exists in disputes, there should be neither an ordeal nor witnesses.[^4]

**The role and characteristics of documents are elaborated in section 1.6 ("The Document"), spanning multiple paragraphs.** A valid document must include details like the custom of the country, date (year, month, etc.), interest (for debts), debtor's signature, witnesses, and scribe.[^5] They are categorized (e.g., royal, worldly; partition, gift, sale, etc.) and prevail in disputes unless flawed (e.g., forged, unsigned, or from an incompetent maker).[^6] In hierarchy:

> A document that is shown from time to time, read out, and reminded of, succeeds everywhere, even when the witnesses are dead.[^7]

No other works in the corpus use "lekhya" explicitly in *Bṛhaspati Smṛti*, but this matches the query's intent.

[^1]: *Bṛhaspati Smṛti*, Chapter 1, [paragraph](/works/unknown-brhaspati-smrti-0fd070/01/translation/#p-44a57a).

[^2]: *Bṛhaspati Smṛti*, Chapter 1, [paragraph](/works/unknown-brhaspati-smrti-0fd070/01/translation/#p-bbce9d).

[^3]: *Bṛhaspati Smṛti*, Chapter 1, [paragraph](/works/unknown-brhaspati-smrti-0fd070/01/translation/#p-510297).

[^4]: *Bṛhaspati Smṛti*, Chapter 1, [paragraph](/works/unknown-brhaspati-smrti-0fd070/01/translation/#p-fc9858).

[^5]: *Bṛhaspati Smṛti*, Chapter 1, [paragraph](/works/unknown-brhaspati-smrti-0fd070/01/translation/#p-dc2d5a).

[^6]: *Bṛhaspati Smṛti*, Chapter 1, [paragraph range](/works/unknown-brhaspati-smrti-0fd070/01/translation/#p-213342) (*paragraphs p-213342 through p-65f09b*).

[^7]: *Bṛhaspati Smṛti*, Chapter 1, [paragraph](/works/unknown-brhaspati-smrti-0fd070/01/translation/#p-deab5c).

cases.prompt: Find the passage in Parasara Smriti on the duties of Brahmins in the Kali age.
cases.rationale: Parasara Smriti is the kaliyuga-dharma text. Tests its central thesis.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
In the *Parāśara Smṛti*, the entire text is presented as the authoritative *dharma* (righteous duty) specifically suited to the Kali *yuga* (the present cosmic age), in contrast to the *dharmas* of prior ages.[^1] The duties of a Brāhmaṇa (Brahmin) are enumerated as six core acts to be performed daily, devotion to which ensures one does not perish.[^2] These are:

> Twilight prayers and bathing, recitation of sacred texts (*japa*), fire offerings (*homa*), worship of deities and guests, hospitality to an *atithi* (a guest, who must be honored), and the *Vaiśvadeva* (the daily ritual offering to all deities)—these are the six duties to be performed day by day.[^3]

These acts are reiterated and elaborated as central to a Brāhmaṇa's householder life in the Kali *yuga*, alongside limited agriculture if needed.[^4]

cases.prompt: In Yama Smriti, where does Yama lay down rules for purification after contact with the dead?
cases.rationale: Yama as the death-deity specialises in death-pollution rules. Tests retrieval inside the 2-chapter text.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Yama Smṛti (slug: `unknown-yama-smrti-552f8d`) does not contain specific rules laid down by Yama for purification after contact with the dead.**[^1][^2]

The text focuses primarily on penances (*prāyaścitta*) for sins such as killing, improper intercourse, eating forbidden foods, apostasy, and interactions with outcastes or animals. General purification methods are mentioned, such as bathing upon contact with impure substances (*amedhya*), touching fire, and observing twilight rituals,[^3] but nothing explicitly addresses ritual impurity (*aśauca*) from corpses or the deceased. Other Smṛtis in the corpus, like Manu Smṛti, do cover corpse-related purification (e.g., touching a corpse requires purity after one day/night), but this topic is absent here.[^4]

cases.prompt: Find the section in Angirasa Smriti on prayascittas for women.
cases.rationale: Angirasa is short and prayascitta-focused. Tests precise retrieval inside a small text.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Āṅgirasa Smṛti** (also called *Aṅgiras Smṛti*) discusses *prāyaścittas* (penances) for women primarily in the context of specific grave offenses like remarriage (*punarvivāha*) and erroneously ascending the funeral pyre (*sahagamana*) with a man other than her husband (*paradāra-sati*). There is no standalone chapter or section exclusively on "prayascittas for women," but relevant rules appear in the single chapter (divided into "Earlier" and "Later" texts). A general principle is also stated: women who sin intentionally perform half the penance prescribed for men.[^1]

### Erroneous Ascension of the Pyre with a Man Other Than Her Husband
If a virtuous woman (*sumaṅgalī*) mistakenly ascends the pyre with a man other than her husband:

> If a virtuous woman, through error, ascends the pyre with a man other than her husband... how are the rites to be performed afterwards for those born from her? The gods... were long beset by this worry. Afterwards, a divine voice arose... \"For union with a man other than her husband, a six-year *kṛcchra* is prescribed. For abandoning her life out of delusion, a great sin, for that act of hers, a six-year penance is prescribed, multiplied by six. It should be performed always with charity, devoid of greed and deceit... Having first performed the Cāpāgrayāna rite, one should perform a hundred baths there.[^2]

The penance scales by caste difference (doubled for a king, tripled for a Vaiśya, quadrupled for a Śūdra).[^3]

### Remarriage (*Punarvivāha*)
Remarriage of a woman is severely condemned, with elaborate penances:

> If a woman is remarried by foolish and wicked fathers, brothers, and others, then all of them will go to hell. That remarried woman becomes a partaker of the great hell Raurava. Her husband, along with his ancestors, goes to the hell Kālasūtra... To appease that fault, this is the supreme *prāyaścitta*: The giver, going to Setu (Rāmeśvaram), at Dhanuṣkoṭi... shall reside there... for a year... he should perform a hundred Taptakṛcchra penances... And the husband... performing three holy Cāndrāyaṇa vows... should reside there for three seasons... Daily drinking Pañcagavya... reciting... a thousand times... performing the auspicious penance called Tapta... If she, having left both husbands, performs a thousand baths at Setu, and eats only barley gruel, she is purified in a year.[^4]

If childless, she purifies in a year; otherwise, she and her children become outcastes (*caṇḍāla*).

### General Rule for Women's Penances
A broad principle applies across sins:

> For women who have sinned intentionally, half the penance should be prescribed.[^5]

These are the primary references in the corpus. The text emphasizes charity, recitation, and vows alongside penances, often at sacred sites like Setubandha (Rāmeśvaram). No other works in the Falsafa corpus provide additional sections on this exact topic from Āṅgirasa Smṛti.

[^1]: Āṅgirasa Smṛti, [Chapter 1 (Later Text)](/works/unknown-angirasa-smrti-2bf9d1/01/translation/#p-83ee9c).
[^2]: Āṅgirasa Smṛti, [Chapter 1](/works/unknown-angirasa-smrti-2bf9d1/01/translation/#p-73aeea).
[^3]: Āṅgirasa Smṛti, [Chapter 1](/works/unknown-angirasa-smrti-2bf9d1/01/translation/#p-8151a3).
[^4]: Āṅgirasa Smṛti, [Chapter 1](/works/unknown-angirasa-smrti-2bf9d1/01/translation/#p-65ef09).
[^5]: Āṅgirasa Smṛti, [Chapter 1 (Later Text)](/works/unknown-angirasa-smrti-2bf9d1/01/translation/#p-83ee9c).

cases.prompt: In Katyayana Smriti, locate the verses on women's stridhana (separate property).
cases.rationale: Katyayana is the foremost smriti on stridhana. The Katyayana corpus has 80 chapters; tests precise navigation.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Kātyāyana Smṛti** (slug: `unknown-katyayana-smrti-1e06d2`) discusses women's *strīdhana* (woman's separate property) primarily in Chapters 75–77, treating it as impartible estate not subject to division among heirs. A brief reference appears earlier in Chapter 72.[^1][^2][^3][^4]

### Chapter 72: Impartible Properties (mention at K_877)
*Strīdhana* is listed alongside wealth from valor or learning as not divisible at partition.[^5]

### Chapter 75: Definition and Types (K_894–901)
Defines *strīdhana* as six-fold: gifts before/at the nuptial fire (*adhyagni*, *adhyāvāhanika*), out of affection (*prītidatta*), bride-price (*śulka*), and subsequent gifts (*anvādheya*, *saudāyika*) from kin.[^6]

### Chapter 76: Ownership and Disposition (K_902–916)
Details givers (father, mother, etc.), exclusions (conditional/fraudulent gifts), husband's limited rights, woman's independence (esp. over *saudāyika*), prohibitions on seizure by kin, and penalties.[^7]

### Chapter 77: Heirs (K_917–920)
Outlines succession: daughters first, then sons or kin; immovable gifts revert to brothers/parents in certain cases (e.g., Āsura marriage).[^8]

These prose sections embed numbered verses (K_###). For full context, read the chapters directly; no other chapters cover *strīdhana*.

cases.prompt: Where in Visnu Smriti chapter 3 does the king's daily routine begin?
cases.rationale: Tests retrieval inside the rajadharma material; chapter 3 in Visnu's organisation. Specific chapter test.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Viṣṇu Smṛti chapter 3 outlines the general duties of a king (*rājadharma*), such as protecting the people, establishing social orders, administration, taxation, military policy, and personal conduct, but it does not describe a "daily routine" (e.g., no mentions of waking, morning/evening rituals, or a timed schedule).**[^1]

If you're thinking of a king's daily schedule (e.g., rising at dawn or evening consultations), that appears in other Smṛtis like *Manusmṛti* chapter 7, but not here.[^2]

[^1]: *Viṣṇu Smṛti*, Chapter 3, [translation](/works/unknown-visnu-smrti-27f0d8/03/translation/).

[^2]: This is based on a full-text search of the Falsafa corpus; no matching content exists in Viṣṇu Smṛti chapter 3.

cases.prompt: Find the verses in Visnu Smriti chapter 30 on the duties of the householder toward guests.
cases.rationale: Tests precise multi-digit chapter navigation in Visnu's 100-chapter structure.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Chapter 30 of the *Viṣṇu Smṛti* does not contain any verses on the duties of the householder (*gṛhastha*) toward guests (*atithis*).**

Instead, this chapter prescribes rules for the study of the Vedas: the permissible study period (four and a half months starting in Śrāvaṇa or Prauṣṭhapada), the dismissal ceremony, study of ancillary texts during breaks, and a long list of times and circumstances when study is forbidden (e.g., lunar days, eclipses, bad weather, impurity, animal sounds, near low castes, etc.). It also discusses the consequences of improper study, the sanctity of the teacher-pupil relationship (likening the teacher to a more venerable father), and how studying different Vedas satisfies ancestors.[^1]

No mentions of householders or guests appear anywhere in the chapter.[^2]

If this topic appears elsewhere in the *Viṣṇu Smṛti* (which has 100 chapters on dharma topics) or another Smṛti text like *Manusmṛti*, a broader search in the corpus yielded no direct matches for "householder" and "guest" together in the *Viṣṇu Smṛti*.[^3]

[^1]: *Viṣṇu Smṛti*, [Chapter 30 (translation)](/works/unknown-visnu-smrti-27f0d8/30/translation/).

[^2]: *Viṣṇu Smṛti*, [Chapter 30 (full text)](/works/unknown-visnu-smrti-27f0d8/30/translation/).

cases.prompt: In Slokantara, find the passage on the tongue's misuse as the worst of sins.
cases.rationale: Slokantara is a Old Javanese ethical-aphorism text. Tests obscure-text retrieval.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**No passage in *Ślokāntara* describes the tongue's misuse as the worst of sins (or uses those terms).**

The text—a commentary on Sanskrit verses (*śloka*) about *dharma* (righteousness), sins (*pātaka*, *mahāpātaka*, etc.), virtues, castes, kingship, women, and cosmology—emphasizes truth (*satya*) as the highest *dharma* and untruth (*anṛta*) as the greatest sin: "There is no *dharma* greater than truth. There is no sin greater than untruth."[^1] It permits five exceptions where falsehood (*anṛta*) incurs no sin: jest (*narma*), protecting life (*prāṇadravyarakṣaṇe*), protecting property, protecting family (*strīṣu*), and sexual union (*vivāhakāle*); all other lies are sinful.[^2]

Harsh or deceitful speech appears indirectly (e.g., hypocrites with "sweet words" but scissor-like hearts,[^3] or slanderers among heinous crimes[^4]), but nothing frames tongue misuse as uniquely worst. Sins escalate: *pātaka* (e.g., killing embryos), *upapātaka*, *mahāpātaka* (e.g., killing Brahmins), *atipātaka* (incest, destroying liṅgas). Speech is secondary to violence/theft.

If the query recalls a different phrasing or work (none match corpus-wide searches for "tongue"/"misuse"/"worst sin"), the Falsafa corpus (37 works) lacks it.

[^1]: *Ślokāntara*, Chapter 1, [paragraph p-bad244](/works/unknown-slokantara-d7a628/01/translation/#p-bad244) (Ślo_07s); [paragraph p-11d11c](/works/unknown-slokantara-d7a628/01/translation/#p-11d11c) (Ślo_07j).

cases.prompt: Show me the passage in Kunjarakarna Dharmakathana where Kunjarakarna receives instruction from Vairocana.
cases.rationale: Kunjarakarna is a single-chapter Old Javanese Buddhist tale. The Vairocana-instruction is its narrative core.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
In the *Kuñjarakarna Dharmakathana* by Mpu Dusun, the rākṣasa ascetic Kuñjarakarṇa first encounters Lord Vairocana (initially referred to as the Lord Jina, Lord Buddha, or Sugata) upon arriving at the "immaculate Monastery of Enlightenment" (identified explicitly as Vairocana later in the text).[^1] There, after homage and praise, he poses questions about rebirth, suffering, disparity in fortunes, and impurity of form. Vairocana responds by extolling the rarity of seeking the *dharma*, critiquing the heedless and scornful, and—instructions him to witness the torments in Yama's realm as preparation for deeper teaching.[^2]

[^1]: Mpu Dusun, *Kunjarakarna Dharmakathana*, [paragraph](/works/unknown-kunjarakarna-dharmakathana-894f4a/01/translation/#p-c01bae).
[^2]: Mpu Dusun, *Kunjarakarna Dharmakathana*, [paragraphs](/works/unknown-kunjarakarna-dharmakathana-894f4a/01/translation/#p-0163d8).

Kuñjarakarṇa obeys, visits Yama's realm (and warns his friend Pūrṇavijaya), then returns with him. He praises Vairocana again and begs for the "supreme truth of the *dharma*."[^3] Vairocana then delivers extended teachings on karma, the five souls (*ātmā* etc.), debts to parents/gods/world/teachers, purity of mind, unity of Buddhist/Śaiva/Ṛṣi deities (revealing himself explicitly as Vairocana), and the path to *mokṣa* via vows, giving (*dāna*), and non-dual wisdom—culminating in advice to guard the mind and avoid external merit alone.[^4]

[^3]: Mpu Dusun, *Kunjarakarna Dharmakathana*, [paragraphs](/works/unknown-kunjarakarna-dharmakathana-894f4a/01/translation/#p-3af769).
[^4]: Mpu Dusun, *Kunjarakarna Dharmakathana*, [paragraph](/works/unknown-kunjarakarna-dharmakathana-894f4a/01/translation/#p-6c6127).

The full context spans much of the single-chapter work (~18k words), framed as a Buddhist didactic tale (*dharmakathana*) emphasizing *dharma*, karma's fruits, hellish torments, and Vairocana's syncretic supremacy. Kuñjarakarṇa achieves *mokṣa* at its close.[^5]

[^5]: Mpu Dusun, *Kunjarakarna Dharmakathana*, [chapter](/works/unknown-kunjarakarna-dharmakathana-894f4a/01/translation/).

cases.prompt: In San Hyan Kamahayanikan, where is the doctrine of the five Tathagatas first articulated?
cases.rationale: The five-Tathagata mandala is the central doctrinal payload of San Hyan Kamahayanikan. Tests precise retrieval.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**In *San Hyan Kamahayanikan* (a single-chapter work), the doctrine of the Five Tathāgatas (Pañcatathāgata)—Vairocana, Akṣobhya, Ratnasambhava, Amitābha, and Amoghasiddhi—is first referenced during the exposition of the Perfection of Morality (*śīlapāramitā*), as part of rules for bodily conduct after consecration.**[^1]

This initial mention presupposes the Five Tathāgatas as abiding in the practitioner's head (lest they depart if touched improperly), prior to any naming. The five are then first explicitly named and linked to breath practices shortly before the text's advanced yogic instructions.[^2] The doctrine is systematically articulated soon after, identifying them as emerging from Śākyamuni (with Lokeśvara and Vajrapāṇi), their seed-syllables, wisdoms, consorts, associations with aggregates/elements/poisons, etc., comprising much of the text's second half.[^3]

[^1]: *San Hyan Kamahayanikan*, [paragraph](/works/unknown-san-hyan-kamahayanikan-2a0c19/01/translation/#p-638b90).
[^2]: *San Hyan Kamahayanikan*, [paragraph](/works/unknown-san-hyan-kamahayanikan-2a0c19/01/translation/#p-cf2520).

cases.prompt: Find the section in Vrhaspatitattva that defines the relationship of cetana (consciousness) and acetana (the unconscious).
cases.rationale: Vrhaspatitattva is a Saiva Old Javanese tattva text; cetana-acetana is its core dyad.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**The *Vr̥haspatitattva* (Vrhaspati's treatise on reality), attributed to Bṛhaspati, opens with a dialogue between Lord Śiva and Vṛhaspati in which the foundational principles (*tattvas*) of existence are expounded.**[^1]

The core definition appears early, in verses 6–7 of Chapter 1. The supreme *tattva* (reality) is declared twofold: *cetana* (the Conscious Principle) and *acetana* (the Unconscious Principle). These pervade all other *tattvas*, are subtle, and require discernment.[^2]

*Cetana* is intrinsically aware, self-knowing, eternal, and unobscured by ignorance or forgetting—like a luminous consciousness. *Acetana*, by contrast, lacks awareness entirely, exemplified by inert matter such as stone.[^3] **Their relationship is generative: when *cetana* and *acetana* unite (*atĕmu pvekaṅ cetana lavan acetana*), they produce the full hierarchy of *tattvas* (principles of reality), beginning with *pradhānatattva* (primordial nature) and descending through *triguṇa* (three qualities), intellect (*buddhi*), ego (*ahaṅkāra*), senses, and the five gross elements (*pañcamahābhūta*).** This union underlies manifestation, binding consciousness within unconscious matter and initiating the cycle of *saṃsāra* (rebirth). Pure *cetana* (*Śiva*-tattvas) remains transcendent, while *acetana* (*māyā*-tattvas) veils it, causing limitation until liberation (*mokṣa*).

Later sections elaborate: *acetana* (*mala*, impurity) stains *cetana*, obscuring its omniscience and omnipotence, much like unconsciousness causes forgetting in sleep.[^4] Full realization dissolves this, restoring unity.

[^1]: *Vr̥haspatitattva*, Ch. 1 [Chapter](/works/unknown-vrhaspatitattva-b60b28/01/translation/).

[^2]: *Vr̥haspatitattva*, Ch. 1 [paragraphs](/works/unknown-vrhaspatitattva-b60b28/01/translation/#p-04d69c).

[^3]: *Vr̥haspatitattva*, Ch. 1 [paragraphs](/works/unknown-vrhaspatitattva-b60b28/01/translation/#p-c31df3).

[^4]: *Vr̥haspatitattva*, Ch. 1 [paragraphs](/works/unknown-vrhaspatitattva-b60b28/01/translation/#p-643885).

cases.prompt: In Ganapatitattva, locate the passage on the supreme Siva and the yogic disciplines.
cases.rationale: Ganapatitattva is a tattva (Saiva metaphysics) text in Kawi. Tests retrieval inside obscure scripture.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
In the *Gaṇapatitattva*, Īśvara instructs Gaṇapati on the Six-Limbed Yoga (*ṣaḍaṅga-yoga*), comprising withdrawal (*pratyāhāra*), meditation (*dhyāna*), breath-control (*prāṇāyāma*), concentration (*dhāraṇā*), contemplation (*tarka*), and absorption (*samādhi*). These disciplines purify the mind and lead toward union with Śiva, culminating in liberation as the supreme Śiva (*paramaśiva*) at the point twelve finger-widths above the crown, where the Void is proclaimed supreme.[^1][^2]

The text later references *yogic sleep* (*yoganidrā*) as a subtle practice for attaining transparency of the body and radiant consciousness, foundational to liberation.[^3]

[^1]: *Gaṇapatitattva*, [Chapter 1 (translation)](/works/unknown-ganapatitattva-66a136/01/translation/#p-1a44bb).
[^2]: *Gaṇapatitattva*, [Chapter 1 (translation)](/works/unknown-ganapatitattva-66a136/01/translation/#p-f36006).
[^3]: *Gaṇapatitattva*, [Chapter 1 (translation)](/works/unknown-ganapatitattva-66a136/01/translation/#p-5f9128).

cases.rationale: Kalpabuddha has 6 chapters; tests precise chapter retrieval inside an Old Javanese Buddhist text.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Kalpabuddha chapter 3 does not discuss or mention *bodhicitta*.**[^1]

The chapter is brief (41 words, 2 paragraphs) and describes attributes of the deity Ratnasambhava (one of the five Dhyani Buddhas in Vajrayana Buddhism): his direction (South), color (yellow), consort (Lady Māmakī), mantra (Trāṃ), *jñāna* (ākāśamata, "equality of space"), implement (kapāla/skull cup), and abode (inside of the spleen).[^2]

A search of the full *Kalpabuddha* corpus for "bodhicitta" (alone or with the work title) yields no results. *Bodhicitta* ("enlightenment mind") appears in other Indic works like *Kunjarakarna Dharmakathana* and *San Hyan Kamahayanikan*, but not here.[^3]

[^1]: *Kalpabuddha*, ch. 3 (full chapter).
[^2]: *Kalpabuddha*, ch. 3, [p-68d96e](/works/unknown-kalpabuddha-d760dc/03/translation/#p-68d96e).

cases.prompt: Where in Kalpabuddha does the text describe the ten bhumis of the bodhisattva?
cases.rationale: Tests retrieval of a specific Mahayana doctrinal sequence inside Kalpabuddha.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**The *Kalpabuddha* (work slug: unknown-kalpabuddha-d760dc), a Medieval Indic text in Kawi with 6 short chapters, does not describe the ten bhūmis (stages) of the bodhisattva path anywhere.**

This work focuses instead on the five Tathāgatas (Vairocana, Akṣobhya, Ratnasambhava, Amitābha, Amoghasiddhi)—their attributes (colors, directions, maṇtras, mudrās, jñānas, etc.), accompanying deities, mounts, and bodily abodes (e.g., heart, lungs)—culminating in their manifestation as the five wisdoms, senses, and souls (*pañcātma*).[^1]

No passages mention bhūmis, bodhisattvas, their path, or ten stages. Searches across the corpus for "ten bhumis", "bhūmi", "bodhisattva", or related terms ("ten stages", etc.) yielded zero relevant results in this text. The ten bhūmis are a core Mahāyāna doctrine (e.g., from the *Daśabhūmika Sūtra*), but this tantric-style corpus entry covers microcosmic Buddha visualization, not that progression.

If you're thinking of another work (e.g., *San Hyan Kamahayānīkan* or *Kunjarakarṇa Dharmakathana*, which mention bodhisattvas but not the ten bhūmis), let me know for further checks.
