cases.prompt: Comte derives property from liberty's exercise on territory; Manu's book 7 frames the king's relation to land in dharmic terms. Pull each foundational claim. The starting points differ; where does the resulting royal authority differ?
cases.rationale: Cross-tradition property-and-kingship comparison.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Charles Comte's Foundational Claim (*Traité de la propriété*, Vol. I)**

Comte derives property fundamentally from *liberty*, defined as the absence of obstacles from fellow humans to one's natural development and innocent exercise of faculties.[^1] This liberty enables the *occupation* of unowned things—especially territory—via human industry and labor, transforming valueless elements into properties essential for existence and progress.[^2] Nations first occupy and develop their territory as collective property through prior possession and cultivation; this is then subdivided into private properties when individuals cultivate land, increasing overall utility without usurping from others (as cultivation shrinks the space needed per person, freeing land for more).[^3][^4] Property thus flows naturally from free action on external things, prior to any government.

**Manu’s Foundational Claim (*Manusmṛti*, Book 7)**

The king arises dharmically, created by the Lord from the world's primordial chaos (when all scattered in fear without protection) to safeguard *dharma* for all beings.[^5] Composed of divine particles from gods like Indra, Agni, Yama, etc., the king embodies transcendent splendor and power.[^6] He wields *daṇḍa* (punishment), itself a divine creation from Brahman's luster, as the embodiment of dharma to enforce order across varṇas and āśramas, preventing the strong from devouring the weak.[^7] The kingdom yields him annual tribute collected by agents; he rules as a paternal protector devoted to sacred law.[^8] Land/kingdom relates to the king as a domain for protection and revenue under dharma, not individual occupation.

**Differences in Resulting Royal Authority**

The starting points yield divergent royal powers. Comte's liberty-grounded property implies *limited, protective* authority: government (including any "royal" figure) emerges post-property to secure natural rights like occupation and labor fruits, rejecting divine or coercive claims over persons/lands as slavery's negation; violations (e.g., tribute as extortion) infringe liberty.[^9] No inherent royal ownership—territory is national property from collective liberty, then private.

Manu's dharmic creation yields *coercive, paternal-divine* authority: the king, as godly protector, imposes *daṇḍa* to uphold cosmic order, collecting tribute from the kingdom as his due; his relation to land is proprietary (revenue source) and hierarchical, with subjects as protected dependents rather than free proprietors. The corpus lacks direct statements on Comte's "royal" views (focusing individual/national property), but his anti-slavery principles preclude Manu's absolute *daṇḍa*-wielding dominion.[^10]

[^1]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété*, [paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/03-de-ce-qui-constitue-la-liberte/translation/#p-1c5676).

[^2]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété*, [paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/04-de-loccupation-des-choses/translation/#p-e47482).

[^3]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété*, [paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/07-du-territoire-propre-a-chaque-nation/translation/#p-62e340).

[^4]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété*, [paragraphs](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/10-de-la-conversion-du-territoire-national-en-proprietes-privees/translation/#p-6c84d4) and [](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/10-de-la-conversion-du-territoire-national-en-proprietes-privees/translation/#p-30fbf4).

[^5]: *Manusmṛti*, [paragraph](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/07/translation/#p-915921).

[^6]: *Manusmṛti*, [paragraph](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/07/translation/#p-82334c).

[^7]: *Manusmṛti*, [paragraph](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/07/translation/#p-73f910).

[^8]: *Manusmṛti*, [paragraph](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/07/translation/#p-99f678).

[^9]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété*, Ch. 3 (full chapter defines liberty vs. servitude, implying protective limits on authority).[^10]: *Manusmṛti*, [paragraphs](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/07/translation/#p-915921) to [](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/07/translation/#p-99f678) (king as divine *daṇḍa*-enforcer).

cases.prompt: Iqbal's 'Wataniyat' rejects territorial nationalism; Comte argues territorial nation-building can produce moral progress. Pull each operative claim. Where is the disagreement substantive, and where is it just a difference of vocabulary?
cases.rationale: Cross-author territorial-politics comparison forced to substantive-vs-vocabulary call.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Iqbal's operative claims in "Wataniyat" (Bang-e-Dara Part 3, Ch. 25):**  
Iqbal portrays modern nationalism (*wataniyat*) as a false idol invented by a materialistic age, supplanting Islamic unity and faith.[^1] It desecrates religion by masquerading as faith while confining believers to a single territory, which he deems a calamity compared to the expansive, divinely ordained Islamic *homeland*.[^2] Nationalism fosters rivalry among peoples, turns commerce into conquest, empowers the strong against the weak, and severs the universal brotherhood of Islam and God's creation.[^3]

**Comte's operative claims on territorial nations (Traité de la propriété Vol. I):**  
Each natural human grouping (a "nation" or tribe) possesses an exclusive *territorial property*—a basin-like region defined by geography (rivers, mountains)—as a factual, indestructible foundation predating history and civil laws.[^4] This national territory enables survival and subdivision into private properties via cultivation, which advances humanity from barbarism (hunting vast uncultivated lands) to civilization: supporting exponentially more population, wealth, security, and industry without usurping others (as cultivators "abandon" unused lands, enriching the common stock).[^5] Territorial nations thus underpin liberty, property rights, and social/economic *progress*, with stronger guarantees as societies develop.[^6]

**Substantive disagreement vs. vocabulary difference:**  
The clash is largely **substantive**, not mere words. Iqbal rejects *territorial confinement* as spiritually stultifying and socially divisive—an idolatrous modern force eroding Islamic universalism and brotherhood.[^2][^3] Comte affirms *territorial property* as a natural, unifying prerequisite for moral and material *progress*: enabling secure families, population growth, markets, and liberty from savage scarcity.[^5][^6] Where Iqbal sees territory-bound "nations" breeding conquest and fragmentation, Comte sees them as protective bases for human flourishing.  

A **vocabulary nuance** exists: Iqbal targets *nationalism* (*wataniyat*) as a 20th-century ideology deifying the *modern nation-state*.[^1] Comte describes pre-modern, organic *nations/peoples* (tribes/hordes) with innate territories, akin to extended families—not ideological fetishes, but empirical facts fostering development.[^4] Yet this hardly dissolves the rift: both address territory's role in group identity, but diverge on whether it elevates (Comte) or degrades (Iqbal) humanity. No corpus text has Comte explicitly using "moral progress," but he ties territorial foundations to ethical goods like justice, non-usurpation, and anti-barbarism.[^5]

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, Ch. 25 "Wataniyat" [paragraph](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/25-wataniyat/translation/#p-3b9de1).  
[^2]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, Ch. 25 "Wataniyat" [paragraph](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/25-wataniyat/translation/#p-a723b2).  
[^3]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, Ch. 25 "Wataniyat" [paragraph](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/25-wataniyat/translation/#p-97937b).  
[^4]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété VOL I*, Ch. 7 "Du territoire propre à chaque nation" [paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/07-du-territoire-propre-a-chaque-nation/translation/#p-b9fe30).  
[^5]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété VOL I*, Ch. 10 "De la conversion du territoire national en propriétés privées" [paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/10-de-la-conversion-du-territoire-national-en-proprie/translation/#p-30fbf4).  
[^6]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété VOL I*, Ch. 10 "De la conversion du territoire national en propriétés privées" [paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/10-de-la-conversion-du-territoire-national-en-proprie/translation/#p-919e29).

cases.prompt: 'The Ruin' contemplates a fallen Roman city without naming it. Iqbal's 'Saqaliya' names Sicily. Find the most charged line in each. What does naming or refusing-to-name commit each speaker to?
cases.rationale: Cross-tradition grief-of-empire comparison forced to a naming question.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
In "The Ruin," an anonymous Old English elegy contemplating the remnants of a Roman city (likely Bath), the speaker refuses to name the place, universalizing its decay as an emblem of *wyrd* (fate). The most charged line opens the poem with stark wonder at inexorable destruction: "Wondrous is this wall-stone, by Wyrd broken;"[^1] followed immediately by "Strongholds shattered, the work of giants crumbles."[^2] This anonymity commits the speaker to philosophical detachment: the ruins embody timeless mutability, a *memento mori* for all earthly glory, stripped of parochial history to emphasize inevitable entropy over any specific cultural loss.[^3]

Iqbal's "Saqaliya" (from *Bang-e-Dara Part 2*) explicitly names Sicily as the "grave of Hejazi civilization," lamenting its fall from Arab-Islamic splendor under Muslim rule.[^4] The most charged line is the direct apostrophe: "Ah, O Sicily!"[^5]—a visceral cry that personifies the island, blending eroticized praise ("You are the honor of the sea") with grief.[^6] Naming commits Iqbal's speaker to historical specificity and revivalist activism: Sicily evokes a recoverable Muslim golden age (e.g., echoing conquests by "desert-dwellers" whose "thunderous echoes" recall the *takbir*), urging communal mourning and renewal rather than abstract fatalism.[^7]

[^1]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 16 [*The Ruin*], [p-d0d2bd](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/16-wraetlic-is-thes-wealstan-wyrde-yebraecon-burgsted/translation/#p-d0d2bd).

[^2]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 16 [*The Ruin*], [p-d0d2bd](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/16-wraetlic-is-thes-wealstan-wyrde-yebraecon-burgsted/translation/#p-d0d2bd).

[^3]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 16 [*The Ruin*], [full passage](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/16-wraetlic-is-thes-wealstan-wyrde-yebraecon-burgsted/translation/#p-d0d2bd;p-ba51da;p-dfdbda).

[^4]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 2*, ch. 21 [*Saqaliya*], [p-08760e](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/21-saqaliya/translation/#p-08760e).

[^5]: *Bang-E-Dara Part 2*, ch. 21 [*Saqaliya*], [p-379aeb](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/21-saqaliya/translation/#p-379aeb).

[^6]: *Bang-E-Dara Part 2*, ch. 21 [*Saqaliya*], [p-379aeb](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/21-saqaliya/translation/#p-379aeb).

[^7]: *Bang-E-Dara Part 2*, ch. 21 [*Saqaliya*], [p-ac137c](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/21-saqaliya/translation/#p-ac137c); [p-26728b](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/21-saqaliya/translation/#p-26728b).

cases.prompt: Which work in the corpus contains a saint who addresses a marble pillar inside a heathen city, calling it 'better than gold' because the King of glory once inscribed the Ten Commandments on it and gave it to Moses (and 'as the truth-fast ones afterwards held it… Joshua and Tobias'), then commands it to spew a 'wide-running water for the ruin of men' upon the corrupt folk? Quote the saint's address to the stone and identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: The fusion of an animate marble pillar (figured as the original Sinai tablet kept by Joshua and Tobias) with a flood unleashed inside a city to drown its inhabitants is a signature, narratively unique passage of this work; no other Old English Christian poem in the corpus features the pillar-flood image, and the dharmaśāstra/Old Javanese/19th-century works are unrelated genres.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**The work is *Andreas* by Cynewulf, Chapter 1.**[^1]

Saint Andrew (Andreas), imprisoned in the heathen city of Mermedonia, addresses a marble pillar (described as a "marble-stone" from ancient giants' work) near the city wall. He praises it as superior to gold because the King of glory inscribed the Ten Commandments ("the righteous law... in ten words") upon it and gave it to Moses, after which the truth-fast Joshua and Tobias held it. He then commands it, by God's decree, to release a flooding river ("wide-running water for the ruin of men") to punish the corrupt cannibalistic populace.

> “Hear me now, marble-stone, by the Maker's decree,
> before whose face all of creation
> becomes afraid, when they see the Father
> of heavens and earth with the greatest of hosts
> on middle-earth seeking mankind.
> Let now from your foundations streams start to well,
> a river in-flooding, for the Almighty now,
> Heaven's High King, commands you to hasten
> and upon this corrupt folk to send forth
> a wide-running water for the ruin of men,
> a pouring ocean. Behold, you are better than gold,
> than treasure-gifts! On you the King Himself
> wrote, the God of glory, He made known in words
> the ready mysteries, and the righteous law
> He marked out in ten words,
> the Maker mighty in power. To Moses He gave it,
> as the truth-fast ones afterwards held it,
> the brave young thanes, his own kinsmen,
> the God-fearing men, Joshua and Tobias.
> Now you can know that the King of angels
> adorned you far more with gifts in old days
> than all the kindred of gems.
> Through His holy command you must quickly show
> if you have any understanding of Him.”[^1]

[^1]: Cynewulf, *Andreas*, [paragraph](/works/cynewulf-andreas-07b573/01/translation/#p-2e50c1).

cases.prompt: Which work in the corpus stages a dialogue at sea in which a saint, not yet recognizing that his helmsman is the disguised Lord of glory, praises him as a wave-rider who is 'young, not old in winters' yet steers a 'foam-necked' ship that fares 'most like a fowl' — and then says he himself has been 'sixteen times on a sea-boat' yet has never seen such a steersman? Quote the saint's praise of the helmsman and identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: A specific numerical boast (sixteen prior sea-voyages) embedded in a Christ-as-disguised-helmsman dialogue is unique to this work. The Seafarer in the Old English Elegies has a sea-voyager but no disguised-Christ dialogue, no foam-necked-fowl simile, and no sixteen-voyages count; Elene and Juliana have no sea-journey at all.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**The work is *Andreas* by Cynewulf, Chapter 1.**[^1]

This Old English poem stages a dialogue at sea between the apostle Andreas (Andrew) and the disguised Christ (the "Lord of glory"), who appears as the helmsman of a ship. Andreas praises the helmsman for his extraordinary skill in navigating the vessel through a storm, without yet recognizing his divine identity. The saint notes his own seafaring experience ("sixteen times on a sea-boat"), marvels at the ship's speed ("fares, foam-necked, most like a fowl"), and highlights the helmsman's youth ("young ... not old in winters") despite his masterful command as a "wave-rider."[^1]

[^1]: Cynewulf, *Andreas*, [paragraph](/works/cynewulf-andreas-07b573/01/translation/#p-ca9772).

cases.prompt: Which work in the corpus narrates a saint breaking into a cannibal city's prison after seven gate-guards drop dead at his approach, embracing a fellow apostle inside, and then leading out a precisely numbered company of captives — 'one hundred and two… and also forty' men, plus 'of women there yet, in addition to the host, one less than fifty'? Quote the verses giving these numbers and identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: The exact triple-count of rescued prisoners (102 + 40 men, plus 49 women) following the seven dead doorwardens, inside an apostle-rescues-apostle scene, is unique to this work. Elene's True-Cross narrative, Juliana's martyrdom, and the anonymous Old English elegies contain no such jailbreak or count; the cannibal-city setting and Mermedonian frame are exclusive to Andreas.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**The work is *Andreas* by Cynewulf (work slug: `cynewulf-andreas-07b573`), an Old English poem in a single chapter (Chapter 1).**[^1]

This matches the narrative of Saint Andrew (Andreas) journeying to the cannibalistic land of Mermedonia, where his brother-apostle Matthew is imprisoned. Andrew approaches the prison; **seven guards die suddenly** ("before the barred doors guards were standing, / seven together. Sudden death took them all, / they fell, fame-less"). He enters, **embraces Matthew** ("Each one embraced the other with his arms, / they kissed and they clasped"), and **leads out the captives**: **"one hundred and two, counted in number, / and also forty, saved from the strife— / he left not one of them under the city-lock / fastened in fetters—and of women there yet, / in addition to the host, one less than fifty / he freed, the fearful."**[^2]

[^1]: Cynewulf, *Andreas*, [Chapter 1](/works/cynewulf-andreas-07b573/01/translation/).

[^2]: Cynewulf, *Andreas*, [paragraph](/works/cynewulf-andreas-07b573/01/translation/#p-74e29a).

cases.prompt: Which work in the corpus rules that if a Brahmin's top-knot is cut by enemies five times, his Brahminhood is irrecoverably lost — explicitly stating that even a fresh saṃskāra cannot restore it — while in earlier passages it had laid out that for a single cutting one wears hair from a cow's tail on the ear and performs a Prājāpatya penance, and for cutting in the middle one is again purified by a Prājāpatya? Quote the verses on the fivefold cutting and the cow's-tail remedy verbatim, and identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: The exact threshold of five enemy-cuttings as the irreversible loss of Brahminhood, combined with the specific intermediate remedies (cow's-tail hair on the ear plus Prājāpatya for a single cutting; Prājāpatya for a midway cutting), is unique to this short prayaścitta smṛti; Manu, Yājñavalkya, Parāśara, Yama, Viṣṇu and the other corpus smṛtis do not enumerate this graded top-knot rule with the five-strikes-and-out clause.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Āṅgirasa Smṛti, Chapter 1**

The *Āṅgirasa Smṛti* sets out escalating penalties for a Brahmin whose top-knot (*śikhā*) is damaged or severed, treating it as essential to Brahminhood alongside the sacred thread.[^1] For a single cutting by an enemy, "he should immediately wear hair from a cow's tail on his ear and, being pure, should perform a Prājāpatya penance."[^2] If "the top-knot is cut in the middle, he is purified by a Prājāpatya penance."[^3] However, "If the top-knot is cut by enemies five times, his Brahminhood is lost, even with a new *saṃskāra*."[^4]

[^1]: /works/unknown-angirasa-smrti-2bf9d1/01/translation/#p-54d41e
[^2]: /works/unknown-angirasa-smrti-2bf9d1/01/translation/#p-94f7a6
[^3]: /works/unknown-angirasa-smrti-2bf9d1/01/translation/#p-6dc072
[^4]: /works/unknown-angirasa-smrti-2bf9d1/01/translation/#p-f210ca  
(Note: *Ang_1.65* spans these paragraphs for full context.)

cases.prompt: Where in the corpus does a Sanskrit penitential text declare that one who eats in the same row, or together with, a woman who has been married multiple times must be buried covered in a pit dug in the earth for either one watch (three hours) or two ghaṭikās (forty-eight minutes), then perform the birth-rites and other saṃskāras anew, perform a thousand Taptakṛcchra penances, subsist on barley while performing the Cāpāgrayāna rite, and bathe five thousand times — either himself or through a Brahmin — to be purified for himself alone, the rule attributed to Bhṛgu in ancient times? Quote the passage verbatim and identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: The specific compound penance — burial in an earthen pit for the precise alternative durations of one watch / two ghaṭikās, followed by exactly a thousand Taptakṛcchra penances and five thousand baths (with the option of a Brahmin proxy), attributed to Bhṛgu, for the offense of merely eating in the same row with a multiply-married woman — is uniquely this smṛti's formulation; the other dharmaśāstras and digests in the corpus (Manu, Yājñavalkya, Nārada, Parāśara, Yama, Viṣṇu, Bṛhaspati, Kātyāyana, Vīramitrodaya) do not pair these specific numbers with this specific transgression.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Āṅgirasa Smṛti, Chapter 1 (verses Āṅg_1.219–223)**

This passage occurs in a penitential section discussing expiations for grave ritual faults, immediately after rules on remarriage and the status of remarried women (a woman married "one, two, three, four, or five times," i.e., previously married multiple times). Eating "in the same row" (*panktyā*) or "together" (*saha*) with such a woman during rites causes loss of caste.[^1]

> A *veśyā* (prostitute/courtesan), though fit only for pleasure, is superior to her.[^2] If one eats in the same row with her during these rites, or eats together with her, it is a wicked act that causes loss of caste.[^3] For the purification of that sin, one should, according to rule, be covered in a pit in the earth, having dug it for a period of one watch (three hours) or two *ghaṭikās* (forty-eight minutes).[^4] // Ang_1.220 // After being lifted out of it, one should then perform the birth rites (*jātakarma*) and others anew. One should also perform a thousand Taptakṛcchra penances according to rule.[^5] // Ang_1.221 // With a controlled self, subsisting on barley, he should perform the Cāpāgrayāna rite; then he becomes pure. He should perform five thousand baths, either himself or through a Brahmin.[^6] // Ang_1.222 // Then he becomes pure for himself alone. He is not fit for others. Thus spoke Bhṛgu in ancient times.[^7] // Ang_1.223 //

cases.prompt: Which work in the corpus narrates that the lotus-born creator placed all the divine śrāddha-vegetables on one scale-pan and the jackfruit (panasa) on the other and weighed them with his hand, finding the jackfruit slightly heavier — and then specifies, on that same balance, that the eggplant (bṛhatī) was equal to three hundred, ginger (ārdraka) to six hundred, and sesame (tila) to one hundred, establishing this trio from that day forward as equal to a thousand vegetables for the satisfaction of the ancestors? Quote the weighing and the three numerical equivalences verbatim and identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: The mythic weighing-scene with Brahmā's hand-balance, the panasa tipping the scale, and the precise pan-equivalences of eggplant 300 / ginger 600 / sesame 100 making up a 'thousand vegetables' trio for śrāddha is a signature passage unique to this smṛti; no other corpus work — neither the other smṛtis (Manu, Yājñavalkya, Parāśara, Yama, Viṣṇu, Nārada, Bṛhaspati, Kātyāyana), the Vīramitrodaya digest, nor any Old Javanese or non-Indic text — contains this specific cosmogonic-scale episode with these numerical weights.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**This specific narrative—describing a lotus-born creator weighing divine śrāddha-vegetables against a jackfruit (panasa), finding the jackfruit slightly heavier, and then establishing numerical equivalences for eggplant (bṛhatī) as three hundred, ginger (ārdraka) as six hundred, and sesame (tila) as one hundred (together equaling a thousand vegetables for ancestral satisfaction)—does not appear in the Falsafa corpus.**

Extensive searches using distinctive phrases and terms from the query (e.g., "jackfruit", "panasa", "lotus-born", "scale-pan", "śrāddha-vegetables", "eggplant ginger sesame", "three hundred" with vegetables/ancestors, etc.) across the English translations returned zero results. The corpus's 37 works, including relevant Smṛti texts like *Manusmṛti*, *Parāśara Smṛti*, *Viṣṇu Smṛti*, *Kātyāyana Smṛti*, *Yama Smṛti*, *Aṅgirasa Smṛti*, and *Bṛhaspati Smṛti* (which cover dharmaśāstra topics such as rituals and ancestral offerings), do not contain this passage or matching content. Not every topic from classical Indic literature is covered in the current corpus.

cases.prompt: Which Sanskrit dharmaśāstra, in its enumeration of the twelve kinds of witnesses, describes a category in which a man is hidden behind a wall and made to overhear a statement about a debt, so that — when the debt is later denied — he can come forward and report what he heard? Quote the verse that defines this concealed witness, and name the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: The 'concealed by a wall, made to hear a statement about a debt' formulation of the gūḍha-sākṣin appears verbatim in Bṛhaspati's twelve-witness scheme; Manu, Yājñavalkya, Nārada, Kātyāyana etc. either lack this category entirely or do not phrase it with the wall-eavesdropper image.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Bṛhaspati Smṛti, Chapter 1**

In its section on witnesses (1.5), the *Bṛhaspati Smṛti* enumerates twelve kinds: a subscribed witness, a recorded witness, **a concealed witness**, a reminded witness, a kinsman, a messenger, a chance witness, a secondary witness, one present at the transaction, a king, a chief judge, and the village.[^1]

**The concealed witness (*gūḍhaḥ sakṣī*)** is defined as follows:

> He who, concealed by a wall, is made to hear a statement about a debt, and who, when it is denied, relates it as it happened, is called a concealed witness.[^2]

[^1]: Bṛhaspati Smṛti, Chapter 1, [paragraph](/works/unknown-brhaspati-smrti-0fd070/01/translation/#p-0cc2ab).

[^2]: Bṛhaspati Smṛti, Chapter 1, [paragraph](/works/unknown-brhaspati-smrti-0fd070/01/translation/#p-2ee353).

cases.prompt: Which Sanskrit legal treatise warns against forged copies of written instruments by comparing them to a reflection in a mirror — saying that just as a reflection appears real though it is unreal, so skilled persons make copies of documents? Quote the simile and identify the work and chapter section on documents.
cases.rationale: The mirror-reflection simile for document forgery (Brh_1,6.33) is a distinctive Bṛhaspati image embedded in his elaborate treatment of lekhya, which goes well beyond the document-rules of Manu, Yājñavalkya or Nārada; no other smṛti in the corpus uses this metaphor.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Bṛhaspati Smṛti (Chapter 1, section 1.6 "The Document")** warns against forged copies of documents using this simile: "Just as a reflection in a mirror, though unreal, appears as real, so skilled persons make copies of documents."[^1]

The nearly identical warning also appears in **Kātyāyana Smṛti (Chapter 26 "The Examination of Documents")**: "Just as an image in a mirror, though unreal, appears real, so do skillful people create copies of documents."[^2]

Both are classical Sanskrit legal treatises (Smṛtis) on judicial procedure, addressing document forgery in their respective sections on written instruments (e.g., deeds of debt, sale, partition).

[^1]: Bṛhaspati Smṛti, *Chapter 1*, [paragraph](/works/unknown-brhaspati-smrti-0fd070/01/translation/#p-c67c7e).

[^2]: Kātyāyana Smṛti, *Chapter 26*, [paragraph](/works/unknown-katyayana-smrti-1e06d2/26/translation/#p-ffea01).

cases.prompt: Which ancient Indian work on judicial procedure anatomizes the court of justice as a 'ten-limbed instrument' in which the king is the head, the appointed judicial officer is the mouth, the assessors are the arms, the Smṛti is the hands, and the accountant and the scribe together form the shanks? Quote the verse listing these limbs and name the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: The ten-limbed body-of-the-court metaphor — with the accountant and scribe explicitly assigned to the shanks, alongside the king-as-head and Smṛti-as-hands — is unique to Bṛhaspati's section on the court; no other corpus smṛti (Manu, Yājñavalkya, Nārada, Kātyāyana, Parāśara, Yama, Viṣṇu, Aṅgirasa) anatomizes the court this way.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**The Bṛhaspati Smṛti (Chapter 1)** anatomizes the court of justice (*dharmādhikaraṇa*) as a "ten-limbed instrument" (*daśāṅga*), presided over by the king.[^1][^2]

The verse listing the specific limbs mentioned—king as head, appointed judicial officer (*prāḍvivāka*) as mouth, assessors (*sabhāsthāyinaḥ*) as arms, Smṛti as hands, and accountant (*lekhaka*) and scribe (*kāyastha*) together as shanks—is as follows:

> Of these limbs, the king is the head, and the appointed officer is called the mouth;  
> the assessors are the arms, the Smṛti the hands, and the accountant and scribe the shanks. // Brh_1,1.84 //

The remaining limbs (eyes: gold, fire, water; heart: king's conscience; feet: his own officer) follow immediately in the next verse.[^3]

[^1]: Bṛhaspati Smṛti, Chapter 1, [paragraph](/works/unknown-brhaspati-smrti-0fd070/01/translation/#p-a30c28).

[^2]: Bṛhaspati Smṛti, Chapter 1, [paragraph](/works/unknown-brhaspati-smrti-0fd070/01/translation/#p-508073).

[^3]: Bṛhaspati Smṛti, Chapter 1, [paragraph](/works/unknown-brhaspati-smrti-0fd070/01/translation/#p-db3b97).

cases.prompt: Where in the corpus does an author ridicule the jurists' theory that property arose from a universal contract among all humans by asking how one would demonstrate to an inhabitant of Peru that he could not pick the fruit at his hand without infringing the rights of an inhabitant of Siberia, or make an inhabitant of Kamchatka understand that he could not clothe himself in an animal's skin without injuring the rights of the Arabs, or persuade the Gauls that they could not in conscience cultivate the plains of Auvergne without first obtaining permission from the inhabitants of Tibet? Quote the cascade of geographic examples and identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: The Peru/Siberia, Kamchatka/Arabs, Gauls/Auvergne/Tibet triplet is a verbatim-quotable rhetorical cascade unique to this chapter's refutation of the universal-convention theory of property; no other corpus work — including the Législation volumes or Property Vol II — uses this exact geographic gag.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**In Charles Comte's *Traité de la propriété: VOL I* (1834), Chapter 4 ("De l'occupation des choses" / "On the occupation of things"), the author ridicules jurists' theory of a prior universal right over all things (leading to a supposed global contract of renunciation), which he calls a "chimera."**[^1]

He argues that such a right would require implausibly convening all humanity for permission to use local resources, then provides this cascade of geographic examples:

> By what reasoning would one have succeeded in demonstrating to **an inhabitant of Peru that he could not pick the fruit that grew at his hand, without infringing upon the rights of the inhabitants of Siberia**? How would one have made **an inhabitant of Kamchatka understand that he could not clothe himself in the skin of an animal, without injuring the rights of the Arabs**? How would one have gone about showing **the Gauls that they could not, in conscience, bring the plains of Auvergne under cultivation, without having obtained the permission of the inhabitants of Tibet**?[^1]

Comte deems the supposed contract equally absurd, as it is "impossible" and "unintelligible," needing constant renewal for new generations, and contrary to evident human needs and progress.[^1]

[^1]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété: VOL I*, [paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/04-de-loccupation-des-choses/translation/#p-5f2b15).

cases.prompt: Which work in the corpus argues that the value of land rises in step with civilization by tabulating, from Humboldt's 1803 report, the population per square league across the intendancies of the former kingdom of Mexico in the precise progression '6, 7, 10, 12, 38, 65, 66, 81, 109, 120, 255, 301, 586' — concluding that an extent of land worth six francs in Sonora would be worth two hundred and fifty-five in Mexico and five hundred and eighty-six in Guanaxuato? Quote the line that gives the numerical sequence and identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: The exact 13-term numerical progression drawn from Humboldt's 1803 census of Mexican intendancies, with Sonora and Guanaxuato as endpoints, is a verbatim and unique factoid; no other corpus work treats land-value via Mexican intendancy demographics.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Charles Comte's *Traité de la propriété* (Vol. I), Chapter 9 ("On the primitive utility and value of lands"), argues that the value of land rises in step with civilization by tabulating population densities per square league of land across the intendancies of the former kingdom of Mexico, drawing from Alexander von Humboldt's 1803 report (*Political Essay on New Spain*, vol. 2, bk. 3, ch. 8).**[^1]

The key line giving the numerical sequence is: "we will find that the value of lands increases, from one intendancy to another, in the following progression: **6, 7, 10, 12, 38, 65, 66, 81, 109, 120, 255, 301, 586**."[^2] Comte then concludes: "The extent of land that would be worth only six francs in the former intendancy of Sonora would be worth two hundred and fifty-five in that of Mexico, and five hundred and eighty-six in that of Guanaxuato."[^2]

[^1]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété: VOL I*, [Chapter 9](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/09-de-lutilite-et-de-la-valeur-primitives-des-fonds-d/translation/).

[^2]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété: VOL I*, [paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/09-de-lutilite-et-de-la-valeur-primitives-des-fonds-d/translation/#p-0fd6d5).

cases.prompt: Where in the corpus is Arthur Young's tour of France during 1787, 1788, and 1789 cited as eyewitness testimony to the destruction of the Pyrenees forests — describing men near Bagnères de Luchon cutting and splitting young birches to make barrel hoops, and lamenting that the 'beautiful and noble forest of Lartigues' had been so devastated that within ten or twelve years it would be 'nothing more than a bare mountain, with some miserable shrubs browsed by goats'? Quote the Lartigues passage and identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: The named forest 'Lartigues', the locality 'Bagnères de Luchon', the detail of birch-splitting for barrel hoops, and the Arthur Young 1787-89 dating together pin this passage to a single chapter on mountain deforestation; no other corpus work cites Young on the Pyrenees.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Charles Comte's *Traité de la propriété: VOL I*, Chapter 13 ("Influence du déboisement des montagnes sur les fleuves et les rivières.")**[^1]

Comte cites Arthur Young's *Travels in France* (1787–1789) as eyewitness testimony to forest destruction in the Pyrenees, including men near Bagnères de Luchon "cutting and splitting young birches, to make barrel hoops."[^2] The Lartigues passage, quoted below verbatim, laments the devastation:

> This beautiful and noble forest of Lartigues has experienced a devastation so general, that it is almost entirely destroyed; there are no young shoots to replace the trees that have disappeared, and, in ten or twelve years, it will be nothing more than a bare mountain, with some miserable shrubs browsed by goats or by other animals.[^3]

[^1]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété: VOL I*, [Chapter 13](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/13-influence-du-deboisement-des-montagnes-sur-les-fle/translation/).

[^2]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété: VOL I*, [paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/13-influence-du-deboisement-des-montagnes-sur-les-fle/translation/#p-4ce3c5).

[^3]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété: VOL I*, [paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/13-influence-du-deboisement-des-montagnes-sur-les-fle/translation/#p-5e30bb). (Quoting Arthur Young, *Travels during the years 1787, 1788 and 1789...*, vol. 2, p. 106.)

cases.prompt: Where in the corpus does an author ridicule the doctrine that every invention is the natural property of its inventor by tracing it to absurd conclusions — that the first man to turn wood into clogs or animal skin into sandals would acquire 'the exclusive right to shoe the human race', so that an inhabitant of the austral pole could not wear clogs without buying them from an inventor at the boreal pole, and that the first cultivator of wheat would have left the rest of mankind reduced to eating snakes, rats, or frogs, or even one another, to remain faithful to the rights of nature? Quote the relevant lines and identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: The clogs/sandals reductio with the boreal-pole/austral-pole geography and the snakes-rats-frogs trio is a distinctive verbatim image attacking the 1790 Constituent Assembly's patent-as-natural-right doctrine; it is specific to this work's treatment of industrial-invention property.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
Charles Comte ridicules the doctrine that every invention is the natural property of its inventor in **Traité de la propriété: VOL II**, Chapter 29 ("De la propriété des inventions ou des procédés industriels.").

He writes:

> Here are truths evident by themselves, engraved in all minds and in all hearts, and which can be contested only by men who have closed their eyes to the light. Let us follow them in their application, and we will be even more vividly struck by them. The first man who conceived and executed the idea of transforming a piece of wood into a pair of clogs, or an animal skin into a pair of sandals, acquired by this sole fact the exclusive right to shoe the human race. From that moment, all men found themselves under the obligation to walk barefoot, or to go and provide themselves with footwear from the fortunate inventor. If the discovery was made by an inhabitant of the boreal pole, the inhabitants of the austral pole could not, without injuring the rights of man and without violating the principles engraved in all hearts, permit themselves to wear clogs without having bought them from the other extremity of the globe. If the inventor could not manufacture a sufficient quantity to shoe all the nations of the world, or if he put a price on them that one did not have the possibility of paying, one had to go without footwear and skin their feet, for fear of injuring the rights of nature. All this is clear as day, incontestable as the light for some of our doctors.[^1]

> It is no less evident to their eyes that the first man who, discovering a grain of wheat, took it into his head to deposit it in the bosom of the earth, to make it multiply, and to make bread, acquired the exclusive right to feed himself and to feed the human race with this new kind of food. From that moment, the peoples of all races, white, black, yellow, red, and swarthy, had to cross the seas and the mountains to go and provide themselves with bread from the inventor. Those who could not make the journey were obliged to continue eating snakes, rats, or frogs, or even to eat one another, to remain faithful to the rights of nature.[^2]

[^1]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété: VOL II*, [p-a18ebe](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-ii-6beea5/29-de-la-propriete-des-inventions-ou-des-procedes-ind/translation/#p-a18ebe).

[^2]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété: VOL II*, [p-8cebda](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-ii-6beea5/29-de-la-propriete-des-inventions-ou-des-procedes-ind/translation/#p-8cebda).

args.query: "clogs" OR "sandals" "wheat" OR "snakes" OR "frogs" OR "rights of nature"

cases.prompt: In which work is the modern public debt described as a mechanism by which 'the most intelligent, the most active, the most industrious nation could be transformed into a people of helots, working for a few thousand of the idle' — with the Restoration government's two successive billion-franc loans (one to pay foreigners who had reinstated it, the second to indemnify the émigrés) given as concrete examples of how borrowed capital permanently transfers a nation's revenues to foreign capitalists? Quote the helots line and the passage on the Restoration's two billions, and name the chapter.
cases.rationale: The 'people of helots' formulation paired with the specific Restoration-era two-billion-franc loans (foreign reinstatement + émigrés) is unique to this volume's chapter on annuities and state rentes; no other corpus work combines this verbatim image with that historical detail.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
This passage appears in Charles Comte's *Traité de la propriété: VOL II*, Chapter 37 ("De la propriété des rentes sur des particuliers ou sur l'État.").

The "helots" line is: "One can conceive that by pushing such a system to excess, **the most intelligent, the most active, the most industrious nation could be transformed into a people of helots, working for a few thousand of the idle** who would buy the products of its lands, of its industry, and of all its labors, from a government that would have itself paid the value for them, and that would share it among its favorites or its satellites: only a good national representation can shelter a people from such a spoliation."[^1]

The passage on the Restoration's loans reads: "If, for example, when **the government of the Restoration borrowed a billion to deliver it to the foreigners who had established it**, this billion was advanced by lenders from other nations, these lenders really acquired a billion worth of French properties. **If, when a little later, it created a debt of a second billion to deliver it to the émigrés**, this billion had been advanced by foreign capitalists, these capitalists would have again acquired the right to perceive in perpetuity from the products of our industry the interest on the capital lent."[^2]

[^1]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété: VOL II*, [paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-ii-6beea5/37-de-la-propriete-des-rentes-sur-des-particuliers-ou/translation/#p-c462b5).

[^2]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété: VOL II*, [paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-ii-6beea5/37-de-la-propriete-des-rentes-sur-des-particuliers-ou/translation/#p-9df833).

cases.prompt: Where in the corpus does an author attack the practice of letting books fall into the public domain after a few years' enjoyment by asking why the State, when it needs land for a canal, a great road, or a fortified place, pays the owner an indemnity, but treats writers' heirs as legitimate prey — closing with the rhetorical question whether 'the labors of the writers who have enlightened the world, of Descartes, of Bacon, of Franklin', should be less worthy of protection and respect than the labors of a candlemaker? cases.rationale: The named triad Descartes/Bacon/Franklin set against a candlemaker, in the context of a canal/road/fortress eminent-domain analogy applied to literary property, is verbatim-locatable and tied specifically to this volume's chapter on the deprivation of guarantees for literary compositions.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
This passage appears in Charles Comte's *Traité de la propriété: VOL II*, Chapter 32 ("Des causes qui ont privé les compositions littéraires des garanties accordées aux autres propriétés").

> Would the labors of the writers who have enlightened the world, of Descartes, of Bacon, of Franklin, be less worthy of protection and respect than the labors of a candlemaker?[^1]

[^1]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété: VOL II*, [paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-ii-6beea5/32-des-causes-qui-ont-prive-les-compositions-litterai/translation/#p-e01757).

cases.prompt: Where in the corpus is it argued that distinguishing 'arbitrary' from 'legal' rule is largely illusory — because a master can write a perfectly orderly code for governing his slaves and herds, and orderly extortion is still extortion? Quote the passage that makes the analogy and identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: Tests whether the model can find a distinctive deflationary argument that legal regularity is not the same as liberty; the slaves-and-herds analogy is unusual and verbatim-quotable.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
This argument appears in Charles Comte's *Traité de Législation: VOL I*, Chapter 13 ("Distinction entre un régime arbitraire et un régime légal. De ce qui constitue la différence.").

The key passage draws the requested analogy between a master's orderly code for his herds and slaves, a "legal regime" that nonetheless fails to confer liberty (and thus renders the arbitrary/legal distinction largely illusory), while emphasizing that the true issue is subjection to *good* laws:

> A proprietor can put regularity into the management of a farm; he can trace for each of his agents the rules they must follow in the administration of his herds; he can determine the hours at which they will be led to pasture, the times at which they will be shorn, the time at which it will be permitted to mate them, and even the age at which they will be delivered to the butcher. If he has slaves, he can make, for them, regulations analogous to those he will have made for his herds; he can determine the hours of work they will owe per day, the quantity of food that will be left to them, the number of lashes of the whip that will be given to them in determined circumstances; he can make, in a word, a regulation as well written and as provident as the most admired code. When everything has been thus regulated, the beasts and the men will be subject to a legal regime; that is to say, the action of the power to which they will be subject will have been described in advance; but must we conclude that they are much better off for it? Will they, for that reason, have a greater sum of liberty? If, to be free and to be well, it were enough to be subject only to laws whose dispositions were described, it would not be worth the trouble to argue: the least accommodating governments could consent to it, without losing anything of their power.[^1]

The chapter reinforces this with the closely related claim that regularity merely produces "more uniformity in the extortions," even under a "legal regime."[^2]

[^1]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL I*, [paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/13-distinction-entre-un-regime-arbitraire-et-un-regim/translation/#p-9f5b65).

[^2]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL I*, [paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/13-distinction-entre-un-regime-arbitraire-et-un-regim/translation/#p-5bc401).

cases.prompt: Which work compares a government that legislates without published rules to a steam engine missing its regulator, and warns that adding a regulator does not change the nature of the machine — only its uniformity? Quote the passage and name the chapter.
cases.rationale: Tests retrieval of a striking industrial-age metaphor for written law. The image is specific enough that paraphrase would not satisfy.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Charles Comte's *Traité de Législation: VOL I*, Chapter 13 ("Distinction entre un régime arbitraire et un régime légal. De ce qui constitue la différence.").**[^1]

> The government that acts without having described in advance the diverse kinds of action it tends to exercise could be compared to a steam engine that lacked a regulator; its movements are irregular, by turns slow or abrupt. The government that, on the contrary, acts only after having described the actions it wishes to produce, moves in an equal manner: the description it publishes is, in a way, the regulator that gives uniformity to its movements. But one must be very careful not to believe that the addition of the regulator to the machine of government changes its nature or its effects; if it is set up to draw the people's subsistence toward the men of power, the more regular it is in its movements, the better it will fulfill its office; it will be more durable and more energetic for it.[^1]

[^1]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL I*, [paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/13-distinction-entre-un-regime-arbitraire-et-un-regim/translation/#p-5bc401).

args.query: "machine" regulator OR governor government OR legislation OR legislate OR law

cases.prompt: In the corpus, where is Chinese infanticide used as a worked example to show that even when a government criminalizes the exposure of children, the actual benefit reaches almost no one — the author estimating that the good 'would perhaps not be felt by one single individual out of two hundred thousand'? Quote the line that gives that ratio and identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: The numerical claim is unique and verbatim-quotable; the topic is treated here through a utilitarian-legislation lens distinct from how parental duty appears in any other corpus work.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL I*, Chapter 19 ("Des effets particuliers à chacun des principaux éléments de force dont une loi se compose ; et de l’").**[^1]

In this chapter, Comte analyzes the limited utility of government intervention by contrasting European laws that criminalize infanticide and child exposure (requiring parents to raise their children under penalty) with the situation in China, where no such laws exist and parents freely expose children (estimated at ~18,000 annually across 353 million people). He argues that *even if* China adopted similar criminalization, the net benefit would be negligible due to poverty, high natural infant mortality, and enforcement challenges—ultimately affecting at most a few hundred children, whose lives would likely involve more suffering than joy.[^2]

The precise line estimating the ratio is:  
> This good would perhaps not be felt by **one single individual out of two hundred thousand**, and it would be reduced to almost nothing.[^3]

[^1]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL I*, Ch. 19, [translation](https://falsafa.corpus/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/19-des-effets-particuliers-a-chacun-des-principaux-el/translation/).

[^2]: Ibid., [¶ p-ed3094](https://falsafa.corpus/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/19-des-effets-particuliers-a-chacun-des-principaux-el/translation/#p-ed3094) to [p-66a263](https://falsafa.corpus/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/19-des-effets-particuliers-a-chacun-des-principaux-el/translation/#p-66a263) (discussing China); [p-849c8b](https://falsafa.corpus/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/19-des-effets-particuliers-a-chacun-des-principaux-el/translation/#p-849c8b) (hypothesizing government action).

[^3]: Ibid., [¶ p-0cb109](https://falsafa.corpus/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/19-des-effets-particuliers-a-chacun-des-principaux-el/translation/#p-0cb109).

cases.prompt: Which work argues that a civilized people, to be happy, needs only 'not to be pillaged, and to be abandoned to itself' — and that governments are far more powerful at doing harm than at doing good? Quote the line and give chapter attribution.
cases.rationale: Distinctive minimal-state formulation; the exact phrase is verbatim and the framing is specific to this passage.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Charles Comte's *Traité de Législation: VOL I* (Chapter 19)** argues precisely this.[^1]

There, Comte writes: "perhaps one would arrive at this consequence, **that a people already civilized needs, to be happy, only not to be pillaged, and to be abandoned to itself**." He immediately continues by explaining that such a people "would do better by the sole force of its customs... than our learned politicians can do."[^2]

A few paragraphs earlier, he observes that "governments, **so weak when they wish to do good, sometimes possess an immense influence for doing evil**," after analyzing how governmental interventions often produce more harm than natural customs or instincts alone.[^3]

cases.prompt: Where in the corpus does an author argue that Stoic contempt for pain and pleasure was not a metaphysical doctrine but a sociological response — the predictable maxims of men subject to proscriptions, confiscation, and slavery, such that Epictetus's lessons fit a colonial slave as well as a subject of Nero? Quote the line that draws the slave-of-our-modern-colonists comparison, and name the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: Tests retrieval of an unusual historical-materialist reading of Stoicism; the colonial-slave comparison is specific to this passage and not present in other Stoic-related corpus material.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Charles Comte argues this in *Traité de Législation: VOL I*, Chapter 15 ("Of physical pains and pleasures considered as elements of the power of laws. — Of the judgments that have been passed on pleasures and pains of this kind, by religious sects and by philosophical sects").**[^1]

Comte contends that the Stoics' "contempt for physical pleasures and pains" was no abstract metaphysical doctrine but a practical response to the brutal sociological realities of ancient Rome and Greece: constant threats of proscription, confiscation of property, exile, enslavement of families, and death under tyrants like Marius, Sulla, the triumvirs, Tiberius, and Nero.[^2] These conditions made all evils "plausible," demanding maxims of resignation and preparation for inevitable calamity—doctrines suited to "enslaved men" that persisted across "savages," slaves, and subjects of despotism.[^3]

The specific line drawing the comparison is: "The maxims of Epictetus would suit **a slave of our modern colonists** no less than a subject of Nero."[^4]

[^1]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL I*, [/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/15-des-peines-et-des-plaisirs-physiques-consideres-co/translation/](https://falsafa.corpus/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/15-des-peines-et-des-plaisirs-physiques-consideres-co/translation/).

cases.prompt: Which work in the corpus closes its first volume by quoting the inscription over the gate of Dante's hell — 'PER ME SI VA NELLA CITTA DOLENTE...LASCIATE OGNI SPERANZA' — to mark the entrance to the career of vices and crimes? Identify the work and chapter, and quote enough of the inscription to confirm the citation.
cases.rationale: Distinctive closing flourish: a treatise on legislation ending with Dante is unusual and easy to verify verbatim.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Charles Comte's *Traité de Législation: Volume I* (work slug: `charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2`) closes its first volume at the end of Chapter 21 ("De l’action des lois de la morale, et des obstacles que cette action rencontre quelquefois dans cell" / "Of the action of the laws of morality, and of the obstacles that this action sometimes encounters in that of governments, in public institutions, or in popular errors").**[^1]

The chapter concludes by contrasting two life paths—one of virtues, the other of "vices and crimes"—and proposes placing Dante's inscription from the gate of Hell at the entrance to the latter career as a warning: 

> PER ME SI VA NELLA CITTA DOLENTE :  
> PER ME SI VA NELL’ ETERNO DOLORE :  
> PER ME SI VA TRA LA PERDUTA GENTE.  
> GIUSTIZIA MOSSE ‘L MIO ALTO FATTORE.  
> ……………………………………………….  
> LASCIATE OGNI SPERANZA, VOI CH’ ENTRATE.[^2]

This is immediately followed by "#### END OF THE FIRST VOLUME."

[^1]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL I*, Chapter 21, [/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/21-de-l-action-des-lois-de-la-morale-et-des-obstacles/translation/#p-dbf447](https://falsafa.corpus/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/21-de-l-action-des-lois-de-la-morale-et-des-obstacles/translation/#p-dbf447).

cases.prompt: Which work argues that charitable institutions — foundling hospitals, lying-in houses for unwed mothers, asylums for 'repentant' prostitutes — actually encourage the vices they aim to remedy, because they reduce the natural penalties that would have repressed them while leaving the pleasures intact? Quote the passage that compares such institutions to a society that, out of humanity, would break down the doors of prisons.
cases.rationale: Distinctive reactionary-utilitarian critique of philanthropy; the prison-doors analogy is verbatim-quotable and unlikely to appear elsewhere in the corpus.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Charles Comte's *Traité de Législation*, Volume I (Chapter 21),** argues precisely this point.[^1]

Comte contends that natural pains inflicted on those indulging in vices—physical sufferings from misery and disease, moral pains like contempt and abandonment—serve as society's primary check against such behaviors, since the pleasures remain immediate while the penalties are distant.[^2] Charitable institutions undermine this balance by alleviating these penalties for the vicious without touching the pleasures, thereby encouraging vice. He illustrates with prostitution: foundling hospitals (or lying-in houses) relieve mothers of childbirth costs and cares; orphanages support illegitimate children; venereal disease hospitals treat related illnesses; and asylums for "repentant" prostitutes offer shelter, sustenance, and social reintegration, shielding them from contempt.[^3]

The key passage likening these to breaking prison doors reads:

> Now, any act by which an individual, a society, or a government diminishes the publicity, intensity, duration, or certainty of any of these pains, is an attack on good customs. Such an act has the effect of weakening the only guarantee that each of us possesses against the vices of others; **it acts, with regard to vicious habits, as would act, with regard to the actions that authority represses, the existence of a society that, out of humanity, made it a duty to go and break down the doors of prisons**.[^4]

[^1]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL I*, [Chapter 21](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/21-de-l-action-des-lois-de-la-morale-et-des-obstacles/translation/).

[^2]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL I*, [p-b31bb4](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/21-de-l-action-des-lois-de-la-morale-et-des-obstacles/translation/#p-b31bb4).

[^3]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL I*, [paragraphs p-cd2fdf to p-273291](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/21-de-l-action-des-lois-de-la-morale-et-des-obstacles/translation/#p-cd2fdf).

[^4]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL I*, [p-b31bb4](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/21-de-l-action-des-lois-de-la-morale-et-des-obstacles/translation/#p-b31bb4).

Say in political economy, treating laws and institutions as facts to be observed rather than systems to be invented? Quote the passage that names Smith and Say in this connection, and identify the work and chapter.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:

Extensive searches across the English translations for phrases like "Adam Smith," "J.-B. Say," "Jean-Baptiste Say," "political economy" (with or without Smith/Say), "legislative science," "moral science," and key conceptual terms like "laws" "institutions" "observed" "invented" returned zero results. The corpus contains relevant works on legislation and political theory, such as Charles Comte's *Traité de Législation* (Vols. I–IV) and *Traité de la Propriété* (Vols. I–II), and Charles Dunoyer's *Nouveau traité d'économie* (Vols. I–II), which discuss laws, institutions, morality, and economy from a liberal perspective.[^1] However, no passages name Smith or Say in this connection, nor match the described methodological claim.

cases.prompt: Where in the corpus is a famous philosopher-jurist's climate theory discredited by pointing out that he derived it from an experiment performed on half a sheep's tongue — alternately heating it and freezing it and inspecting the fibers under a magnifying glass — and where the author concludes that 'a moral and political system that rested on such experiments would seem to me to rest on a scarcely solid base'? Quote the passage that describes the half-tongue experiment, and identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: The half-a-sheep's-tongue experiment as the empirical basis of climate theory is a uniquely vivid factoid; the authorial sneer at it is verbatim-quotable and not present in any other corpus work, including Comte's other volumes which do not treat Montesquieu's climate system at this length.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**This passage appears in Charles Comte's *Traité de Législation*, Volume II (1837), Chapter 6 ("Of the difficulties presented by the question of the influence of climates on human faculties"). Here, Comte criticizes Montesquieu's climate theory from *The Spirit of the Laws* (Book XIV).**[^1]

Comte recounts Montesquieu's infamous physiological "experiment" — which Montesquieu describes in *The Spirit of the Laws*, Book XIV, Chapter 2 — as follows:

> The philosopher-jurist took **half a sheep’s tongue**; he subjected it alternately to a **hot temperature**, and to a **cold temperature down to freezing**; he examined, **with the aid of a magnifying glass, the effects** produced by the cold and by the heat on this **half-tongue**, and these effects served him to determine the influence that cold and heat exercise on the physical and moral nature of man, in all parts of our globe.[^2]

Comte then discredits the theory it supposedly supports:

> Although little versed in physiology, I have difficulty persuading myself that these facts can be ascertained by experiments done on **half a sheep’s tongue**; and even if they were ascertained by such experiments, I cannot see any link between them and the moral consequences that Montesquieu deduces from them. **I confess that a moral and political system that rested on such experiments would seem to me to rest on a scarcely solid base**.[^3]

Comte's point is that such a flimsy basis cannot support sweeping claims about how climate supposedly determines human courage, vices, virtues, laws, and servitude across entire continents.

[^1]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL II*, [Chapter 6](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-ii-20331d/06-des-difficultes-que-presente-la-question-de-l-infl/translation/).

[^2]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL II*, [paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-ii-20331d/06-des-difficultes-que-presente-la-question-de-l-infl/translation/#p-4a7c37).

[^3]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL II*, [paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-ii-20331d/06-des-difficultes-que-presente-la-question-de-l-infl/translation/#p-f2ee64).

cases.prompt: Which work in the corpus argues that European colonization in America, southern Asia, the islands of the great Ocean, and southern and western Africa has produced a wholly new kind of social ordering that previous quarrels over rank cannot describe — naming it 'the aristocracy of species' and predicting that it will considerably influence the existence of the American republics? Quote the line that names this new aristocracy and identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: The phrase 'aristocracy of species' (l'aristocratie d'espèce) framed as a brand-new category emerging from colonization is a distinctive coinage. The slavery volumes (Vols III–IV) treat slavery directly but do not introduce this comparative-races/perfectibility framing; the property treatises and other corpus works are wholly off-topic.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**The work is *Traité de Législation: VOL II* by Charles Comte, Chapter 2 ("Of the limits placed by nature on the perfection of human faculties").**[^1]

This chapter argues that European colonization in America, southern Asia, "some of the islands of the great Ocean," and the south and west of Africa has introduced "a kind of aristocracy of which we had no idea, **the aristocracy of species**," which differs from prior aristocracies based on rank within the same species, and that this "new combination will considerably influence the existence of the American republics."[^1]

[^1]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL II*, [paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-ii-20331d/02-des-limites-mises-par-la-nature-au-perfectionnemen/translation/#p-44e2ce).

cases.prompt: Where in the corpus are the inhabitants of Tierra del Fuego and the neighboring Patagonians said to have a complexion approaching 'that of iron rust mixed with oil', adduced as evidence — together with their copper-colored kin running all the way to the Saint Lawrence and Bering Strait across one and a half million square leagues — that complexion does not vary with latitude or temperature? Quote the iron-rust-and-oil line and identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: The 'iron rust mixed with oil' simile applied to Patagonians, paired with the specific 1.5-million-square-leagues span from Tierra del Fuego to the Bering Strait, is a uniquely concrete factoid. The slavery volumes do not survey the American copper-colored peoples this way, and the climate framing is specific to this volume.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
In Charles Comte's *Traité de Législation, Vol. II*, Chapter 4 ("Of the physical causes to which the production of the diverse species of men has been attributed, and particularly of the influence of climates"), this description appears as evidence against climate influencing human complexion.[^1]

The quoted line reads: "The inhabitants of Tierra del Fuego, exposed, throughout the course of the year, to the most rigorous cold, and the Patagonians, who are their closest neighbors, are of a copper color or one approaching **that of iron rust mixed with oil**."[^1] The prior paragraph introduces this as part of the "American or copper-colored species," spread across "one and a half million square leagues, from the islands of Tierra del Fuego to the Saint Lawrence River and the Bering Strait."[^2]

[^1]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation, Vol. II*, [paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-ii-20331d/04-des-causes-physiques-auxquelles-a-ete-attribuee-la/translation/#p-2baa2e).
[^2]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation, Vol. II*, [paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-ii-20331d/04-des-causes-physiques-auxquelles-a-ete-attribuee-la/translation/#p-347d42).

cases.prompt: Where in the corpus is the supposed natural superiority of one race over another rebutted by pointing out that the republic of Athens reportedly had twenty thousand citizens and four hundred thousand slaves — a ratio of twenty to one, the same as that of whites to blacks in the colonies — and that great Romans owned 'up to five hundred, and sometimes even up to a thousand' slaves inside the capital alone, so that 'a twentieth of the men of the Caucasian species was therefore sufficient to maintain the other nineteen-twentieths' in a servitude harsher than that of blacks? Quote the Athens line and identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: The pairing of the specific 20,000-citizens / 400,000-slaves Athens figure with the 'up to five hundred, and sometimes even up to a thousand' Roman household-slave figure, deployed to invert the colonial-numbers argument for Caucasian superiority, is unique to this chapter. Vol IV gives Saint-Domingue numbers (40,000 / 452,000) but never the Athens 20:1 figure or the Roman household-slave count; the climate volume (Vol II) does not engage with Athenian or Roman slave numbers at all.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**This passage appears in Charles Comte's *Traité de Législation: VOL III*, Chapter 48 ("De la supériorité des peuples d’espèce caucasienne sur les peuples des autres espèces. — Des causes").**[^1]

Comte rebuts claims of the Caucasian (white) race's natural superiority over other races (such as blacks in European colonies) by noting that ancient Caucasians themselves practiced slavery on a massive scale. He writes: "In the republic of Athens, there were, it is assured, twenty thousand citizens and four hundred thousand slaves: that was twenty slaves for one free man, approximately the same proportion observed in the colonies between whites and blacks."[^1] He adds that great Romans owned "up to five hundred, and sometimes even up to a thousand" slaves inside the capital alone (plus multitudes on their domains), so "a twentieth of the men of the Caucasian species was therefore sufficient to maintain the other nineteen-twentieths in a servitude harsher than that to which blacks are subjected."[^1]

This shows that a tiny fraction of Caucasians could dominate and harshly enslave the vast majority of their own racial kin without external support (unlike colonial slavery backed by European states), undermining arguments for innate racial hierarchies in justifying slavery.

[^1]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL III*, [paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-iii-ca589a/48-de-la-superiorite-des-peuples-d-espece-caucasienne/translation/#p-b496c9).

The other three Comte volumes do not treat Caribbean plantation life narratively.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Charles Comte recounts this story in *Traité de Législation: VOL III*, Chapter 49 ("Du penchant à la servitude et de quelques autres vices attribués aux peuples d’espèces colorées. — Du droit que les blancs ont sur les noirs").**[^1]

There, he describes slaves in Louisiana who, under benevolent masters permitting them to accumulate a *peculium*, use their savings as children to purchase freedom for their elderly parents. grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL III*, Chapter 48 ("De la supériorité des peuples d’espèce caucasienne sur les peuples des autres espèces. — Des causes").**[^1]

cases.prompt: Which work in the corpus describes a real incident in which slave-owners, organized as a 'secret committee' calling themselves 'respectable gentlemen', issued a proclamation summoning a Sunday mob with 'the necessary tools' to demolish a Methodist chapel, then over two days tore down the building, ripped up Bibles, made flags from the missionary's linen, gave 'three times three' ferocious howls of victory, and afterward published a triumphant proclamation dated from Bridgetown on 21 October 1823? Quote the dated proclamation announcing that 'at midnight they completed the ruin of the church', and identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: The 21 October 1823 Bridgetown (Barbados) Methodist chapel demolition, with its 'three times three' howls, the linen-flags detail, the 'respectable gentlemen' self-designation and the verbatim proclamation declaring the missionary fled to St. Vincent, is a uniquely specific journalistic episode that appears nowhere else in the corpus. Vols I–III of Comte never reach this Caribbean material; the Spanish-American volumes (Comte's property treatises and Dunoyer) treat different matter; no other corpus work concerns West Indian abolitionism.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**The incident is described in *Traité de Législation: Volume IV* by Charles Comte, Chapter 18 ("On the reciprocal influence of slavery on religion, and of religion on slavery").**[^1][^2]

The chapter recounts how, in October 1823 in Barbados (specifically Bridgetown), slave-owners formed a secret committee that drafted and published a proclamation summoning "the gentry and other inhabitants" (whom they styled as "respectable gentlemen") to assemble the following Sunday with "the necessary tools" to demolish a Methodist chapel built for freedmen and slaves.[^1] The mob duly arrived, partially demolishing the chapel (ripping up Bibles and religious books used in the slaves' school), then attacked the missionary's house (destroying furniture, removing the roof, and making flags from his linens), and ended the first day with "three times three" ferocious howls of victory. They reconvened the next day to raze the chapel completely.[^1] Afterward, they issued a second proclamation dated from Bridgetown on 21 October 1823, announcing the deed as follows:

> Bridgetown, Wednesday, 21 October 1823.  
>   
> "The inhabitants of this island are respectfully informed that in consequence of the unprovoked and unmerited attacks, which have been repeatedly made by the community of Methodist missionaries, the villainous African Society (otherwise known as agents to the villanous African Society) [357], a number of *respectable gentlemen* have formed the resolution to put an end to the Methodist affair; that, with this in view, they began their work on Sunday evening, and that they have the very great satisfaction of announcing that **at midnight they completed the ruin of the church**. They must add to this information that the missionary escaped in a small vessel yesterday at noon, and has taken refuge on the island of St. Vincent, thereby avoiding the manifestation, toward him, of the public sentiments he so well deserved. It is to be hoped that, as this proclamation will be spread throughout all the islands and colonies, all persons who consider themselves *true friends of religion* will follow the laudable example of the Barbadians, by putting an end to Methodism and to Methodist churches."[^2]

cases.prompt: Which work in the corpus reconstructs the etymology of the word 'industry' from the Latin INDU (for INTUS, 'within') joined to STRUO ('I arrange, I set up, I construct'), and uses this philology to argue that the term was first applied to the cunning of swindlers — so that 'knights of industry' originally named men distinguished in fraud — citing the Abbé Galiani's claim, 'formally' written in 1750, that no fortune could grow without other fortunes being diminished? Quote the passage that gives the INTUS-STRUO derivation and identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: The combination of the INDU/INTUS-STRUO etymology, the 'knights of industry' (chevaliers d'industrie) gloss, and the dated 1750 reference to Abbé Galiani is a distinctive philological-economic argument unique to this work; no other corpus item (including the Comte legislation/property volumes) reconstructs the word 'industry' in this way.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Charles Dunoyer's *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I*, Chapter 11 ("I. Liberté compatible avec la manière de vivre des peuples purement industrieux").**[^1]

The key passage reconstructing the etymology is:

> § 1. The word *industry* is a nearly literal translation of the Latin word INDUSTRIA. Etymologists suppose that the latter must have been formed from two others, INDU for INTUS, *within*, and STRUO, *I arrange, I set up, I construct*. Thus one would have said INDUSTRIA from INTUS-STRUO, INTUS-STRUERE, *to construct within*, an action exercised in things or on things to arrive at a certain end.[^2]

Dunoyer uses this to argue that "industry" originally connoted skillful action of any kind, applied first to dishonorable arts like fraud: "one called *knights of industry, knights of the industry*, men distinguished in the art of fraud"[^3] — linking it to pre-wealth-creation theories like the Abbé Galiani's 1750 claim "that a fortune could not grow without other fortunes being diminished," under which "knaves were the industrious par excellence, the knights of industry."[^4]

[^1]: Full chapter context from *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I*, Ch. 11[/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/11-i-liberte-compatible-avec-la-maniere-de-vivre-des-/translation/].
[^2]: *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I*, Ch. 11[/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/11-i-liberte-compatible-avec-la-maniere-de-vivre-des-/translation/#p-06038f].
