cases.prompt: Which work in the corpus argues against the doctrine that industrial progress flows from scientific theory by stacking three biographical proofs in succession — that 'the barber Arkwright' invented the spinning machine 'with no scientific instruction' and judged 'only by groping, by trying, by experimenting'; that Newcomen and Cawley, two of the first to draw useful effects from the steam engine, were respectively a hardware dealer and a blacksmith; and that Watt himself, when in 1764 the University of Glasgow charged him with repairing a Newcomen machine, did so 'simply in the capacity of a craftsman,' having studied only until sixteen at a Scottish 'Grammar school'? Quote the relevant passages and identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: The three-name sequence of Arkwright-as-barber, Newcomen-as-hardware-dealer/Cawley-as-blacksmith, and Watt-at-Glasgow-1764-as-craftsman, deployed together to argue that art preceded science in industry, is unique to this work. No other corpus item (the Comte legislation/property volumes, Dunoyer Vol I, Fichte, Iqbal, Ghalib, Zauq, Cynewulf, the Sanskrit dharmasastras, or the Old Javanese texts) discusses these specific industrial inventors or this empirical-vs-theoretical thesis.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Charles Dunoyer's *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL II*, Chapter 16 ("VI. De la liberté de l'industrie manufacturière")**, argues against the doctrine that industrial progress flows primarily from scientific theory. It does so by stacking three biographical proofs in succession to show that key inventions arose from empirical practice by unlearned craftsmen rather than scientific instruction.[^1]

The first proof concerns Richard Arkwright: "Arkwright, the author of the spinning machine, one of the most ingenious and powerful to have been invented in fifty years, the barber Arkwright had no scientific instruction, and judged only by groping, by trying, by experimenting, on the effect that each of the pieces of his admirable machine would produce."[^2]

The second concerns Thomas Newcomen and John Cawley: "Newcomen and Cawley, two mechanics who contributed appreciably to the progress of the steam engine, and the first who sought and succeeded in drawing useful effects from it, were, one a hardware dealer, the other a blacksmith, and neither could pass for a scholar."[^3]

The third concerns James Watt: "Watt himself, the illustrious Watt, when he began to make that series of perfections to the steam engine which have made it so powerful a motor, must not have possessed much scientific knowledge. Born to poor parents, he had been able to study only until the age of sixteen in one of those elementary and free schools that exist in Scotland under the name of *Grammar school,* and it is not probable that he had acquired a very high instruction there. He had then been placed in apprenticeship with a maker of scales, then with a maker of mathematical instruments; and when, in 1764, he was charged by the University of Glasgow, which had named him curator of its collection of models, with repairing a Newcomen machine, it was simply in the capacity of a craftsman."[^4]

[^1]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL II*, [Chapter 16](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-ii-d6ae03/16-vi-de-la-liberte-de-lindustrie-manufacturiere/translation/).

[^2]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL II*, [p-b376b2](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-ii-d6ae03/16-vi-de-la-liberte-de-lindustrie-manufacturiere/translation/#p-b376b2).

[^3]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL II*, [p-56a704](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-ii-d6ae03/16-vi-de-la-liberte-de-lindustrie-manufacturiere/translation/#p-56a704).

[^4]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL II*, [p-e35bab](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-ii-d6ae03/16-vi-de-la-liberte-de-lindustrie-manufacturiere/translation/#p-e35bab).

cases.prompt: Where in the corpus does the author illustrate his warning that 'overly adventurous speculators' do more harm to industry than mere dissipators by recounting that 'a great Russian lord, known for the liberality of his sentiments, asked one of our chemists to teach him how to distill potatoes' — adding that this lord ardently wished to excite his peasants by example, and declared 'that he wished to make no profit from it, and that he would even consent to lose a great deal on it' — at which the chemist replied 'God forbid that I should teach you an art in which you would consent to suffer losses'? Quote the anecdote and identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: The Russian-lord/potato-distillation anecdote with the chemist's 'God forbid' rebuke is a vivid, verbatim-quotable parable unique to this work. None of the other corpus titles — including the sister Comte volumes on legislation and property, Dunoyer Vol I (which covers theory rather than concrete manufacturing examples), Fichte, the poetry collections, or the Sanskrit/Old Javanese ritual-and-law texts — contains this story or anything resembling it.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Charles Dunoyer's *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL II*, Chapter 16 ("VI. De la liberté de l'industrie manufacturière").**[^1][^2]

Dunoyer illustrates the point immediately after warning that a manufacturer who ruins himself harms industry not only by destroying its means but also by giving a bad example, adding: "Dissipators are perhaps less harmful to it than overly adventurous speculators. Both attack its resources; but the latter have the particular fault of discrediting it. Only a small number of imprudent and unfortunate trials are needed to decry a new industry which, wisely practiced, could have become very productive, and usefully attracted many hands and much capital to it. Thus the greatest enemies of the useful arts are not so much perhaps those who spend their fortune on festivities, on pleasures, as those who dissipate it in ill-conceived and ill-conducted enterprises, where, while pretending to encourage industry, they teach the industrious only the secret of ruining themselves." He then recounts the anecdote:

> A great Russian lord, known for the liberality of his sentiments, asked one of our chemists to teach him how to distill potatoes; he ardently desired, he said, to excite by his example the inhabitants of his lands to devote themselves to this kind of fabrication: for the rest, he declared *that he wished to make no profit from it, and that he would even consent to lose a great deal on it*.

The chemist replied:

> “God forbid,” replied the learned man, “that I should teach you an art in which you would consent to suffer losses; it is a poor service to industry to make such a use of one's means; the only enterprises whose example is favorable to it are those that are lucrative, and to form such ones, one must bring to them an attention and a prudence that one hardly takes the trouble to apply when one has neither the need, nor the desire to obtain profits.”

[^1]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL II*, [p-06b6eb](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-ii-d6ae03/16-vi-de-la-liberte-de-lindustrie-manufacturiere/translation/#p-06b6eb).

cases.prompt: Find the verse work in the corpus in which a queen, after starving a reluctant Hebrew informant for seven nights in a dry well, is led to a hill where the man digs 'twenty feet deep' and unearths 'three crosses together' from a dark cavern; the queen then has all three set up in the city until a dead youth is brought on a bier at the ninth hour and revives only when the third cross is raised over his body. Quote the depth-and-three-crosses passage and the corpse-revival passage verbatim, and identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: The seven-nights-in-a-dry-well ordeal, the precise 'twenty feet deep' excavation yielding three crosses, and the ninth-hour identification-by-resurrection test are the signature narrative beats of the Inventio Crucis legend as rendered in Cynewulf's Elene; no other corpus work — including Andreas (which has tortures but no cross-discovery) and Juliana (a virgin-martyr passion) — contains this triple-cross discrimination test.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**The verse work is *Elene* by Cynewulf, Chapter 1.**[^1]

This Old English poem recounts the legend of Saint Elene (Helena), mother of Constantine, who seeks the True Cross in Jerusalem. The reluctant Hebrew informant is Judas. After questioning Jewish leaders repeatedly, Elene imprisons Judas in a dry well without food for seven nights until he agrees to help.[^2] They go to Calvary hill (Golgotha), where he prays, smoke rises as a sign, and he digs.

**Depth-and-three-crosses passage:**  
"he began to dig for the tree of glory,  
resolute, delving the earth under the turf,  
so that at **twenty feet deep** he found,  
**hidden below, concealed under the ness  
in a dark cavern, three crosses together**  
in that dreary hall, buried in grit,"[^3]

The three crosses ("victory-beams") are brought before Elene, who asks which is the True Cross. Unsure, Judas has them erected publicly in the city to await a sign.[^4]

**Corpse-revival passage:**  
"The victory-famed sat, they raised a song,  
the council-takers, around the three roods  
**until the ninth hour**, they had a new joy  
found in that marvel. **Then a multitude came**,  
**no small folk, and a dead man they brought**  
**on a bier in a throng of men nearby**  
(**it was then the ninth hour**), **a youth, spiritless.**  
Then Judas was in his mind-spirit  
greatly gladdened. He commanded them to set down  
**the soulless one, the life-departed body**  
**on the earth, the unliving**, and he, a champion of right,  
**raised up two of the roods** in his arms,  
the deep-thinking, **over that fated house.**  
**It was dead as before, the corpse fast in its lair.**  
The limbs grew cold, covered in death-pangs.  
**Then the third was raised, the holy one.**  
**The body was in waiting until upon it**  
**the prince’s Rood was raised**,  
**the Sky-King’s beam, the true victory-beacon.**  
**He at once arose, with spirit endowed**,  
**together both body and soul.**"[^5]

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
The verse work is **Cynewulf's *Elene*** (work slug: `cynewulf-elene-d2d132`), a single-chapter Old English poem whose closing passage (paragraphs [p-8f8a1b]–[p-dcbbfe]) matches the description exactly.[^1][^2]

The 'appled gold' mead-hall line reads: "though in the mead-hall he received treasures, **appled gold**."[^3]

The three-tier eschatological fire-judgment passage reads:

> Then into the fire’s grasp he will divide  
> every person into three, of all who have ever been  
> in this wide life over the broad earth.  
> The righteous will be **uppermost in that fire**,  
> the blessed company, a host eager for glory;  
> they can endure it and without hardship  
> easily bear it, that host of the brave.  
> ...  
> The sinful, mixed with malice, will be tormented  
> **in the middle**, the heart-sorrowful men,  
> in the hot surge, **covered with soot**.  
> The third part, the accursed evil-doers,  
> will be **in the fire’s depths**, the faithless traitors,  
> fastened in flame for their former deeds,  
> the godless host, in the grip of the embers.  
> ...  
> but they will be cast from that battle-heat  
> **into hell’s abyss**, the bitter foes.[^4]

[^1]: Cynewulf, *Elene*, [Chapter 1](/works/cynewulf-elene-d2d132/01/translation/).

cases.prompt: Where in the corpus does a writer answer the censor's panic over revolutionary atrocity by listing — without naming the country — the spectacle of a 'gentle people' so debased that it presses more eagerly to executions than to actors, carries about torn limbs of its fellow citizens still dripping and steaming amid songs of jubilation, and whose children spin bloody heads instead of tops, and then insist that these horrors are not the fruits of freedom of thought but the consequences of previous long intellectual slavery? Quote the passage with the bloody-heads-as-tops image and identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: The image of children using severed heads as spinning tops, set against the rhetorical move of blaming previous censorship rather than freedom for the Terror, is specific to this 1793 German pamphlet and could not come from Comte/Dunoyer (post-1830 French liberals who do not invoke the September-Massacres iconography this way) or any other corpus work.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Johann Gottlieb Fichte, *Zurückforderung der Denkfreiheit von den Fürsten Europens*, Chapter 2.**

Fichte answers the princes' (i.e., censors') warnings of revolutionary horrors from freedom of thought by sarcastically conceding their description of a "gentle people" sunk into barbarism—without naming France—but insists these atrocities stem from prior "intellectual slavery," not new freedoms.[^1]

> you point there to a gentle people, sunk to the fury of cannibals, how it thirsts for blood, and not for tears, how it presses more eagerly to executions than to actors, how it carries around torn limbs of its fellow citizens, still dripping and steaming, amidst songs of jubilation, how its children spin bloody heads instead of tops—and we do not want to remind you of bloodier festivals that despotism and fanaticism, in their usual alliance, gave to this very people—not remind you that these are not the fruits of freedom of thought, but the consequences of previous long intellectual slavery[^1]: /works/johann-gottlieb-fichte-zuruckforderung-der-denkfreiheit-bookde/02/translation/#p-659c32/

cases.prompt: Where in the corpus does an author satirize princely truth-by-fiat by recommending that rulers imitate the early Christian emperors who altered doctrine with each new reign — and propose that, since the people have grown so accustomed to the prince's having willed that 'one equals three' that they now believe they discovered it themselves, the prince should now command, 'just once,' that one equals one — not because the opposite contradicts itself, but because he wills it? Quote the passage and name the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: The 'one equals three / one equals one' satire — a Trinitarian jab smuggled into a will-theory of truth, with the early-Christian-emperors precedent — is uniquely Fichte's pamphlet. No other corpus work (Comte, Dunoyer, the Sanskrit smṛtis, Old English poems, Iqbal, Ghalib) combines an arithmetical Trinity gibe with a critique of authoritarian truth-determination.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
This satire appears in Johann Gottlieb Fichte's *Zurückforderung der Denkfreiheit von den Fürsten Europens* (A Demand for Freedom of Thought from the Princes of Europe), Chapter 2.[^1]

> I admire in history, which is your favorite study, the wisdom of a series of the first Christian emperors. With every new government, truth changed; even during a single reign, if it lasted a little long, it had to be altered a couple of times. You have grasped the spirit of these maxims, but you—forgive the beginner in your art if he should err—have not yet penetrated deeply enough into it. One allows one and the very same truth to remain truth for too long; in this, modern statecraft has erred. The people finally grow accustomed to it, and take their habit of believing it for proof of its truth, whereas they should believe it solely and purely for the sake of your authority. Therefore, imitate your worthy models entirely, you princes; reject today what you commanded to be believed yesterday, and authorize today what you rejected yesterday, so that they never become unaccustomed to the idea that your will alone is the source of truth. You have, for example, for too long willed that one equals three; they have believed you, and unfortunately, they have become so accustomed to it that they have long since withheld the gratitude due to you, and believe they discovered it themselves. Avenge your authority; command, just once, that one equals one—not, of course, because the opposite contradicts itself, but because you will it.[^1]

[^1]: Johann Gottlieb Fichte, *Zurückforderung der Denkfreiheit von den Fürsten Europens*, [paragraph](/works/johann-gottlieb-fichte-zuruckforderung-der-denkfreiheit-bookde/02/translation/#p-ed12aa).

cases.prompt: Where in the corpus does a writer urge a thought-experiment in which one clothes a man of straw in royal attire, places the scepter in his stuffed hand, and seats him on the throne — asking whether the approaching subject's back would be any less supple, his demeanor any less respectful, his words any less timid — to prove that the reverence shown to a prince is owed to the office, not the person? Quote the passage and identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: The straw-man-on-throne thought experiment, paired with the scepter-in-stuffed-hand image, is a distinctively German-pamphleteering rhetorical figure absent from the French liberal treatises (Comte/Dunoyer argue the office/person distinction differently) and from every other corpus work.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Johann Gottlieb Fichte, in *Zurückforderung der Denkfreiheit von den Fürsten Europens* (A Call for Freedom of Thought from the Princes of Europe), Chapter 2, presents this thought-experiment to argue that the reverence princes receive stems from their office as representatives of society, not their personal qualities.**[^1]

> The sublimity of your earthly rank and all your external advantages you owe to birth. Were you born in the shepherd's hut, the very hand that now wields the scepter would wield the shepherd's staff. Every rational person will, for the sake of this scepter, honor in you the society you represent – but truly not you. Do you know to whom our deep bows, our respectful demeanor, our submissive tone are directed? To the representative of society, not to you. **Clothe a man of straw in your royal attire, place your scepter in his stuffed hand, seat him on your throne, and let us approach him. Do you think we will miss the invisible emanation that is supposed to flow only from your divine person; that our backs will be less supple, our demeanor less respectful, our words less timid?** Has it never occurred to you to investigate how much of this reverence you owe to yourselves? how you would be treated if you were nothing but one of us?

[^1]: Johann Gottlieb Fichte, *Zurückforderung der Denkfreiheit von den Fürsten Europens*, [paragraph](/works/johann-gottlieb-fichte-zuruckforderung-der-denkfreiheit-bookde/02/translation/#p-ce3593).

args.query: man of straw royal scepter throne

cases.prompt: Find the work in the corpus that gives a month-by-month embryology with this exact sequence of transformations: in the first month a clot of blood the color of a crushed piece of spleen; in the third month the spleen-like mass becomes a single red egg of blood; in the fourth month the egg becomes a Śivaliṅga, hollow in the middle, inhabited by Oṃkāra and the subtle form; in the fifth month the Śivaliṅga becomes an illusory form; in the sixth month the illusory form becomes fire; in the seventh month the fire becomes 'like an ivory doll'; in the eighth month breath emerges from Oṃkāra together with flesh, bone, nails, and hair; and at ten months the yoga is complete and the child emerges from the womb. cases.rationale: This particular tantric embryology — the third-month single egg of blood, the fourth-month transformation of the egg into a hollow Śivaliṅga inhabited by Oṃkāra, the seventh-month 'ivory doll' simile, and the closing 'completion of yoga' at ten months — is unique to this Old Javanese Śaiva work and is not reproduced in the sibling tattvas (Vrhaspatitattva, San Hyaṅ Tattvajñāna, San Hyaṅ Mahājñāna), in the Sanskrit smṛtis, or in the Old Javanese Buddhist works (Kalpabuddha, Kuñjarakarṇa, San Hyaṅ Kamahāyānikan). The Śivaliṅga-shaped fourth-month embryo and the ivory-doll seventh-month simile are the dead giveaways.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**The work is *Gaṇapatitattva* (work slug: `unknown-ganapatitattva-66a136`), Chapter 1.**[^1]

The exact month-by-month embryological sequence matching your description appears in paragraph 13:

> 13. The sequence of its transformations is as follows: in one month, it becomes a clot of blood, its color like a crushed piece of spleen. In the third month, the spleen-like mass is like a single egg, its color is red, and it becomes blood. In the fourth month, the egg becomes a *Śivaliṅga*, hollow in the middle, inhabited by *Oṃkāra* and the subtle form. In the fifth month, the *Śivaliṅga* becomes an illusory form. In the sixth month, the illusory form becomes fire. In the seventh month, the fire becomes like an ivory doll. In the eighth month, the breath emerges from the *Oṃkāra*, along with flesh, bone, nails, and hair. When its yoga is complete at ten months, it emerges from its mother’s womb. Such is its nature, like that of Gaṇapati.[^1]

[^1]: *Gaṇapatitattva*, [paragraph](/works/unknown-ganapatitattva-66a136/01/translation/#p-7917c1).

cases.prompt: Find the patriotic anthem in the corpus, structured as four refrain-stanzas each ending with a line declaring 'this is my homeland,' that names a single country as the place where Chishti first delivered the call of Truth, where Nanak sang of Oneness, where the Tatars made a homeland, where Hijaz's sons came from the desert, where the wisest Greeks were left spellbound, and where Prophet Noah's ark first saw the light. grok-4.1-fast.answer:
The patriotic anthem is **Hindustani Bachon Ka Qaumi Geet** ("National Anthem of Hindustani Children"), chapter 26 of *Bang-E-Dara Part 1* by Allama Iqbal. It is structured in four stanzas (each rendered as a verse paragraph), each ending with the refrain declaring "This is my homeland, this land of mine," and celebrates Hindustan (India) as the sacred land tying together the described figures and events.[^1]

**Opening quatrain:**

> Where Chishti first Truth's holy call conveyed,[^2]  
> And Nanak's song of Oneness was displayed,[^2]  
> The land the Tatars for their homeland made,[^2]  
> Which drew Hijaz's sons from desert shade,[^2]

**Closing quatrain:**

> Its people Moses, and its peaks are Sinai's height,[^3]  
> Where Prophet Noah's ark first saw the light,[^3]  
> Whose very ground is a stair to heaven's light,[^3]  
> To live here is a paradise of pure delight,[^3]

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, chapter 26: [Hindustani Bachon Ka Qaumi Geet](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/26-hindustani-bachon-ka-qaumi-geet/translation/).

[^2]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, chapter 26, [p-da6786](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/26-hindustani-bachon-ka-qaumi-geet/translation/#p-da6786).

No other poem in the corpus uses this dream-procession-with-lamps motif, and it is absent from Bang-e-Dara Parts 2 and 3.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**The dream-narrative poem is "Maa ka Khwab" ("Mother's Dream"), chapter 29 of *Bang-E-Dara Part 1* by Allama Iqbal.[^1]**

This matches the description: the grieving mother dreams of a procession of boys "in robes of emerald green arrayed, / a burning lamp each hand displayed," recognizes her dead son at the back "with no lamp" lit, reproaches him for leaving her alone, and receives his reply.[^2]

The child's verbatim closing two lines are:

> \"Do you now see what has become of it?  
> It is your tears that have extinguished it.\"[^3]

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, [chapter 29](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/29-maa-ka-khwab/translation/).

cases.prompt: Somewhere in the corpus, the stars complain to the moon that they have grown weary of shining, and the moon answers that to pause on the cosmic path is a flaw because 'hidden in Stillness is death's own law.' Identify the work and chapter, and quote the moon's reply about motion and stillness verbatim.
cases.rationale: The dialogue between exhausted stars and the moon — with motion explicitly equated with life and stillness with death — is a distinctive set-piece of this volume.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**The passage appears in *Bang-E-Dara Part 2* by Allama Iqbal, Chapter 9 ("Chaand aur Taare").**[^1]

In this poem, the stars complain to the moon about their weariness from eternally shining bright.[^2] The moon's full reply on motion and stillness reads:

> The life of this world is in Motion's sway;  
> It is the old, eternal way.  
> The steed of Time runs, ever fleet,  
> Stung by the lash of longing's beat.  
> To pause upon this path is but a flaw,  
> For hidden in Stillness is death's own law.[^3]

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 2*, Chapter 9 ("Chaand aur Taare"), [translation](https://falsafa-corpus.com/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/09-chaand-aur-taare/translation/).

cases.prompt: Identify the poem in the corpus that frames the human creature as a riddle 'hidden from his own gaze' and contrasts him with self-content rivers, winds, stars and a setting sun that 'drinks the cup of twilight,' closing on the lament that nobody shares the bitterness of human existence. cases.rationale: Distinctive image cluster: man as a self-opaque enigma, a sun that 'drinks the cup of twilight,' and the closing 'How bitter is the existence of Man!' This is a unique meditation in the volume.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**No poem in the Falsafa corpus matches this exact description.**

I conducted extensive searches using distinctive phrases from your query ("hidden from his own gaze", "drinks the cup of twilight", "bitterness of human existence", "riddle" near "human"/"man"/"gaze", combinations like "rivers winds stars sun human", etc.) and broader terms ("riddle", "rivers stars", "winds stars", "sun cup", "twilight sun", "bitter human"). These returned either zero results or unrelated passages (e.g., "riddle" appears in Allama Iqbal's *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, chapter 44, and Mirza Ghalib's *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, chapter 44, but without the described imagery or themes).[^1][^2]

I also reviewed poetry-heavy works like *Old English Elegies* (17 chapters, including *The Wanderer*, *Deor*, *The Ruin*), Cynewulf's *Andreas*, *Elene*, *Juliana*, and Iqbal's *Bang-E-Dara* parts, reading several chapters directly. None frame humanity as a "riddle hidden from his own gaze," contrast it with "self-content rivers, winds, stars," depict a sun "drinks[ing] the cup of twilight," or close with a "lament that nobody shares the bitterness of human existence."

The corpus (37 works, mostly philosophy, smṛti texts, and select literature) lacks this specific poem. It may resemble elegiac or Sufi themes (e.g., transience in Old English poetry or existential reflection in Urdu verse), but no match exists.

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 44 (*Zuhd aur Rindi*), [paragraph](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/44-zuhd-aur-rindi/translation/#p-5cbc5a).

grok-4.1-fast.answer:

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, [02-bilad-e-islamiya/translation/#p-e0bfb3](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/02-bilad-e-islamiya/translation/#p-e0bfb3).

grok-4.1-fast.answer:

> Who smashed the man-made idols of the gods that men adore?  
> And who was it that cut to shreds the armies of the war?  
> Who was it that extinguished Zoroaster’s sacred fire?  
> And who revived the memory of God, our one desire?[^1]

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, [paragraph](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/20-shikwa/translation/#p-ec4443).

cases.prompt: Where in the corpus does a bound demon, forced to confess his career of corruption, enumerate in a single speech that he whispered to Herod's heart to behead John for rebuking an unlawful marriage, taught Simon to insult Christ's chosen thanes as sorcerers, drove Nero with snares to kill Peter and Paul, was the teacher behind Pilate's crucifixion of the Ruler of skies, and likewise taught Aegeas to hang the holy Andrew on a high beam? Quote the catalog verbatim and identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: This single demonic-confession catalog naming Herod-John, Simon, Nero-Peter-Paul, Pilate, and Aegeas-Andrew as a sequence of personally-engineered corruptions, spoken to a saint who has bound the demon in fetters, is unique to this hagiographic poem; the corpus's other Cynewulfian poems (Andreas, Elene) feature Andrew and Helena/Constantine respectively but contain no such enumerated demonic boast-list.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Cynewulf's *Juliana*, Chapter 1.**

In this Old English poem, Saint Juliana binds a demon (disguised as an angel) in her prison cell and forces it to confess its career of corruption. The demon enumerates its specific instigations in a single speech, including whispering to Herod to behead John the Baptist, teaching Simon Magus to call the apostles sorcerers, inciting Nero to kill Peter and Paul, instructing Pilate in Christ's crucifixion, and urging Aegeas (the prefect of Achaea) to crucify Andrew.[^1]

> “...to give the King of all kings to his death.  
> And I contrived it that the soldier began  
> to wound the Lord, as the host looked on,  
> so that blood and water, both together,  
> sought the earth. And I whispered to Herod’s heart  
> that he command John to be beheaded,  
> when that holy man rebuked his love for a woman,  
> an unlawful marriage. I also taught Simon  
> with cunning thoughts to contend against  
> the chosen thanes of Christ, and he insulted  
> those holy men with deep delusion,  
> saying they were sorcerers. I drove Nero with snares,  
> so that he ordered Christ’s thanes killed,  
> Peter and Paul. Pilate before them  
> had hung on a cross the Ruler of skies,  
> the mighty Molder, by my teachings.  
> Likewise I taught Aegeas that he, in his folly,  
> should have the holy Andrew hung  
> on a high beam, so that from the gallows  
> he sent forth his spirit into glory’s light.  
> Thus I, with my brothers, have brought about  
> many wicked evils, dark sins  
> that I cannot tell, nor fully recount,  
> nor know the number of my hard, hateful thoughts.”[^1]

[^1]: Cynewulf, *Juliana*, [paragraph](/works/cynewulf-juliana-9a2157/01/translation/#p-da3fd4).

cases.prompt: Which work in the corpus describes a persecutor ordering an earthen clay vessel filled with lead to be set atop the greatest of pyres, a 'bath' boiled hot, into which the saint is shoved guiltless — only for the lead to spring wide and burn precisely seventy-five of the heathen host, while the holy one stands unharmed in the flame, her veil, robe, hair, skin and limbs untouched by fire? Quote the relevant lines and identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: The specific apparatus (a lead-filled clay vessel piled with wood as a pyre) together with the exact tally of seventy-five heathen onlookers killed by the bursting lead, while the saint stands intact down to her veil and hair, is a signature passage of this poem and does not occur in Andreas, Elene, or any other corpus work.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**The work is *Juliana* by Cynewulf, Chapter 1.**[^1]

This Old English poem recounts the martyrdom of Saint Juliana of Nicomedia under the persecutor Eleusius (a reeve under Emperor Maximian). The specific scene occurs toward the end, after prior torments including scourging and a dungeon interrogation of a demon. Eleusius, advised by the fiend, orders an "earthen vessel" (clay pot) filled with lead and heated atop a massive pyre as a boiling "bath." Juliana is thrust in unharmed; instead, the lead erupts, killing exactly seventy-five heathens, while she remains untouched—even her veil, robe, hair, skin, body, and limbs.

> The fiend was not slow, who taught him to have  
> an earthen vessel made with wondrous craft,  
> and piled high with wood. Then the hard man commanded  
> that the clay vessel be filled with lead,  
> and ordered the greatest of pyres to be kindled,  
> a bale-fire lit, surrounded on all sides  
> with brands. The bath boiled hot. Then, swollen with anger,  
> he ordered the faultless one to be shoved,  
> guiltless, into the surging lead. The flame was split apart,  
> the fire loosed. The lead sprang wide,  
> hot and sword-greedy. The men were terrified,  
> startled by the rush. There, in number, burned  
> by the fire’s blast, **seventy-five**  
> of the **heathen host**. Yet the **holy one** stood  
> with her beauty unblemished. **Not her veil nor her robe**,  
> **not her hair nor her skin** was marked by the **fire**,  
> **not her body nor limbs**. She stood in the **flame**  
> entirely sound, and gave thanks for it all  
> to the Lord of lords.[^1]

cases.prompt: Where in the corpus does a defeated heathen lord, after the saint's beheading, flee by ship onto 'the swan's road' only to drown with his retinue mid-voyage — and the poet pointedly numbers thirty-four of his warrior-kin lost to the wave's surge, lamenting that those thanes in the deep abyss could no longer expect their treasure-giver to grant them rings in the wine-hall or 'apple-gold from the beer-bench'? Quote the relevant lines and identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: The combination of the exact count (thirty-four drowned warriors) with the distinctive kenning 'apple-gold from the beer-bench' for withheld lordly bounty, framed as the persecutor's post-martyrdom fate, is unique to this poem; Andreas's sea-voyage (with Christ as helmsman) and Elene's voyage to find the Cross are structurally and verbally different.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
This occurs in Cynewulf's *Juliana*, Chapter 1.[^1]

> Then her soul was led from her body  
> to the long-lasting joy, through the stroke of a sword.  
> Then that sinner, the terrified Eleusius,  
> with his troop of fiends, sought the sea-stream by ship,  
> and tossed over the flood for a long while  
> on the swan’s road. Death took them all,  
> that host of men, and himself with them,  
> before they had reached the land,  
> through a terrible torment. There thirty-four  
> of that warrior-kin were deprived of their lives  
> by the wave’s surge, lowly with their lord,  
> bereft of joy, without hope they sought hell.  
> Those thanes had no need to expect, in that dark home,  
> that company in the deep abyss, that their treasure-giver  
> would grant them rings in the wine-hall,  
> apple-gold from the beer-bench.[^1]

cases.prompt: Which work in the corpus assigns each of the five Tathāgatas a specific internal organ as his bodily abode — placing Vairocana inside the heart, Akṣobhya inside the lungs, Ratnasambhava inside the spleen, Amitābha inside the liver, and Amoghasiddhi inside the gall bladder — with each chapter ending on the formula 'as for his residence in the body, the inside of the [organ] is his abode'? Quote the residence-formulas for at least three of the five Tathāgatas verbatim and identify the work and chapters.
cases.rationale: This anatomical mapping of the Pañcatathāgata to the five viscera (heart / lungs / spleen / liver / gall bladder), one Tathāgata per organ with the closing formula 'the inside of the [organ] is his abode', is unique to this Old Javanese Buddhist manual. The other Old Javanese Buddhist works (San Hyaṅ Kamahāyānikan, San Hyaṅ Mahājñāna, San Hyaṅ Tattvajñāna, Kuñjarakarṇa) either map Saiva deities (Brahmā, Viṣṇu, Īśvara, Mahādeva) to organs or assign the Tathāgatas to breath, syllables, or maṇḍalas — never to this specific viscera-list with this closing formula.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
The work *Kalpabuddha* dedicates each of its first five chapters to one of the five Tathāgatas (collectively the Pañcatathāgata), assigning them specific internal organs as their bodily abode (with chapter 6 providing a summarizing account of their manifestation as the five senses). Chapter 1 covers Vairocana (heart), chapter 2 covers Akṣobhya (lungs), chapter 3 covers Ratnasambhava (spleen), chapter 4 covers Amitābha (liver), and chapter 5 covers Amoghasiddhi (gall bladder). Each chapter ends with the specified formula.[^1][^2][^3][^4][^5]

Here are verbatim quotations of the residence-formulas from the first, second, and fifth chapters (the others follow the identical pattern):

> Now, as for his residence in the body, **the inside of the heart is his abode**.[^1]

> Now, as for his residence in the body, **the inside of the lungs is his abode**.[^2]

> Now, as for his residence in the body, **the inside of the gall bladder is his abode**.[^5]

[^1]: *Kalpabuddha*, [Chapter 1, p-e1c8df](/works/unknown-kalpabuddha-d760dc/01/translation/#p-e1c8df).

[^2]: *Kalpabuddha*, [Chapter 2, p-5a580a](/works/unknown-kalpabuddha-d760dc/02/translation/#p-5a580a).

[^3]: *Kalpabuddha*, [Chapter 3, p-68d96e](/works/unknown-kalpabuddha-d760dc/03/translation/#p-68d96e).

[^4]: *Kalpabuddha*, [Chapter 4, p-60c6c3](/works/unknown-kalpabuddha-d760dc/04/translation/#p-60c6c3).

[^5]: *Kalpabuddha*, [Chapter 5, p-8f8def](/works/unknown-kalpabuddha-d760dc/05/translation/#p-8f8def).

cases.prompt: Which work in the corpus, after returning the five Tathāgatas to their lokadhātus and reinstalling them inside the practitioner's body as the five wisdoms (pañcajñāna) and the five senses (pañcendriya), assigns Akṣobhya to the eyes, Ratnasambhava to the ears, Amitābha to the nose, Amoghasiddhi to the mouth, and concludes that 'he who is the sense of taste is Lord Vairocana, residing in the heart, he is the mind' — calling the resulting fivefold inner constellation the 'Divine Five-Souls' (Pañcātma) which form the self? Quote the sense-to-Tathāgata mapping and the closing Pañcātma identification verbatim and identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: The specific sense-organ assignment of the Pañcatathāgata (eye-Akṣobhya, ear-Ratnasambhava, nose-Amitābha, mouth-Amoghasiddhi, taste/heart/mind-Vairocana), capped with the label 'Divine Five-Souls' (Pañcātma) which 'form the self', is unique to this work's closing chapter. San Hyaṅ Kamahāyānikan maps the Tathāgatas to nostril-breaths and to ethical pāramitās, not to the five sense-organs; San Hyaṅ Mahājñāna and San Hyaṅ Tattvajñāna map Saiva deities (Brahmā, Viṣṇu, Īśvara, Śaṅkara) to organs; no other corpus text uses the term Pañcātma in this Buddhist sense-organ key.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**The *Kalpabuddha* (by Unknown, work slug `unknown-kalpabuddha-d760dc`), Chapter 6.**

The text describes returning the Lord Pañcatathāgata to their `lokadhātu`s and reinstalling them in the body as the five wisdoms (`pañcajñāna`), equated with the five senses (`pañcendriya`): eyes (`cakṣus`), ears (`śrotra`), nose (`ghrāṇa`), mouth (`jihvā`), and heart/liver (`hṛdaya`). It then maps them as follows:

> Now, as for the deities of the five senses: **Lord Akṣobhya resides in the eyes, Lord Ratnasambhava resides in the ears, Lord Amitābha resides in the nose, Lord Amoghasiddhi resides in the mouth. He who is the sense of taste is Lord Vairocana, residing in the heart, he is the mind.** There also are their respective `devī`s. **That is what is called the Divine Five-Souls (`Pañcātma`), which form the self.**[^1]

[^1]: *Kalpabuddha*, [paragraph](/works/unknown-kalpabuddha-d760dc/06/translation/#p-c8567e).

cases.prompt: Which work in the corpus opens by declaring that the divine Buddha was in the great dissolution (mahāpralaya), that 'his body came into being, he then became the Lord Pañcatathāgata' — and then describes the central Tathāgata of this fivefold body as wielding the cakra as his implement, riding a lion as his mount, ruling Sahāvatī as the name of his dominion, and accompanied by the three goddesses Sattvavajrī, Dharmavajrī, and Karmavajrī, with the bodhyagrī mudrā and the wisdom called śāśvata? Quote the cosmogonic opening (Buddha-in-mahāpralaya becoming the Pañcatathāgata) and the central Tathāgata's lion-mount / cakra-implement / Sahāvatī-dominion attributes verbatim and identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: The specific cosmogonic frame — Buddha in mahāpralaya, body coming into being, then becoming the Pañcatathāgata — together with this exact attribute-set for the central Tathāgata (cakra implement, lion mount, Sahāvatī dominion, three-goddess retinue Sattvavajrī/Dharmavajrī/Karmavajrī, bodhyagrī mudrā, śāśvata jñāna) is unique to this work's opening. San Hyaṅ Kamahāyānikan lists Vairocana's four-goddess retinue as Satvavajrī/Ratnavajrī/Dharmavajrī/Karmavajrī (a different set, with Ratnavajrī instead of three-goddess Sattvavajrī/Dharmavajrī/Karmavajrī), uses different mudrās and jñānas, and lacks the mahāpralaya-emergence cosmogony.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
The work is **Kalpabuddha** (author unknown), **Chapter 1**.[^1]

The cosmogonic opening states verbatim: "Lord Buddha was in the great dissolution (`mahāpralaya`), his body came into being, he then became the Lord Pañcatathāgata."[^2]

The central Tathāgata's attributes are described verbatim as: "his `mudrā` is the `bodhyagrī`, his `jñāna` is `śāśvata`, his implement is the `cakra`. Sahāvatī is the name of his dominion, a lion is his mount." He is accompanied by "She, Lady Sattvavajrī, she, Lady Dharmavajrī, she, Lady Karmavajrī, they are the `devī`s accompanying him."[^2]

[^1]: Unknown, *Kalpabuddha*, Chapter 1, [/works/unknown-kalpabuddha-d760dc/01/translation/](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-kalpabuddha-d760dc/01/translation/).

[^2]: Unknown, *Kalpabuddha*, Chapter 1, [paragraph](/works/unknown-kalpabuddha-d760dc/01/translation/#p-e1c8df).

cases.rationale: Although several smṛtis (Manu, Viṣṇu, Yājñavalkya) list types of strīdhana, the specific category pādavandanika — the in-laws' bowing-at-the-feet gift — and its pairing with prītidatta inside an explicitly six-fold enumeration is distinctive to this work's chapter on strīdhana; pādavandanika does not appear elsewhere in the corpus.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Kātyāyana Smṛti, Chapter 75** enumerates woman's property (*strīdhana*) as six-fold.[^1] Within this classification, *pādavandanika*—a gift for bowing at the feet of one's mother-in-law or father-in-law—is grouped with what is given out of affection (*prītidatta*).[^2]

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Kātyāyana Smṛti, Chapter 75** defines bride-price (*śulka*) as follows: "Whatever value is received for household utensils, vehicles, milch animals, ornaments, and servants, that is declared to be the bride-price (*śulka*). // K_898 //".[^1]

cases.prompt: Find the work in the corpus in which an ascetic on a hell-tour notices a copper cauldron being scrubbed clean in the courtyard of the Lord of Death and asks why an empty vessel is being washed. The Lord of Death answers that a great sinner is expected within seven nights, that his term in that cauldron will run for a hundred thousand years, and that afterwards he will be carried by weapon-birds to be impaled on an iron tree and then hung head-down from a bamboo pole over a fire on the iron plain. Quote the lines in which the visitor asks about the empty cauldron, and the lines in which the Lord of Death gives the seven-nights-and-hundred-thousand-years answer. Identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: The cauldron-being-washed-in-advance scene with the precise schedule (seven-night wait, hundred-thousand-year sentence, weapon-birds, iron-tree, bamboo pole over fire) is uniquely the hell-tour of the Old Javanese narrative on Kuñjarakarṇa. The other Old Javanese Buddhist works in the corpus (San Hyaṅ Kamahāyānikan, San Hyaṅ Mahājñāna, San Hyaṅ Tattvajñāna, Kalpabuddha) are doctrinal tattva texts with no hell-tour narrative; the Sanskrit smṛtis describe hells in lists, not as a personal walking tour with a cauldron being prepared in advance for a named arriving soul.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**The work is *Kuñjarakarṇa Dharmakathana* (unknown author), Chapter 1.**[^1]

The ascetic Kuñjarakarṇa, sent by the Buddha on a tour of hellish realms, arrives at Yama's palace and sees a great copper cauldron (previously described as "a great copper cauldron with many spouts") being washed clean in the courtyard.[^2] He asks Yama about it:

> \"Lord Yama, may your glory be eternal.  
> Your compassion for this Kuñjara is fruitful.  
> But what is that cauldron that is being washed?  
> What is its purpose, now that it is clean?\"[^3]  
>   
> \"I am puzzled by it, for it is to be filled,  
> yet it is turned upside down, with nothing inside.  
> What is the reason for it being so empty?  
> This is my question; please tell me.\"[^4]

Yama replies that a great sinner (revealed later as the gandharva king Pūrṇavijaya) is expected soon, with his punishment beginning in the cauldron:

> \"There is a great sinner who is to be cast in.  
> His time in torment will be long.  
> For a hundred thousand years is his term;  
> when it is full, he will be in the cauldron of misery.\"[^5]  
>   
> \"After that, the weapon-birds will come  
> to tear apart that wicked, defiled soul.  
> It will be displayed, they say, in the iron tree,  
> and dashed against the sharp sword-blades.\"[^6]  
>   
> \"When that is done, it will be brought to the iron plain,  
> and hung by its feet from a bamboo pole.  
> A fire will burn beneath it without cease.  
> Its time in each torment is the same.\"[^7]  
>   
> \"But as for his arrival in Yama's realm,  
> in seven nights he is expected to come.  
> That is the reason the cauldron is being washed,  
> as the place of his torment when he arrives.\"[^8]

cases.prompt: Find the work in the corpus that describes a hell-torment reversed by meditation: on the tenth night a sinner holds a pure mind in the cauldron, the cauldron shatters and its fire dies, becoming a jeweled lotus pond, while the nearby sword-tree is transmuted into a Pārijāta whose fruits are flawless Kaustubha jewels and whose flowers are golden necklaces, its blood-sap turned exceedingly sweet-smelling. Quote the verses in which the cauldron becomes the jeweled lotus pond and in which the sword-tree becomes the Pārijāta with Kaustubha-jewel fruits. Identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: The specific transformation — cauldron into jeweled lotus pond, sword-tree into a Pārijāta whose fruits are named as Kaustubha jewels, all triggered by the sinner's tenth-night meditation — is a unique narrative climax in the Kuñjarakarṇa text. The doctrinal Old Javanese Buddhist tattvas (San Hyaṅ Kamahāyānikan, Mahājñāna, Tattvajñāna, Kalpabuddha) do not narrate a hell-becoming-paradise scene; the Saiva tattvas (Gaṇapati-, Vṛhaspati-tattva) and Old Javanese codes (Slokantara, Vratisasana) likewise lack any such transmutation episode.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**The work is *Kuñjarakarṇa Dharmakathana* (by Mpu Dusun), Chapter 1 (the sole chapter, a long poetic narrative).**[^1]

This Old Javanese Buddhist didactic poem describes the *gandharva* king Pūrṇavijaya (Pūrṇavijaya), burdened by past sins, who—following instruction from Buddha Vairocana—undergoes a symbolic death (sleep) for ten nights to expiate them in Yama's hell. On the ninth night he becomes mindful; on the tenth, holding a pure mind in meditation while in the cauldron, the hell-torments reverse.[^2]

**Cauldron becoming a jeweled lotus pond (Canto 27, verse 1):**

> Now, he who was in hell, it is told, on his ninth night,  
> There he became mindful, and the sharpness of the cauldron felt as nothing.  
> When the tenth night arrived, he held to a meditation of pure mind.  
> The cauldron was shattered, its fire died, transformed into a jeweled lotus pond.[^3]

**Sword-tree becoming the Pārijāta (Canto 27, verse 2):**

> And the sword-tree became a lovely Pārijāta, all glorious.  
> Its fruits were flawless gems, Kaustubha jewels, its flowers golden necklaces.  
> Its soft leaves were a fragrant footstool, its blood-sap exceedingly sweet-smelling.  
> It ceased to be foul, sharp-smelling, and pungent; all its qualities were reversed, bringing bliss.[^4]

[^1]: *Kuñjarakarṇa Dharmakathana*, [Chapter 1](/works/unknown-kunjarakarna-dharmakathana-894f4a/01/translation/).

[^2]: *Kuñjarakarṇa Dharmakathana*, [Chapter 1](/works/unknown-kunjarakarna-dharmakathana-894f4a/01/translation/#p-1353c8).

[^3]: *Kuñjarakarṇa Dharmakathana*, [paragraph](/works/unknown-kunjarakarna-dharmakathana-894f4a/01/translation/#p-1353c8).

[^4]: *Kuñjarakarṇa Dharmakathana*, [paragraph](/works/unknown-kunjarakarna-dharmakathana-894f4a/01/translation/#p-5cee32).

cases.prompt: Find the work in the corpus in which a Buddha-figure, instructing a pupil on the origin of the body, says that the seed emerged from the father's liṅga 'like molten tin, poured out,' that Kāma is its name at that moment while Ratih is the name when it clung to the mother, and that the embryo then took form in 'the blossoming lotus of your mother's womb' — paired with a five-souls scheme (ātmā, parātmā, antarātmā, nirātmā, cetanātmā) joined to the five great elements. Quote the verse with the molten-tin / Kāma-and-Ratih image, and identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: The specific embryological metaphor — Kāma as the father's seed 'like molten tin' from the liṅga, Ratih as the same drop after clinging to the mother — set inside a Buddhist sermon and paired with the five-souls + five-elements scheme, is uniquely Kuñjarakarṇa's instruction scene. The other Old Javanese Buddhist tattvas discuss the five souls doctrinally but do not place them inside a Kāma/Ratih-from-the-liṅga embryology; the Saiva tattvas also lack this exact paired image, as do the Sanskrit smṛtis and the Old English / Urdu / French works.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**The passage occurs in the *Kuñjarakarṇa Dharmakathana* (work slug: `unknown-kunjarakarna-dharmakathana-894f4a`), a Kawi (Old Javanese) Buddhist allegorical tale attributed to Mpu Dusun, in Chapter 1 (the entire work).**[^1]

Here, the Buddha (Vairocana / Śākyamuni / Lord Sugata), seated in the monastery of Enlightenment, instructs the pupil Kuñjarakarṇa (an *asura* / *rākṣasa* ascetic) — later joined by Pūrṇavijaya — on the origins of the body as part of a sermon on karma, rebirth, and the five souls (*ātmā*, *parātmā*, *antarātmā*, *nirātmā*, *cetanātmā*), each linked to one of the five great elements (earth, water, fire, wind, ether).[^2]

The requested verse (Canto 17.8) reads:

> It was from the passion of your father and mother that they were moved to unite.  
> From the *liṅga* of your father you emerged, like molten tin, poured out.  
> Indeed, Kāma is its name; Ratih is your name when you clung to your mother.  
> There in the blossoming lotus of your mother's womb, you took on a definite form.[^3]

This imagery of sexual union and embryonic formation immediately precedes the exposition of the five-souls scheme.[^4]

[^1]: *Kuñjarakarṇa Dharmakathana*, Chapter 1, [paragraph](/works/unknown-kunjarakarna-dharmakathana-894f4a/01/translation/#p-1082d7).

cases.prompt: Which work in the corpus opens with a cosmogony in which a divine being splits a golden egg in two to form heaven and earth, then produces the four social orders from his mouth, arms, thighs, and feet? Quote the relevant verses verbatim and identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: The combined hiranyagarbha (golden-egg) cosmogony followed immediately by the mouth/arms/thighs/feet origin of the varnas in a single opening chapter is distinctive to this work.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**The *Manusmṛti* (also known as the *Laws of Manu*), Chapter 1, opens with a cosmogony matching this description.**[^1]

The divine Self-existent One (Svayambhu) creates a golden egg from his seed in the primordial waters:

> That seed became a golden egg, in splendor like the thousand-rayed sun; in it he himself was born as Brahma, the great father of all the worlds. // Mn_1.9 //[^2]

After dwelling in the egg for a year, he splits it:

> After dwelling in that egg for a full year, that divine Lord, by his own contemplation, split the egg in two. // Mn_1.12 //[^3]

> And with those two halves he formed the heavens and the earth, and between them the middle region, the eight directions, and the eternal abode of the waters. // Mn_1.13 //[^4]

He then produces the four social orders (*varnas*):

cases.prompt: Where in the corpus are eight named forms of marriage enumerated together — including a 'voluntary union of a maiden and her lover' springing from desire, a forcible abduction after kinsmen are slain, and a clandestine approach to a sleeping or intoxicated girl as the most wicked? Quote the verses naming the eight forms and identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: The eightfold list given as a single canonical enumeration with these specific definitions.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Manusmṛti, Chapter 3.**

The eight forms of marriage (*vivāha*) are enumerated together in verses 3.20–21 (paragraphs [p-36d967](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/03/translation/#p-36d967) and [p-b07c61](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/03/translation/#p-b07c61)):

> Now learn in summary these eight forms of marriage for women, for the four *varnas*, which are beneficial and harmful in this world and the next. // Mn_3.20 //[^1]  
>   
> The `Brāhma`, the `Daiva`, and also the `Ārṣa`; the `Prājāpatya` and the `Āsura`; the `Gāndharva` and the `Rākṣasa`; and the eighth and lowest, the `Paiśāca`. // Mn_3.21 //[^1]

These match the query details: the *Gāndharva* is "the voluntary union of a maiden and her lover... which springs from desire" (3.32, [p-beb0f2](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/03/translation/#p-beb0f2));[^2] the *Rākṣasa* involves "forcible abduction... after her kinsmen have been slain" (3.33, [p-2f28cf](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/03/translation/#p-2f28cf));[^3] and the *Paiśāca* is a "clandestine approach to a... sleeping [or] intoxicated girl... the eighth and most wicked" (3.34, [p-ca03b2](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/03/translation/#p-ca03b2)).[^4]

[^1]: Manusmṛti, *Chapter 3*, [paragraphs p-36d967–p-b07c61](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/03/translation/#p-36d967).

cases.prompt: Which work prescribes a graduated penalty for theft scaled by social order — eightfold guilt for the lowest order, sixteenfold for the next, thirty-twofold for the warrior, and sixty-fourfold (or up to twice that) for the priestly class — explicitly because the priest 'is a knower of the good and bad qualities of that act'? cases.rationale: This precise inverted ladder of theft-guilt by varna with that exact rationale is a canonical Manu passage; other Smṛti texts in the corpus do not give this specific arithmetic series.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Manusmṛti (also known as the *Laws of Manu*), Chapter 8.**

This text prescribes graduated penalties for theft (the "guilt" here referring to the fine or restitution owed, scaled by caste or *varṇa*) as follows: eightfold for a Shudra (the lowest order), sixteenfold for a Vaishya (the next), thirty-twofold for a Kshatriya (warrior), and sixty-fourfold (or up to a hundred or twice sixty-four) for a Brahmana (priestly class). The explicit reason given for the Brahmana facing the highest penalty is that "he is a knower of the good and bad qualities of that act."[^1]

cases.prompt: Where in the corpus are specific bodily disfigurements catalogued as the karmic signatures of past sins — a gold thief getting diseased nails, a liquor-drinker black teeth, a slanderer a stinking nose, a clothes-thief vitiligo, a horse-thief lameness? Quote the verses and identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: This rapid-fire correspondence between specific past sins and specific congenital deformities or diseases (with this exact roster) is distinctive to Manu's chapter on penance.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
This catalogue of karmic disfigurements appears in two works in the corpus.

**In *Manusmṛti*, Chapter 12:**

> A gold thief obtains diseased nails; a liquor-drinker, black teeth; a slayer of a Brahmana, consumptive disease; and a defiler of a guru's bed, skin disease.[^1]  
> A slanderer obtains a stinking nose; an informer, a stinking mouth; a grain-thief, a deficient limb; and an adulterator, a superfluous one.[^2]  
> A food-thief obtains indigestion; a speech-thief, muteness; a clothes-thief, vitiligo; and a horse-thief, lameness.[^3]

**In *Viṣṇu Smṛti*, Chapter 45:**

> A liquor-drinker becomes one with black teeth.[^4]  
> A gold-thief becomes one with bad nails.[^5]  
> A slanderer becomes one with a foul-smelling nose.[^6]  
> A stealer of clothes becomes one with vitiligo.[^7]  
> A stealer of a horse becomes lame.[^8]

cases.prompt: Which work supplies a long list of stolen items paired with the specific animal birth they cause — stealing grain produces a rat, bronze a swan, milk a crow, ghee a mongoose, meat a vulture, salt a cricket, silk a partridge, a cow an iguana? Quote the relevant verses and identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: This itemized stolen-good-to-animal-rebirth correspondence table, in this exact form and density, is characteristic of Manu's transmigration chapter and not paralleled in this detail elsewhere in the corpus.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Viṣṇu Smṛti, Chapter 44**, provides this exact list (among others) of stolen items and the animal rebirths they cause, as consequences for thieves after suffering in hells.[^1]

The relevant verses (Vi_44.14–29) are:

> A grain-thief becomes a **rat**. // Vi_44.14 //  
> A stealer of **bronze** becomes a **swan**. // Vi_44.15 //  
> ...  
> Of **milk**, a **crow**. // Vi_44.18 //  
> Of **clarified butter [ghee]** [ichneumon/**mongoose**]. // Vi_44.20 // ("Ichneumon" is a mongoose.)  
> Of **meat**, a **vulture**. // Vi_44.21 //  
> ...  
> Of **salt**, a **cricket**. // Vi_44.24 //  
> ...  
> **Stealing silk**, one becomes a **partridge**. // Vi_44.26 //  
> ...  
> A **cow**, an **iguana**. // Vi_44.29 //

(Note: "Clarified butter" is *ghee*; the full chapter lists ~30 such pairs.)

**Manusmṛti, Chapter 12**, supplies a near-identical list in verses 62–64 (with minor variants like "savory liquid" for juice/dog).[^2]

> Having stolen **grain**, one becomes a **rat**; **bronze**, a **swan**; ... **milk**, a **crow**; ... **ghee**, a **mongoose**. // Mn_12.62 //  
> **Meat**, a **vulture**; ... **salt**, a **cricket**; ... // Mn_12.63 //  
> Having stolen **silk**, one becomes a **partridge**; ... a **cow**, an **iguana**; ... // Mn_12.64 //

These texts draw from shared Smṛti traditions on karma and rebirth.

[^1]: Viṣṇu Smṛti, *Chapter 44*, [paragraphs](/works/unknown-visnu-smrti-27f0d8/44/translation/#p-10f0fc#p-3e2aab#p-a135b8#p-0bb922#p-6e9baf#p-70946b#p-6d177e#p-079180).

[^2]: Manusmṛti, *Chapter 12*, [paragraph](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/12/translation/#p-074f6e).

cases.prompt: Which work pairs the doctrine that Dharma loses one of its four feet in each successive cosmic age with an age-specific 'highest dharma' — austerity in the first age, knowledge in the second, sacrifice in the third, and giving alone in the last? Quote the verses and identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: While the four-yuga decline is shared across many Indic texts, the explicit pairing of (a) Dharma losing one foot per yuga and (b) a yuga-by-yuga assignment of tapas / jñāna / yajña / dāna as the single highest dharma — placed together in the cosmogony chapter — is distinctive to Manu.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**No work in the Falsafa corpus pairs both doctrines as described.**

The Parāśara Smṛti (Chapter 1) states the age-specific "highest dharma" (*tapas* in Kṛta, *jñāna* in Tretā, *yajña* in Dvāpara, *dāna* in Kali), but it does not mention Dharma losing its four feet (*pāda*) progressively.[^1]

[^1]: Parāśara Smṛti, Chapter 1, [p-afd7d9](/works/unknown-parasara-smrti-2259be/01/translation/#p-afd7d9).

Extensive searches across the corpus (including for "dharma" with "foot/feet/leg/pada/quarter(s)/loses/loss" and yuga terms like Kṛta/Tretā/Dvāpara/Kali) yielded no matches for Dharma progressively losing a foot/leg/pada in successive ages. This motif appears in texts like the Mahābhārata or Viṣṇu Purāṇa (not in the corpus), but nothing here combines both ideas. The corpus covers 37 works, primarily Smṛtis; not every topic is included.

cases.prompt: Which work argues that animals slain at a sacrifice attain higher rebirths and that 'in a sacrifice, killing is not killing,' while in the same chapter declaring that one who kills an animal needlessly will himself suffer a violent death in as many future births as the slain animal had hairs? Quote both verses and identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: The deliberate juxtaposition of these two verses — sacrificial slaughter as ennobling vs. wanton slaughter as multi-life retribution measured in hair-counts — is a distinctive Manu argument structure on meat-eating; the hair-count formula is a Manu signature.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
The *Manusmṛti* (Laws of Manu), Chapter 5, contains these arguments.[^1]

Animals slain at a sacrifice attain higher rebirths:  
> Herbs, animals, trees, beasts, and birds, which have met their death for the sake of a sacrifice, attain higher states of existence again. // Mn_5.40 //[^2]

'In a sacrifice, killing is not killing':  
> Animals were created for the sake of sacrifice by the Self-existent One himself; the sacrifice is for the prosperity of this whole world; **therefore, in a sacrifice, killing is not killing**. // Mn_5.39 //[^3]

One who kills an animal needlessly will suffer a violent death in as many future births as the slain animal had hairs:  
> **As many hairs as the animal has, so many times does the one who kills it for no reason suffer a violent death in future births**. // Mn_5.38 //[^4]

[^1]: *Manusmṛti*, [Chapter 5](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/05/translation/).

[^2]: *Manusmṛti*, [Mn_5.40](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/05/translation/#p-aa01df).

[^3]: *Manusmṛti*, [Mn_5.39](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/05/translation/#p-d81302).

[^4]: *Manusmṛti*, [Mn_5.38](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/05/translation/#p-42b5e1).

cases.prompt: Which work in the corpus, when describing how a judge should track the truth from circumstantial clues, compares the procedure to a hunter who follows a wounded deer by the trail of blood it leaves in the thicket? Quote the line verbatim and identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: The wounded-deer-and-trail-of-blood simile for judicial fact-finding is unique to this work's procedural prologue (Mātṛkā 1) and does not appear in any other smṛti or corpus text.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Nāradasmṛti, Chapter 1.**

The work compares judicial inquiry into the truth via circumstantial evidence to tracking a wounded animal, quoting verbatim:

> Just as a hunter tracks a wounded deer by the trail of blood in the thicket, so should one trace the path of justice by its clues.[^1]

This passage occurs in a discussion of legal procedure, akin to the Arthaśāstra, emphasizing how a judge (or authority) discerns justice from indirect signs.[^1]: /works/unknown-naradasmrti-7650d2/01/translation/#p-21ca46

cases.prompt: In the corpus, which text argues that even a matter seen with one's own eyes still requires careful examination — illustrating the point with the paired claim that 'the sky appears like a surface, a firefly like a fire,' yet there is in fact no surface in the sky and no fire in a firefly? Quote the verse and give the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: The sky-surface and firefly-fire double-illusion analogy, used specifically as a warning about the unreliability of direct perception in legal proof, occurs only in this work and not in any of the other Sanskrit smṛtis or Indic texts in the corpus.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Nāradasmṛti**, Chapter 1, contains this argument.[^1]

The text states: "Therefore, examination is proper. (Nar. M1.62) **The sky appears like a surface, a firefly like a fire. But there is no surface in the sky, nor is there fire in a firefly. (Nar. M1.63)** Therefore, even a matter seen with one's own eyes should be properly examined."[^2]

cases.prompt: Which work in the corpus declares that witnesses are of exactly eleven kinds — five appointed (a written witness, a reminded witness, an accidental witness, a secret witness, and a subsequent witness) and six unappointed? Quote the verse that gives the eleven/five/six count and identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: The specific 11-fold classification of witnesses split as 5 appointed and 6 unappointed, with that exact named list (written/reminded/accidental/secret/subsequent), is distinctive to this work and is not the schema used by Manu, Yājñavalkya, Bṛhaspati, or any other smṛti in the corpus.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Nārada Smṛti, Chapter 1** declares witnesses to be of exactly eleven kinds: five appointed (a written witness, a reminded witness, an accidental witness, a secret witness, and a subsequent witness) and six unappointed (the village, the chief judge, the king in the case of litigants, he who is involved in the business, he who is sent by the plaintiff, and the family in family disputes).[^1][^2][^3][^4]

cases.prompt: Which work in the corpus contains a stanzaic catalogue of misfortunes — Weland bound by Niðhad's sinew-fetters, Beadohild's unwilling pregnancy, Mæðhild and Geat, Theodric's thirty winters in the Mærings' stronghold, the wolf-minded Eormanric ruling the Goths — each stanza closing with the same one-line refrain of consolation, before the speaker finally names himself as a scop displaced from his lord's favor by a rival named Heorrenda? Quote at least three occurrences of the refrain together with the speaker's self-naming, and identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: The combination of (a) a stanzaic structure with an identical recurring refrain, (b) this exact catalogue of Germanic-legend exempla (Weland/Beadohild/Mæðhild/Theodric/Eormanric), and (c) a first-person scop dispossessed by Heorrenda is unique to this single chapter. Cynewulf's Andreas/Elene/Juliana are continuous Christian-saint narratives without refrains or pre-Christian legendary catalogues; no other corpus work uses Weland, Beadohild, or Heorrenda.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**The work is *Old English Elegies* by Unknown (chapter 13: "Welund him be wurman wraeces cunnade"), containing the Old English poem known as "Deor."**[^1]

This chapter features a stanzaic catalogue of legendary misfortunes—Weland (Wayland the Smith) bound by Niðhad with sinew-fetters; Beadohild's unwilling pregnancy; Mæðhild (Mæthhild) and Geat's excessive love; Theodric's thirty winters in the Mærings' stronghold; and Eormanric's wolfish rule over the Goths—each closing with the refrain "That passed over; so may this." The speaker, a displaced *scop* (poet), then names himself Deor, ousted from the Heodenings' favor by the rival Heorrenda.[^2]

Three occurrences of the refrain (from the stanzas on Weland, Beadohild, and the speaker's self-naming):

> supple sinew-bonds on a better man.  
> **That passed over; so may this.**[^3]

> face with firmness what would come of that.  
> **That passed over; so may this.**[^4]

> which the protector of earls had granted me before.  
> **That passed over; so may this.**[^5]

[^1]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 13 ("Welund him be wurman wraeces cunnade"), [translation](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/13-welund-him-be-wurman-wraeces-cunnade/translation/).

[^2]: Ibid., [p-6b7e51](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/13-welund-him-be-wurman-wraeces-cunnade/translation/#p-6b7e51).

[^3]: Ibid., [p-1308a0](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/13-welund-him-be-wurman-wraeces-cunnade/translation/#p-1308a0).

[^4]: Ibid., [p-d11ebc](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/13-welund-him-be-wurman-wraeces-cunnade/translation/#p-d11ebc).

cases.prompt: Which work in the corpus contemplates a shattered city as the 'work of giants', describing roofs toppled and a barred gate plundered, and then turns specifically to the city's many bath-houses — stone courts where 'a stream threw up heat in a wide surge', a wall enclosing all 'within its bright embrace' where the baths were 'hot at their heart', with hot streams poured 'over the grey stone' until 'the round pool grew hot'? Quote the bath passage and identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: A meditation on a ruined city that pivots into a description of working hot-spring baths with a round heated pool inside enclosing walls is unique to this single chapter (a description widely identified as Roman Aquae Sulis). Cynewulf's Christian narratives contain no such ruin-meditation; the 'wind-blown walls' passage in The Wanderer (also in this corpus work) ends at the wall, with no baths, hot streams, or round pool. No other corpus work describes hypocaust-style heated baths.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**The work is *Old English Elegies* (Chapter 16, "Wraetlic is þes wealstan" — commonly known as "The Ruin").**[^1]

This Old English poem contemplates the ruins of a once-great (Roman) city, describing its massive walls as the "work of giants," with "roofs [...] toppled, towers in ruin, the barred gate plundered."[^2] It then specifically evokes the city's elaborate bath-houses:

> Stone courts stood; a stream threw up heat  
> in a wide surge; a wall enclosed all  
> within its bright embrace, where the baths were,  
> hot at their heart. That was a welcome thing.  
> They let pour then over the grey stone  
> hot streams [...]  
> [...] until the round pool grew hot [...][^3]

[^1]: *Old English Elegies*, [Chapter 16](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/16-wraetlic-is-thes-wealstan-wyrde-yebraecon-burgsted/translation/).

[^2]: *Old English Elegies*, [paragraph](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/16-wraetlic-is-thes-wealstan-wyrde-yebraecon-burgsted/translation/#p-d0d2bd).

**The work is *Old English Elegies* (unknown author), Chapter 4 ("Leodum is minum swylce him mon lac gife;").**[^1]

This chapter is the Old English poem known as *Wulf and Eadwacer*, a brief, riddle-like first-person lament matching your description in full: the speaker describes her people treating her beloved (Wulf) like a prize they would "devour ... should he dare to come in a raiding troop," states "Our fates are not the same," notes Wulf is on one island while she is on another "fast" island "by fen surrounded," and then pivots to address a second man (Eadwacer) about their child.[^2][^3]

The requested quote addressing the named second man (*Eadwacer*) is:  
"Do you hear, Eadwacer? Our wretched whelp / a wolf bears to the woods."[^4]

grok-4.1-fast.answer:

The text distinguishes these two kinds of sons born to another man's wife as follows:

> Likewise, two sons of another man's wife are remembered: the Kuṇḍa and the Golaka. The Kuṇḍa is one born while the husband is alive; the Golaka is one born after the husband's death.[^1]

cases.prompt: Which work in the corpus teaches a yogic ritual catalogue in which the breath in the right nostril becomes a triangular fire-maṇḍala, blazing red, with a trident at its center (a means for all actions); or, alternatively, a square Mahendra-maṇḍala blazing gold-yellow with an open five-pronged vajra at its center (for increase of life and wealth); while the breath in the left becomes a formless wind-maṇḍala, black, green, or yellow, blazing at both ends, with a banner-symbol and a hook and ring in the left hand for attraction, immobilization, and expulsion; or, alternatively, a circular white water-maṇḍala (bāruṇamaṇḍala) with a pure-crystal subtle maṇḍala at its center for pacification (śāntika)? Quote the descriptions of the fire-maṇḍala and the water-maṇḍala verbatim and identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: This precise pairing of nostril (right vs left) with element-maṇḍala (fire / Mahendra / wind / water), each with a specific shape, color, central symbol (trident, five-pronged vajra, banner+hook+ring, crystal), and specific siddhi outcome (sarvakarma, life-and-wealth increase, ākarṣaṇa-sthāmbana-uccāraṇa, śāntika), is unique to this Old Javanese Mahāyāna manual; no other Old Javanese Buddhist or Saiva work in the corpus, and none of the Sanskrit smṛtis or non-Indic works, contains this specific breath-element-maṇḍala-siddhi correspondence.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**The work *San Hyan Kamahayanikan* (author unknown), Chapter 1, teaches this yogic ritual catalogue of breath visualizations as maṇḍalas for ritual action.**[^1]

It describes the breath in the right nostril (for action, increase of life/wealth, or well-being) as either: a **fire-maṇḍala**, "triangular in shape, blazing and red in color. In its center is a trident. This is your means of accomplishment for all actions";[^2] or a **Mahendra-maṇḍala**, "square-shaped, blazing and yellow like gold in color. In its center is an open five-pronged *vajra*. This is your means of accomplishment for the increase of life and the increase of wealth";[^3] or (alternatively) a white, tranquil square Mahendra-maṇḍala with a central *vajra* "for well-being."[^4]

The breath in the left nostril (for attraction, immobilization, expulsion, or pacification) becomes either: a **formless wind-maṇḍala**, black, green, or yellow in color, "blaz[ing] at both ends. Its symbol is a banner or flag. The left hand grasps a hook and a ring. This is your means of accomplishment for attraction (*ākarṣaṇa*), immobilization (*sthāmbana*), and expulsion (*uccāraṇa*)";[^5] or a **water-maṇḍala (*bāruṇamaṇḍala*)**, "circular, blazing, and white in color. In its center is a subtle, small maṇḍala, like pure crystal in the middle is its form and its symbol. This is your means of accomplishment for pacification (*śāntika*)."[^6]

cases.prompt: Which work in the corpus, in defining the supreme grade of giving (above ordinary giving and above the giving of one's wife and child), tells of the Mahāsattva who gave his flesh and blood as a gift to demons, tigers, and garuḍas; gave his eyes as a gift to an old, blind brahmin who came to test his heroic generosity; and gave his heart as a gift to a suffering, sorrowful creature — illustrating a verse that says one who has given one's own flesh and blood and conquered the lord of demons, the heart, and the body, and through the power of giving experiences no pain, is what the sages proclaim 'mahātidāna'? Quote the verse and the three specific recipients (demons-tigers-garuḍas / old blind brahmin / suffering creature) verbatim and identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: The threefold dāna hierarchy (dāna / atidāna / mahātidāna) culminating in the Mahāsattva's flesh-to-demons-tigers-garuḍas, eyes-to-blind-brahmin-as-test, and heart-to-suffering-creature triad, illustrating the specific śloka about conquering 'the lord of demons, the heart, the body' through giving, is uniquely framed in this Mahāyāna pāramitā exposition; the other Old Javanese Buddhist narratives (Kuñjarakarṇa, Kalpabuddha) tell different jātaka-style episodes, and no Sanskrit smṛti, Old English religious poem, or European treatise in the corpus catalogues the perfection of giving with this specific verse-and-three-recipient structure.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**The work is *San Hyan Kamahayanikan* (unknown author), Chapter 1.**[^1]

This chapter defines three grades of *dānapāramitā* (Perfection of Giving). Ordinary **giving (*dāna*)** involves material gifts like food, jewels, wealth, clothing, and even one's kingdom.[^2] **Great giving (*atidāna*)** is giving one's wife and beloved child, as exemplified by the Mahāsattva who did so to a begging brahmin.[^2]

The **supreme great giving (*mahātidāna*)**, the highest grade, is defined by the following verse:

*HAVING GIVEN ONE’S OWN FLESH AND BLOOD TO OTHERS, CONQUERING THE LORD OF DEMONS, THE HEART, THE BODY, AND THROUGH THE POWER OF GIVING, NOT EXPERIENCING PAIN, THE SAGES PROCLAIM THIS MAHĀTIDĀNA.*[^1]

It illustrates this with the Mahāsattva "who gave his flesh, his blood, his eyes, and his body," specifically: his flesh and blood as a gift to **demons, tigers, or garuḍas**; his eyes "to an **old, blind brahmin** who was testing his heroic generosity"; and his heart "to a **suffering, sorrowful creature**."[^1]

[^1]: *San Hyan Kamahayanikan*, [paragraph](/works/unknown-san-hyan-kamahayanikan-2a0c19/01/translation/#p-1d8bac).
[^2]: *San Hyan Kamahayanikan*, [paragraphs](/works/unknown-san-hyan-kamahayanikan-2a0c19/01/translation/#p-fb3a09-p-d84734).

cases.prompt: Find the work in the corpus that contains a brief debate about the size of the gall bladder in which a 'great fool' is paraphrased as arguing that, although it is said to be a thumb's breadth in size, the gall bladder is in fact 'as large as the seven islands' — a statement the text frames as 'the argument of a pandit.' Quote the verse and the prose gloss verbatim and identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: The gall bladder = Lord Īśvara identification, paired with a satirical 'fool/pandit' debate measuring it against the seven islands (sapta-dvīpa) of cosmography, is unique to this Old Javanese text. No other tattva-jñāna text in the corpus stages this specific gall-bladder-vs-seven-islands argument, and the smṛti dharmaśāstras do not perform body-organ cosmology of this kind.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**The work is *San Hyan Mahajnana*, Chapter 1.**[^1]

The verse (58) states: "*Let the fool speak thus, [...] the gall bladder [...]. Of the measure of the seven islands, the king becomes powerful. // 58*."[^2]

The immediately following prose gloss elaborates: "Where is this great fool, who argues about its nature? It is said that the gall bladder is a thumb’s breadth in size. The gall bladder is as large as the seven islands. What are the seven islands? Such is the Lord Īśvara; He is of great power, all-pervading. Thus are the words of the fool. This is what is called the argument of a pandit."[^3]

This dismisses the 'great fool' (*maha-bala*, here rendered "great fool") who claims the gall bladder—described two paragraphs earlier as "its measure [...] that of a thumb"[^4]—is only a thumb's breadth, countering with the absurdly vast "seven islands" (a reference to the cosmic Jambudvīpa and its sub-continents in Indian cosmology).

cases.prompt: Find the work in the corpus that compares the Self trapped in worldly suffering to a serpent submerged in terrifying waters, and identifies the sacred syllable oṃ as the Garuḍa that swoops down to lift the serpent-Self up to the abode of Shiva. Quote the verse that names the puruṣa as nāga and oṃkāra as Garuḍa, and the prose line that says oṃkāra brings the Self to śivapada. Identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: The specific mythological transposition — puruṣa-as-nāga drowning in saṃsāra, oṃkāra-as-Garuḍa rescuing it — is a distinctive image in this work and does not appear in the other Old Javanese tattva texts (Tattvajñāna, Vrhaspatitattva, Gaṇapatitattva) or in the Buddhist Kamahāyānikan / Kuñjarakarṇa narratives. grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**The work is *San Hyan Mahajnana* (work slug: `unknown-san-hyan-mahajnana-11c531`), Chapter 1.**[^1]

This chapter uses the metaphor of the *puruṣa* (the Self or pure consciousness) trapped like a *nāga* (serpent) in the "terrible ocean of *saṃsāra*"—the worldly cycle of suffering, akin to terrifying waters.[^2] The sacred syllable *oṃkāra* is identified as *Garuḍa*, the mythical bird that lifts the serpent out, bringing the *puruṣa* to *śivapada* (Shiva's abode).[^3]

**The verse naming *puruṣa* as *nāga* and *oṃkāra* as *Garuḍa*:**  
*In the terrible ocean of saṃsāra, puruṣa is situated like a nāga. Knowing oṃkāra to be Garuḍa [...] for the effort [...] // 64*[^4]
