**The work is *San Hyan Tattvajnana* (unknown author), Chapter 1.**[^1]

This chapter describes a graded fourteen-step cosmic devolution of the Divine *Ātmā*, triggered by negligence and "lacking in *yoga*" at each stage. The verbatim ladder of descent reads:

> The *Tripuruṣa*—the *Tripuruṣa* are Lord Brahmā, Viṣṇu, and Īśvara; they are the primordial *sattva* beings. If that primordial *sattva* is negligent and lacking in *yoga*, it becomes *Pañcaṛṣi*. *Pañcaṛṣi*, if lacking in *yoga*, becomes *Saptaṛṣi*. *Saptaṛṣi*, if lacking in *yoga*, becomes *Devaṛṣi*. *Devaṛṣi*, if lacking in *yoga*, becomes *Devatā*. *Devatā*, if lacking in *yoga*, becomes *Vidyādhara*. *Vidyādhara*, if lacking in *yoga*, becomes *Gandharva*. *Gandharva*, if lacking in *yoga*, becomes *Dānava*. *Dānava*, if lacking in *yoga*, becomes *Daitya*. *Daitya*, if lacking in *yoga*, becomes *Rākṣasa*. *Rākṣasa*, if lacking in *yoga*, becomes *Bhūta Yakṣa*. *Bhūta Yakṣa*, if lacking in *yoga*, becomes *Bhūta Dĕṅĕn*. *Bhūta Dĕṅĕn*, if lacking in *yoga*, becomes *Bhūta Kāla*. *Bhūta Kāla*, if lacking in *yoga*, becomes *Bhūta Piśāca*. *Bhūta Piśāca*, if lacking in *yoga*, becomes human. [...] A human, if lacking in *yoga*, becomes a being of the *triyak* realm.[^1]

Immediately following this ladder, the text classifies the five kinds of *triyak* (animal realm beings) as: *pāśu* (domestic animal, born in the village), *mṛga* (wild beast, born in the forest), *pakṣī* (bird, born everywhere that flies), *mīna* (fish, born in the water), and *pipīlikā* (a belly-mover on the ground, "whose other names are not known").[^1]

[^1]: *San Hyan Tattvajnana*, [Chapter 1, paragraph p-357c74](/works/unknown-san-hyan-tattvajnana-1f29bd/01/translation/#p-357c74).

cases.prompt: Which work in the corpus assigns three classes of named blades to three patron divinities by social function — declaring that the Rākṣasa is the patron divinity of the king's life-taking weapons such as the curik, malalya, and śaṅkuh; that the Daitya is the patron divinity of the farmer's livelihood-producing implements such as the luke and pṛkul (or prĕkul); and that the Dānava is the patron divinity of the priest's ritual knives such as the pamiso (or pakisi), kartrī, and karayu — and immediately frames this within a blacksmith simile in which raw iron, tongs, hammer, anvil, and file all answer to the smith's will, the blade alone striking good and evil while the tools do not? Quote the three blade-divinity assignments verbatim and identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: The exact tripartite king/farmer/priest blade typology with the named indigenous blade-types (curik, malalya, śaṅkuh; luke, pṛkul; pamiso/pakisi, kartrī, karayu) and their pairing with Rākṣasa/Daitya/Dānava as patron divinities is unique to this Old Javanese text; Vrhaspatitattva uses 'blacksmith' only as an example of a worldly occupation and does not contain this typology, and no Sanskrit smṛti, dharmaśāstra digest, or other corpus work pairs these specific Old Javanese weapon-names with these classes of demonic patrons.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**San Hyan Tattvajnana, Chapter 1.**

The assignments are stated as follows:

> the *Rākṣasa* is the patron divinity of the blades that are the weapons of a king.[^1]

> The *Daitya* is the patron divinity of the blades that are the implements of farmers, such as a *luke* or a *pṛkul* (types of agricultural blades).[^1]

> The *Dānava* is the patron divinity of the blades that are the implements of a priest, such as a *pamiso*, *kartrī*, or *karayu* (types of ritual knives).[^1]

This is immediately restated and elaborated within the blacksmith simile:

> There is an illustrative simile, like that of a blacksmith. He works with iron. The tools are of many kinds: hammer, anvil, tongs, file. All of them are ultimately one with the iron. In the same way, the raw iron is made into a blade by the smith. The blades are of many kinds. There is a blade that is the weapon of a king. There is a blade that is the implement of a farmer. There is a blade that is the implement of a priest. All of them are known as blades. As long as the iron is sharp, it is known as a blade. Only their functions are different. The blade that belongs to the king, such as a *curik*, a *malalya*, or a *śaṅkuh* (types of daggers), is used as a means for battle, to take life. The blade that belongs to the farmer, such as a *luke* or a *prĕkul* (agricultural blades), is used as a means to produce a livelihood. The blade that belongs to the priest, such as a *pakisi*, *kartrī*, or *karayu* (ritual knives), is used as a means for trimming or cutting. But it is the blade that strikes good and evil. As for the tools, they do not participate in striking good and evil.[^2]

[^1]: *San Hyan Tattvajnana*, [p-cbb351](/works/unknown-san-hyan-tattvajnana-1f29bd/01/translation/#p-cbb351).

[^2]: *San Hyan Tattvajnana*, [p-9c664a](/works/unknown-san-hyan-tattvajnana-1f29bd/01/translation/#p-9c664a).

Smash his head!' while the scholar's parrot Gāvākṣa instead welcomes the king with words of greeting, offers him a new mat to sit on, betel nut and fresh betel leaf with uncooked lime to chew, and invites him to bathe in the spring? grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**The work is the *Slokāntara* (author unknown), Chapter 1.**[^1]

This story appears in the commentary (*vṛtti*) on verse 45, illustrating how "flaws and virtues arise from association" (*saṁsarga*). A king, separated from his retinue while hunting, arrives first at the hunter's house. The parrot **Girikā**, raised there, cries out: **"Come on, come on! Smash his head!"** The king flees in fear. He is then brought to the scholar's hermitage, where the parrot **Gāvākṣa**, raised there, welcomes him courteously: **"Oh, fortunate are you, O King! My lord has deigned to visit the hermitage. Pray, rest, sir. Sit upon the new mat. Chew this betel nut and fresh betel leaf, with uncooked lime. If my lord is weary, pray, bathe, Your Majesty, in the spring."** The scholar explains that the parrots' contrasting behaviors stem from their associations with the hunter (vice) and scholar (virtue).[^1]

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**The work is *Slokāntara* (unknown author), Chapter 1.**[^1]

This argument appears in verse 47 (Slo_47), which lists the requested flaws in nature, the moon, the ocean, and the gods Viṣṇu, Indra, and Śaṅkara (Śiva), before concluding that humans are even more flawed:

cases.prompt: Which dharmaśāstra digest opens by tracing its patron's dynasty back to a wrestler-king named Medinīmalla of the Sīkāśīrāja lineage—'a wrestler upon the earth with the weight of his mighty forces' whose 'fame [was] as white as the brilliance of the serpent king Ādiśeṣa'—and then continues the genealogy through Arjuna, Malakhāna, and Pratāparudra? grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Vīramitrodaya (Samayaprakāśa), Chapter 1**[^1]

The Vīramitrodaya, a 17th-century digest (*nibandha*) on Dharmaśāstra topics by Mitra Miśra, opens Chapter 1 (*Samayaprakāśa*, "Illumination of Time") with praises of deities and an extensive genealogy of its patron, King Vīrasiṃha of the Bundela dynasty. This traces back to Medinīmalla of the Sīkāśīrāja lineage, described as follows:

> There was, in the lineage of the Sīkāśīrāja, a universal sovereign over all the earth, an ornament to the circle of kings, like the moon in the ocean: the glorious Medinīmalla by name, a wrestler upon the earth with the weight of his mighty forces. He resembled the mighty Bhīma, gladdening the world by crushing the wicked and fulfilling the desires of supplicants, his fame as white as the brilliance of the serpent king Ādiśeṣa. // 7 //[^2]

The genealogy continues through his son Arjuna, grandson Malakhāna, and great-grandson Pratāparudra (verses 9–13), leading to Madhukarasāha, Vīrasiṃha, and finally Mitra Miśra's patron, Vīrasiṃhadeva.[^3]

cases.prompt: Which work in the corpus opens with a deity who, after rescuing the Earth from the cosmic waters in the form of a Boar whose tusks are the sacrificial post and tongue is the sacrificial fire, vanishes — prompting the Earth-goddess to take the form of a beautiful woman with lips red as the Bandhujīva flower, seek out the sage Kashyapa, and at his direction travel to the Ocean of Milk to ask the reclining god 'what will be my support?' before being seated on a golden throne to receive the law? Quote the relevant verses and identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: This Vaiṣṇava cosmogonic frame — the Boar-rescue followed by the Earth taking female form, the Kashyapa intermediary, the journey to the Ocean of Milk to find the god on Śeṣa, and the goddess seated on a golden throne to receive dharma — is the framing device of this smṛti and is found in no other dharmaśāstra in the corpus (Manu, Yājñavalkya, Nārada, Kātyāyana, Bṛhaspati, Parāśara, Aṅgirasa, and Yama all use other framings).

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**The *Viṣṇu Smṛti* (slug: `unknown-visnu-smrti-27f0d8`), Chapter 1, opens precisely with this narrative.**

The chapter begins with the Earth submerged in the cosmic waters at the end of Brahmā's night. Viṣṇu, in the form of a Boar (Varāha), rescues her: "Took on the auspicious form of the Boar... And lifted up the Earth."[^1] His form is described in sacrificial terms: "whose tusks were the sacrificial post... whose tongue was the sacrificial fire."[^2]

After recreating the world and vanishing—"Thus the blessed Boar... Went to a destination then unknown to the worlds"[^3]—the Earth-goddess (Vasudhā/Dhara/Prithvi) ponders her support: "What will be my support now?" She resolves: "I will go and ask Kashyapa."[^4]

Taking the form of a beautiful woman—"her lips auspicious and red like the Bandhujīva flower"[^5]—she approaches the sage. Kashyapa directs her: his abode "is in the Ocean of Milk... Proceed[ed] to the Ocean of Milk to see Keśava (Vishnu)."[^6]

There, she finds the reclining god ("resting upon the couch of Śeṣa") and asks: "what will be my support?"[^7] Viṣṇu seats her "on this excellent golden throne" to expound the law (*dharma*) of castes (*varṇas*) and orders (*āśramas*): "Be seated... hear now the laws."[^8]

cases.prompt: Which work in the corpus closes by having the deity address the Earth-goddess by name — 'O Dhara' — and praise the law-treatise just delivered as 'spoken by the god himself,' calling it a 'destroyer of bad dreams,' an 'abode of auspiciousness,' to be recited at śrāddhas by men who desire prosperity? Quote the closing verses verbatim and identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: The closing benediction in which the speaker is the god himself and the addressee is the Earth-goddess Dhara — combined with the unusual self-praise as a 'destroyer of bad dreams' and the prescription that the text be recited at śrāddhas — is uniquely the closing chapter of this smṛti's frame-tale; no other dharmaśāstra in the corpus closes with a divine first-person address to Pṛthivī.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**The *Viṣṇu Smṛti* (Vishnu Smriti), Chapter 100, closes the work with Vishnu addressing the Earth-goddess (Dhara) in this manner.**[^1]

The deity praises the just-delivered Dharmaśāstra (law-treatise) as follows (verses Vi_100.1–5, quoted verbatim):

> This excellent Dharmaśāstra was spoken by the god himself;  
> Those twice-born men who will uphold it, for them the highest state is in heaven. || Vi_100.1 ||
>
> This is purifying, auspicious, conducive to heaven, and life-giving,  
> And it brings knowledge, fame, and increases wealth and good fortune. || Vi_100.2 ||
>
> It should be studied, upheld, recited, and listened to;  
> And it should be recited at *śrāddhas* (श्राद्धs) by men who always desire prosperity. || Vi_100.3 ||
>
> The man who, desiring prosperity, always recites this,  
> This supreme secret has been told to you, O Dhara. || Vi_100.4 ||
>
> By me, being pleased, for the welfare of the world, this eternal Dharmaśāstra,  
> Which is supreme good fortune, famous, a destroyer of bad dreams, endowed with much merit, and an abode of auspiciousness. || Vi_100.5 ||

[^1]: *Viṣṇu Smṛti*, [Chapter 100, paragraph p-9d11b3](/works/unknown-visnu-smrti-27f0d8/100/translation/#p-9d11b3).

cases.prompt: Which work specifies an exact traveler's allowance for an ascetic short of provisions on a journey, permitting him to take 20 cucumbers, 1 wax gourd, one handful of rice, 1 jackfruit, 5 kalvi, and likewise sentul fruit from an abandoned garden — without being called a thief? Quote the passage verbatim and name the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: The combination of the exact numbered list (20 cucumbers, 1 wax gourd, 1 jackfruit, 5 kalvi) with Indonesian/Javanese fruits (kalvi, sentul) and the 'pari samuṣṭigraha' formula is unique to this Old Javanese ascetic-vow code. Sanskrit smṛtis treat traveler-theft generally but never with these specific Javanese fruit names or counts.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**The work *Vratisasana* (by Unknown), Chapter 1, specifies the exact traveler's allowance for an ascetic (*viku*) short of provisions on a journey.**[^1]

> But what a *viku* may do, if he is short of provisions on a journey, is this: he is not at fault for taking 20 cucumbers, 1 wax gourd, one handful of rice (*pari samuṣṭigraha*), its greens, any kind of vegetable as available, 1 jackfruit, 5 *kalvi* (unidentified fruit), and likewise *sentul* fruit. Whatever fruit he takes, let it not be much. If it is from an abandoned garden far from any house, not a place of dry or wet rice fields, and he gets enough for one meal, he is not called a thief for doing so.[^1]

[^1]: *Vratisasana*, [paragraph](/works/unknown-vratisasana-d0fe75/01/translation/#p-d910d5).

cases.prompt: Which work names five 'primary' outcastes — the liquor-seller, the trapper (or insect-burner), the butcher (life-taker), the potter, and the goldsmith (metal-burner) — and declares that their stalls and houses must be avoided by an ascetic because they are 'worse than the eighteen caṇḍālas'? Quote the verse and the commentary's enumeration verbatim, and name the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: The specific five-vs-eighteen caṇḍāla framing, where the goldsmith and potter are grouped with the butcher and liquor-seller as the primary outcastes 'worse than' a separate set of eighteen, is uniquely formulated in this Old Javanese vow-code. Sanskrit Manu/Yājñavalkya/Parāśara and the Javanese tattva texts in the corpus do not contain this exact five-versus-eighteen ascetic-avoidance rule with goldsmith and potter highlighted.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Vratisāsana, Chapter 1**

The verse is:

> [VS_11s-ab] *The liquor-seller, the insect-burner, the life-taker, the potter,*  
> [VS_11s-cd] *and the metal-burner—these five are declared to be the outcastes. ||11||*[^1]

The commentary enumerates them verbatim as follows:

> And furthermore, concerning the primary outcastes, they are five in number. It is not proper to go to their stalls, nor to rest there. Who are these five primary *caṇḍālas*? One who sells liquor, one who is a trapper, one who is a butcher, one who is a potter, and one who is a goldsmith. The places of these five, their stalls, and especially their houses, must be avoided. A *viku* may not rest in such a place, for they are worse than the eighteen *caṇḍālas*.[^2]

cases.prompt: Which work in the corpus contains a passage in which the divine instructor — speaking in the first person to his pupil — confesses his own back-story: he names himself the seventh of an eight-fold succession of teaching deities (Ananta, Sūkṣma, Śivatama, Ekarudra, Ekanetra, Trimūrti, Śrīkaṇṭha, Śikhaṇḍī) in which each successor replaces the previous, identifies himself as 'that Śrīkaṇṭha,' and admits that having been struck by passion he gazed upon the god of love so that the body of Kāmadeva 'was shattered, incinerated, and turned to ash' — yet 'the passion remained in me,' which is why he took Lady Umā as his wife and fathered the holy Sanatkumāra? Quote the succession-list and the Kāmadeva-and-Umā confession verbatim, and identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: The Aṣṭavidyāsana succession sequence (Ananta → … → Śrīkaṇṭha → Śikhaṇḍī), the first-person identification 'I am that Śrīkaṇṭha,' the poison-sight incineration of Kāmadeva, and the explicit naming of Sanatkumāra as the resulting son are unique to Vrhaspatitattva among the corpus's Old Javanese Saiva texts (Tattvajñāna, Mahājñāna, Gaṇapatitattva contain none of these); no Sanskrit smṛti, dharmaśāstra digest, Iqbal/Ghalib/Zauq divan, Cynewulf poem, or Comte/Dunoyer treatise contains this autobiographical Śrīkaṇṭha-Kāma frame.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**The work is *Vrhaspatitattva* (unknown author), Chapter 1.**[^1]

In this passage, the divine instructor Śrīkaṇṭha speaks in the first person to his pupil Vṛhaspati, identifying himself within the succession and confessing his backstory.

**The succession-list:** "Ananta, Sūkṣma, Śivatama, Ekarudra, Ekanetra, Trimūrti, Śrīkaṇṭha, and Śikhaṇḍī." He explains: "The holy Sūkṣma replaces Ananta, Śivatama replaces Sūkṣma, Ekarudra replaces Śivatama, Ekanetra replaces Ekarudra, Trimūrti replaces Ekanetra, Śrīkaṇṭha replaces Trimūrti, and Śikhaṇḍī replaces Śrīkaṇṭha. I am that Śrīkaṇṭha."[^1]

**The Kāmadeva-and-Umā confession:** "But I was shot with passion by Lord Kāma. I gazed upon the holy Kāma, and because he was gazed upon with my poison-sight, the body of the holy Kāmadeva was shattered, incinerated, and turned to ash. But the passion (*rāga*) remained in me. That is why I took your mother, the Lady Umā, as my wife, and we had a son, the holy Sanatkumāra."[^1]

[^1]: *Vrhaspatitattva*, [p-4d2295](/works/unknown-vrhaspatitattva-b60b28/01/translation/#p-4d2295).

cases.prompt: Which work in the corpus likens the cosmic veil of illusion to a honeycomb crowded with souls — declaring that 'māyā is like the honeycomb, and the ātmā is like the young bees,' that they are 'face-down, which means they are bent over,' and that each soul 'looks only downward, unaware of the tattva above it'? The same passage explains that this honey-comb soul-state arises because Śiva-tattva, originally crystal-clear, becomes stained by mala (impurity) and so 'ceases to be all-knowing and all-doing,' at which point it is renamed ātmā, meaning 'dazed consciousness.' Quote the honeycomb-and-young-bees image verbatim and identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: The honeycomb-and-young-bees simile for the multitude of souls in māyātattva, paired with the 'face-down / bent over / looks only downward' image and the explicit gloss of ātmā as 'dazed consciousness,' is unique to Vrhaspatitattva; the kindred Old Javanese Saiva texts (Tattvajñāna, Mahājñāna, Gaṇapatitattva) describe māyā and crystal-clear Śiva-tattva but use neither the honeycomb image nor the 'young bees looking only downward' phrasing, and no Sanskrit smṛti or non-Indic corpus work contains this specific bee-simile for souls under impurity.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
The passage likening the cosmic veil of illusion (*māyā*) to a honeycomb crowded with souls appears in the *Vrhaspatitattva*, Chapter 1.[^1]

> *Māyā* is like the honeycomb, and the *ātmā* is like the young bees. They are face-down, which means they are bent over; the *ātmā* looks only downward, unaware of the *tattva* above it.[^1]

The surrounding context matches your description precisely: *Śivatattva*, originally crystal-clear, becomes stained by *mala* (impurity) and so "ceases to be all-knowing and all-doing," at which point it is renamed *ātmā*, meaning "dazed consciousness," with the souls (*ātmatattvas*) multiplying until *māyātattva* is "crowded, like a honeycomb, layered and stacked."[^1]

[^1]: *Vrhaspatitattva*, [paragraph](/works/unknown-vrhaspatitattva-b60b28/01/translation/#p-643885).

cases.prompt: Which work in the corpus stages an elephant-and-blind-men parable in a teacher-pupil dialogue, in which the questioner asks why his teacher has bestowed so many different scriptures (naming the three paths Śaiva, Pāśupata, and Alepaka), and is answered with a passage that compares confused seekers to a group of blind men feeling different parts of an elephant — one calling it a water jar, one a winnowing fan, one a snake, one a granary, one an eel, one a pillar — and then coins the technical term andhatattva ('the principle of the blind men') to name the resulting state of bewilderment? Quote the six blind-men-comparisons verbatim and identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: The pairing of the blind-men-and-elephant parable with these specific six Old-Javanese-Saiva analogies (head = water jar, ear = winnowing fan, trunk = snake, belly = granary, tail = eel, leg = pillar), the explicit framing it within the question 'Śaiva or Pāśupata or Alepaka?', and the coinage of the technical term andhatattva are all unique to Vrhaspatitattva — none of Tattvajñāna, Mahājñāna, Gaṇapatitattva, the Sanskrit smṛtis, or any other corpus work uses this andhatattva coinage or this six-part Śaiva-Pāśupata-Alepaka frame around the parable.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**The work is *Vrhaspatitattva* (author unknown), Chapter 1.**[^1]

This chapter stages the described teacher-pupil dialogue between Lord Īśvara (the teacher) and the sage Vṛhaspati (the pupil). Vṛhaspati asks why the Lord "has given so many different versions in His teachings to the host of gods? There is one called *Śaiva*, one called *Pāśupata*, one called *Alepaka*."[^2] The Lord's response includes the elephant-and-blind-men parable, which illustrates bewilderment over multiple scriptures (likened to the elephant's limbs) and coins the term *andhatattva* ("principle of the blind men") for this state.[^3]

The six blind-men comparisons, quoted verbatim:

> One touched the head and said, ‘An elephant is like a **water jar**.’ Another touched the ear and said, ‘An elephant is like a **winnowing fan**.’ Another touched the trunk and said, ‘An elephant is like a **snake**.’ Another touched the belly and said, ‘An elephant is like a **granary**.’ Another touched the tail and said, ‘An elephant is like an **eel**.’ Another touched a leg and said, ‘An elephant is like a **pillar**.’[^3]

cases.prompt: Find the legal-procedure chapter in the corpus that names exactly five ordeals — the balance, fire, water, poison, and the sacred libation — and then prescribes their procedural details with these specific numbers: an iron ball of fifty palas placed in the accused's hands, who must walk through seven circles each of sixteen finger-breadths, with seven Aśvattha leaves bound on his hands; for the poison ordeal the accused must eat 'the Śārṅga poison from the Himalayan mountains'; and for the sacred-libation ordeal the accused is pure if no calamity befalls him within fourteen days. Quote the verse that addresses the Balance as 'O Mother' (asking to be carried up if pure and taken down if a sinner), and identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: The closed pentad balance/fire/water/poison/kosa with these exact numerical specifications — fifty palas, seven circles of sixteen aṅgulas, seven Aśvattha leaves, Śārṅga poison from the Himalayas, fourteen-day kosa wait — and especially the addressing of the Balance as 'O Mother' (mātar) is a signature passage of Yājñavalkya's vyavahārādhyāya. Nārada-smṛti, Bṛhaspati-smṛti, and Kātyāyana-smṛti also discuss ordeals, but none combines this specific set of procedural numbers with the maternal address to the Balance, and Manu only mentions ordeals in passing without these details.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Yājñavalkya Smṛti, Chapter 2 ("On Legal Procedure" / *vyavahārādhyāya*), verses 95–113, details exactly these five ordeals (balance, fire, water, poison, and sacred libation) for cases without proof by documents, possession, or witnesses—or grave accusations where the accuser stakes his life.**[^1]

This matches all specified procedural details:
- **Balance ordeal**: For women, children, aged, lame, blind, sick, or Brahmins. The accused—having fasted, bathed—is weighed equally against a counterweight; a line is marked. He addresses it:  
  > "O Balance, you are the abode of truth, created of old by the gods.  
  > Therefore, speak the truth, O auspicious one, and free me from this doubt.  
  > **If I am a sinner, O Mother, then take me down; if I am pure, then carry me upwards.**"[^2]  
  (Thus he should address the balance.) Hands examined (after rubbing rice grains), seven Aśvattha leaves placed on them and bound with thread.[^3] (He is reweighed; if pure, it rises.)
- **Fire ordeal**: Iron ball of **fifty palas**, smooth and glowing hot, placed in both hands; accused walks slowly through **seven circles**, each **sixteen finger-breadths** (same spacing between). If unburnt (after dropping and rubbing hands with rice), pure; repeat if falls midway or doubt.[^4]
- **Water ordeal**: Accused holds thighs of man in navel-deep water; recites to Varuṇa. Swift man fetches arrow; if still submerged on return, pure.[^5]
- **Poison ordeal**: Seven grains (per Vishnu Smṛti parallel, but here general); accused recites to poison, then eats **the Śārṅga poison from the Himalayan mountains**; digests unharmed = pure.[^6]
- **Sacred libation ordeal**: Water from deities' bathing (strained), three handfuls drunk (after worship); **pure if no calamity** (king/fate) **within fourteen days**.[^7]

cases.prompt: Find the legal text in the corpus that lays down a graduated monthly interest schedule with this exact dual structure: when there is a pledge, the lawful interest is one-eightieth of the principal per month; otherwise, it is two, three, four, or five percent according to the order of the four varṇas; and that further allows ten percent for those who travel through forests and twenty percent for those who travel by sea. The same passage also caps the maximum recoverable interest at eight times the principal for liquids, four times for cloth, three times for grain, and twice the principal for gold. Quote the verses fixing both the per-varṇa monthly rate and the forest/sea traveller rate, and identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: This precise three-tier interest framework — eightieth-part-per-month with a pledge, 2/3/4/5 percent by varṇa without one, plus the 10 percent / 20 percent forest-traveller / sea-traveller rates, capped at 8x liquids / 4x cloth / 3x grain / 2x gold — is the canonical Yājñavalkya schema and is not reproduced in this exact form in Manu, Nārada, Bṛhaspati, Kātyāyana, Parāśara, Viṣṇu, or the Vīramitrodaya digest. The forest/sea-traveller carve-out at 10% and 20% is especially distinctive.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Yājñavalkya Smṛti, Chapter 2 (Vyavahārādhyāya, on Legal Procedure)** lays down the exact interest rules described.[^1]

The passage on debt recovery (ṛṇādānaprakaraṇam) specifies:

> An eightieth part is the interest per month when there is a pledge; otherwise, it is two, three, four, or five percent, according to the order of the *varṇas*. // Yj_2.37 //  
> Those who travel through forests may pay ten percent, and those who travel by sea, twenty percent; or all persons of all castes may pay the interest they have agreed upon. // Yj_2.38 //

The immediately following verse caps recoverable interest: offspring for female animals; eight times the principal for liquids; four times for cloth; three times for grain; and two times for gold.[^2]

**The work is the *Yājñavalkya Smṛti*, Chapter 3 ("Chapter 3," titled "On Expiation" or *prāyaścittādhyāya* in Sanskrit).**[^1]

This chapter provides the requested anatomical inventory of the human body in verses 84–103, matching the specified numbers precisely: three hundred and sixty bones;[^2] sixty-four teeth (with sockets), twenty nails;[^3] seventy-two ribs (with sockets and nodules);[^4] forty-five [bones] in the back, fifteen in the neck;[^5] seventeen [bones] in the chest;[^6] seven hundred veins, nine hundred sinews, two hundred arteries, and five hundred muscles;[^7] one hundred and seven vital spots and two hundred joints;[^8] and body hairs together with the sweat ducts numbering "fifty crores and four crores ... and sixty-seven and a half lakhs."[^9]

cases.prompt: Which Sanskrit penitential text in the corpus prescribes a fourfold-value fine for a very specific list of grocery adulterations — pepper mixed with stone fragments, salt mixed with earth, and asafoetida mixed with tree resin — placed alongside the related rule that ghee sold under the name of ghee but adulterated with sesame oil (or oil with ghee) likewise incurs a fourfold penalty on the merchant? Quote the two verses listing these adulterations verbatim and identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: The exact triplet 'pepper with stone fragments, salt with earth, asafoetida with tree resin' — paired with the ghee-and-sesame-oil rule and the fourfold-value penalty — is a signature commercial-fraud catalogue unique to this smṛti; no other smṛti in the corpus (Manu, Yājñavalkya, Nārada, Bṛhaspati, Viṣṇu, Kātyāyana, Parāśara, Aṅgirasa) names asafoetida-with-resin or pepper-with-stone-fragments as adulteration species, and the word 'asafoetida' appears nowhere else in the corpus.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Yama Smṛti (also known as Yama Smṛti), Chapter 2.**

This Sanskrit Dharmaśāstra text, attributed to Yama, prescribes these fines in two consecutive verses amid rules on merchants' adulterations of groceries and oils:

> Ghee sold mixed with sesame oil under the name of ghee, / the merchant shall be made to pay four times the value; or oil mixed with ghee. // YSS_2.37 //[^1]

> Pepper with stone fragments, salt with earth, / and asafoetida with tree resin—he shall likewise be made to pay four times the value. // YSS_2.38 //[^2]

[^1]: Yama Smṛti, [paragraph](/works/unknown-yama-smrti-552f8d/02/translation/#p-551495).
[^2]: Yama Smṛti, [paragraph](/works/unknown-yama-smrti-552f8d/02/translation/#p-6039eb).

args.query: pepper stone salt earth asafoetida ghee sesame fourfold

cases.prompt: Which Sanskrit penitential text in the corpus declares that for the convergence of all sins together no ordinary penance is known, and that the prescribed remedy in such a case is to take up the skull-bowl and observe the Kāpālika vow — explicitly named by the wise as the ultimate penance — even though for each single sin committed alone the wise know a separate penance? Quote the paired verses on the convergence of sins and the skull-bowl vow verbatim and identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: The specific doctrinal pairing — that no penance exists when all sins converge, and that the skull-bowl Kāpālika vow is the explicit ultimate remedy — is unique to this smṛti; the strings 'skull-bowl', 'Kāpālika vow', and 'all sins converge' do not appear in any other corpus work (Manu, Yājñavalkya, Nārada, Bṛhaspati, Viṣṇu, Kātyāyana, Parāśara, Aṅgirasa, Vīramitrodaya).

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Yama Smṛti**, Chapter 1, verses 1.12–1.13 (translation).

> For each single sin that is committed, the wise know a penance; / but when all sins converge, no penance is known.[^1]  
> When all sins converge, taking up the skull-bowl is prescribed; / that Kāpālika vow is considered its ultimate penance by the wise.[^2]

[^1]: Yama Smṛti, [p-423161](/works/unknown-yama-smrti-552f8d/01/translation/#p-423161).
[^2]: Yama Smṛti, [p-22899e](/works/unknown-yama-smrti-552f8d/01/translation/#p-22899e).

cases.prompt: Which Sanskrit penitential text in the corpus prescribes that the king should banish from the kingdom a Brahmin who has abandoned his sacred fire by first smearing the man's body with ashes and fumigating him with the hair and bones of a corpse — and, in an immediately adjacent verse, makes 'the man who violates convention' go naked after being fanned with the hair of a corpse and his head adorned with a garland of corpse-hair? Quote both verses verbatim and identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: This precise pair of degradation rituals — body smeared with ashes plus fumigation with corpse-hair-and-bones for the abandoner of the sacred fire, then fanning with corpse-hair plus a corpse-hair head-garland for the convention-violator who is sent away naked — is unique to this smṛti; the words 'fanned' and 'fumigated with hair' in this purificatory-banishment sense do not appear in any other corpus smṛti, and no other dharmaśāstra in the collection clusters these specific corpse-hair humiliations together.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**No work in the Falsafa corpus matches this description exactly.**

I searched the English translations of all Sanskrit-language works (Aṅgirasa Smṛti, Bṛhaspati Smṛti, Kātyāyana Smṛti, Manu Smṛti, Nārada Smṛti, Parāśara Smṛti, Viṣṇu Smṛti, Vīramitrodaya, Yama Smṛti, and Yājñavalkya Smṛti) using distinctive phrases from the query (e.g., "sacred fire", "abandoned his sacred fire", "hair bones corpse", "garland corpse", "hair of corpse", "fanned with hair", "smearing ashes", "banish Brahmin", "king" "banish", "violates convention"). No results contained the prescribed punishments or the adjacent verses.

The closest passage (Bṛhaspati Smṛti 1.19.6, ¶ [p-026685]) describes humiliating a Śūdra boundary-guide by smearing his face with corpse-ash and marking him with animal blood, but it lacks the Brahmin abandoning fire, fumigation with corpse-hair/bones, banishment, nakedness, fanning, or corpse-hair garland—and it is not penitential for a Brahmin.[^1]

No other penitential (*prāyaścitta*-focused) text prescribes these rituals verbatim or adjacently. The corpus covers standard Smṛti punishments (e.g., fines, banishment for theft/violence, penances for purity violations), but not this sequence.

[^1]: Bṛhaspati Smṛti, [Chapter 1](/works/unknown-brhaspati-smrti-0fd070/01/translation/#p-026685).

cases.prompt: Find the ghazal in the corpus whose closing signature couplet is a poet's modest concession that he never inherited the elder Delhi master Mir's particular style ('I never attained Mir's manner / mode'), spoken in self-deprecation against fellow-poets who 'struck hard' at the same ghazal. The same poem contains a couplet boasting that the speaker's sigh-arrow struck Saturn's eye in the heavens, and a couplet declaring that from Love's hand neither Qais nor Farhad escaped — 'one struck down in the desert, the other on the mountain.' Quote the Mir-naming maqta and the Saturn couplet verbatim, and identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: A poet's signature couplet that names Mir Taqi Mir as the unattainable master is a biographically specific Zauq move (Zauq was Bahadur Shah Zafar's ustad and the Delhi successor weighed against Mir's legacy); Ghalib's Diwan, the only other 19th-century Urdu ghazal collection in the corpus, contains no comparable Mir-homage maqta. The Saturn-pierced-by-sigh and Qais-and-Farhad couplets are in the same ghazal, fixing it uniquely.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**The ghazal is chapter 42 ("Neemcha yaar ne jis waqt baghal mein maara") of *Diwan-E-Zauq* by Sheikh Ibrahim Zauq.**[^1]

This ghazal's closing *maqta* (signature couplet), where the poet Zauq humbly concedes he could not attain Mir's distinctive style—while noting how his peers fiercely critiqued ("struck hard at") his own attempt at the same *radif* ("maara")—reads:

> I could not match the grace of Mir, that master of the art;  
> Though Zauq, in this ghazal, with force was struck down from the start.[^2]

Its couplet boasting of the sigh-arrow that pierced Saturn's eye (chashm-e-zuhal, the "evil eye" of fate's revolving sky) reads:

> The spiteful sky's eye never burst, for all my cries,  
> But my sigh's arrow struck down Saturn in the skies.[^3]

The poem also includes the couplet on Qais (Majnun) and Farhad: "From the hand of great Love, not Qais nor Farhad could flee; One in the desert, one on the mountain, was struck down for all to see." (This recurs thematically in the repeated motif of being "struck down" (*maara*) by love or rivals.)

[^1]: Sheikh Ibrahim Zauq, *Diwan-E-Zauq*, [chapter](/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/42-neemcha-yaar-ne-jis-waqt-baghal-mein-maara/translation/).

[^2]: Sheikh Ibrahim Zauq, *Diwan-E-Zauq*, [paragraph](/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/42-neemcha-yaar-ne-jis-waqt-baghal-mein-maara/translation/#p-0fdb39).

[^3]: Sheikh Ibrahim Zauq, *Diwan-E-Zauq*, [paragraph](/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/42-neemcha-yaar-ne-jis-waqt-baghal-mein-maara/translation/#p-cc12ee).

cases.prompt: Locate the ghazal in the corpus whose opening couplet (matla) treats a small, ordinary domestic gift — two almonds someone has sent tucked into a pouch / purse — as an offering for which the speaker pledges to give his eyes in return as religious duty. The same ghazal contains a later couplet declaring that no perfect vessel can ever come off the potter's wheel, so one should not waste this heart-dust on the potter's hand. Quote the almonds-in-a-pouch matla and the potter's-wheel couplet verbatim, and identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: The concrete, mundane image of two almonds sent in a pouch as the opening of a ghazal is unusual within the classical Urdu register; Ghalib's Diwan (the only neighbouring work of the same era / language / genre in the corpus) contains no almond / batwa imagery. Pairing it with the potter's-wheel-no-perfect-vessel couplet from the same ghazal isolates the chapter uniquely.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
The ghazal is from *Diwan-E-Zauq* by Sheikh Ibrahim Zauq, chapter 53 ("baadam do jo bheje hain batwe mein daal kar").[^1]

**Matla (opening couplet):**  
These almonds that you sent, a gift however small,  
My faith demands I give my eyes and answer to the call.[^2]

**Potter's-wheel couplet:**  
No perfect vessel from the wheel will form at all,  
So do not waste this heart-dust on the potter's call.[^3]

[^1]: Sheikh Ibrahim Zauq, *Diwan-E-Zauq*, chapter 53.
[^2]: Sheikh Ibrahim Zauq, *Diwan-E-Zauq*, [paragraph](/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/53-baadam-do-jo-bheje-hain-batwe-mein-daal-kar/translation/#p-d86b48).
[^3]: Sheikh Ibrahim Zauq, *Diwan-E-Zauq*, [paragraph](/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/53-baadam-do-jo-bheje-hain-batwe-mein-daal-kar/translation/#p-290709).

cases.prompt: Find the ghazal in the corpus whose every couplet ends on the same short refrain 'is love' (radif: '-aar ka' in the original), in which one couplet declares that even the heart's fire will not be extinguished beneath the dust of the grave — and that therefore a tall chinar tree will grow upon the speaker's tomb. The same ghazal contains a couplet calling the bittersweet juice of teardrops 'the sherbet of the pomegranate of the highest paradise (khuld-e-bareen),' and closes with the poet's signature warning that this tavern has no place for the wise, so one should flee the world. Quote the chinar-on-the-grave couplet and the pomegranate-of-paradise couplet verbatim, and identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: The named species 'chinar' growing on the speaker's grave (a Kashmiri-tree image, not a generic 'tree' or 'cypress') is absent from Ghalib's Diwan and from every other work in the corpus. Pairing it with the very specific 'sherbet of the pomegranate of khuld-e-bareen' from the same ghazal, both within a radif-'is love' refrain, fixes the chapter uniquely.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
The ghazal is Sheikh Ibrahim Zauq's *Diwan-E-Zauq*, chapter 56 ("hungaama garm hasti-e-na-paaidaar ka"). Every couplet ends on the refrain "is love," matching the radif '-aar ka' in the original Urdu.[^3]

**Chinar-on-the-grave couplet:**  
"My heart's own fire, in the grave, will glow, is love.  
A tall Chinar upon my tomb will grow, is love."[^1]

**Pomegranate-of-paradise couplet** (noting "Eden's pomegranate" renders *khuld-e-bareen*, the pomegranate of the highest paradise, with "juice" for *sherbet*):  
"You ask what sweetness bitter teardrops show, is love?  
The juice of Eden's pomegranate in its flow, is love."[^2]

The ghazal closes: "O Zauq, if you are wise, then from this world just go, is love. / This tavern has no place for those who think they know, is love."[^3]

[^1]: Sheikh Ibrahim Zauq, *Diwan-E-Zauq*, [paragraph](/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/56-hungaama-garm-hasti-e-na-paaidaar-ka/translation/#p-f77039).

[^2]: Sheikh Ibrahim Zauq, *Diwan-E-Zauq*, [paragraph](/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/56-hungaama-garm-hasti-e-na-paaidaar-ka/translation/#p-a4f044).

[^3]: Sheikh Ibrahim Zauq, *Diwan-E-Zauq*, [chapter](/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/56-hungaama-garm-hasti-e-na-paaidaar-ka/translation/).
